ℹ Contracts Screen Guide

Action row: ↕ reorder · 🕒 defer to back burner · ✂ split · ⚭ merge · 📌 pin · ✕ archive/delete/recur · 💰 claim gold.

Split: break a task with 2+ sub-tasks into two tasks. Both halves stay in the same bordered panel.

Merge: combine two tasks into one master. Choose the new title and the order of the combined sub-tasks.

Pin (📌): mirrors any task or sub-task at the top of the screen for prominence. Toggling progress on the pinned copy updates the original — they share the same data.

Waiting (⏳): swipe a sub-task right to reveal the ⏳ button, or tap the ⏳ on the task action row — either opens a short note editor for "who are you waiting on?" The row tints teal, a small note badge appears, and the checkbox swaps to an hourglass. When you're no longer waiting, open the editor again and tap "✓ Done waiting." Checking the item off also auto-clears waiting. On desktop you can right-click a checkbox to toggle the flag directly.

Favourites & scheduled contracts: weekly / monthly / yearly tasks scheduled in The Archive auto-activate at your configured start-of-day.

⟲ Recent Actions

Tap Revert on any of the last 20 actions to roll it back.

⭐ Kudos

Tasks and sub-tasks you've completed. Newest first.

✦ Contract Archive

⭐ = favourite (daily/custom auto-activates at morning reset). Weekly, monthly, and yearly tasks can be scheduled with Pick selectors and move to Contracts at the configured start of day. ↑↓ reorder within group. Action icons: ⇡ move to top, 🪙 claim gold, ▶ activate, 🗑 delete.

✦ The Adventuring Party
✦ Recruitment Office
Review candidates, hirelings, or cancel to return to the Party screen.
Standard Recruitment

Choose from five candidates. First roll is free — additional rerolls cost 10 gp.

Roll the dice to reveal candidates…
🪓 Hire a Hireling

Low-cost labour with minimal abilities. Cannot go on quests alone. First roll is free — rerolls cost 5 gp.

Roll to find available labour…
⭐ Custom Mercenary

Hand-pick attributes for a higher fee. Each selected attribute adds 50 gp to the base cost. Skills capped at 4, tools at 2, languages at 2 — same as standard, but you choose which ones. Comes with a class-appropriate equipment kit.

You may pick up to 4 skill pills. Leave all unselected to randomize class-appropriate skills.
You may pick up to 2 tool pills. Leave all unselected to randomize tools.
You may pick up to 2 language pills. Common is always included automatically.

Base 500 gp + 0 gp in options = 500 gp total

Relentless Quest
Relentless Quest
Version 1.7073
Dev mode: navy quick-access bar below the header with Ledger, QP Manager, +1 Day, and Exit Dev Mode.
Branding
Daily Reset
Use an exact time. Favourite contracts auto-activate once each day the first time the app reaches or passes that time.
Contract Preferences
When every sub-task of a contract is checked, the Archive / Delete / Recur modal opens automatically. Turn off to only show the modal when you tap the contract's Done button.
📘 Tutorial & Reference
How things work in Relentless Quest. Pick a category, then tap a topic to expand.
📜 Change Log
Tap to review notable stable updates.
Version 1.7073 — Squad Manager retired from the Party-screen toolbar; its three useful actions migrated to per-squad and top-level Party buttons. Why. The Squad Manager modal had accumulated a four-button toolbar (\uD83C\uDFAF Fill Role / \uD83D\uDD0D Overlap / \u2696 Rebalance / \uD83D\uDED2 Shopping) that always required a follow-up squad-picker step, plus an Overlap toggle that became fully redundant once v1.7045 added the same toggle directly to the Party screen. The v1.7044\u20131.7049 consolidation series moved most squad analysis surfaces (attribute pill columns, overlap coloring, shopping-mode auto-narrow) onto the Party screen itself. This version closes the loop by retiring the manager's entry point and routing its remaining utility through Party-screen UI. (1) Top-level button replaced. The \u2694 Manage Squads button in the #party-tagbar is replaced with \u2696 Rebalance (N QP). Rebalance promoted from a per-modal toolbar action to a top-level button because it operates across every squad simultaneously \u2014 it doesn\u2019t fit a per-squad header slot. Live cost label, disabled when QP is off / no squads exist / insufficient balance, with title text explaining the refusal reason. runAutoRebalance() itself unchanged \u2014 same hill-climb optimizer, same per-character pin respect, same cycle-lock surcharge, same QP confirm dialog. (2) Per-squad Fill Role. Each real squad\u2019s panel header (the same row as \u270F Edit and \u21C4 Relocate) grows a \uD83C\uDFAF Fill Role button. Click opens the Squad Manager pre-targeted at this squad with Focus Mode enabled, skipping the manager\u2019s own \u201Cwhich squad?\u201D picker. Virtual HQ panels (unassigned buckets) don\u2019t get the button \u2014 unassigned characters aren\u2019t a composable squad. (3) Per-squad Shopping. Same row, same pattern: \uD83D\uDED2 Shopping (N) with the live list count on the label. Rendered ONLY when this squad has a non-empty S._shoppingList entry, so squads with no list don\u2019t get a button that would just alert \u201Clist is empty.\u201D Click opens the manager pre-entered into shopping mode for this squad, mirroring the tail of the v1.7049 Quest Preview \u201CShop for this squad\u201D CTA \u2014 same path, different entry point. (4) Overlap dropped, not migrated. The Squad Manager toolbar\u2019s \uD83D\uDD0D Overlap button has no Party-screen equivalent because the Party screen already has its own Overlap toggle (v1.7045) with the same scope semantics. Two surfaces, one signal \u2014 retiring the manager\u2019s version eliminates a redundant control. (5) New helpers. openSquadManagerForFillRole(squadName) and openSquadManagerForShopping(squadName) wrap the existing openSquadManager() with the appropriate post-open state mutations. Both validate the squad still exists (defensive against rename/disband races) and surface a friendly alert if not. The Squad Manager modal itself is unchanged \u2014 it just becomes reachable through narrower entry points. (6) Dead-code retention. The two data-a=\"sqmgr\" dispatcher branches (in the #party-tagbar and #plist click delegators) stay in place even though no button on the page emits that data-a anymore. Cost is two harmless if-branches; benefit is that openSquadManager remains callable from the dev console and any future re-entry path doesn\u2019t need re-plumbing. Matches the v1.6924 pattern of how the Projects code was kept around for safety after Split/Merge/Pin replaced it. Scope boundaries. Pure UI re-routing. openSquadManager, renderSquadManager, renderSqmgrFocusPanel, runAutoRebalance, _openShoppingMode, the S._shoppingList data model, the S._sqmgrPins per-character pin map \u2014 all unchanged. No new state field, no schema change, no migration. The four buttons inside the Squad Manager modal\u2019s own toolbar still function for users who land there via the Quest Preview CTA or via console call. No SQL change. No data-model change.
Version 1.7072 — Two small Contracts-screen fixes. (1) Deferred cards / split-groups are now uniformly dim. User report. When a task or split-group was on back burner, the card body showed the expected #d9cfb0 dim wash but inner regions stayed bright: the .strow-main sub-task rows (opaque parchment strips), the "Add sub-task\u2026" input, and the action-row buttons. Read as banded "light strip, dark strip, light strip" instead of a unified deferred appearance. Root cause. .card.dim's opacity:.7 cascades to children (correct) but the background:#d9cfb0 only paints the card itself. Any descendant with its own background — .strow-main (var(--parchment) opaque, required opaque since v1.7035 because it covers the absolutely-positioned swipe drawers), the global input[type=text] rule (rgba(255,255,255,.7)), and the .btn.bink / .bpur / .bg / .archive-a fills — painted their own color over the dim wash. Fix. Dim-scoped overrides pull each inner background toward the #d9cfb0 family. .strow-main inside a dim card gets solid #d9cfb0 (preserves the swipe-drawer hiding invariant). A dim + waiting row uses a pre-mixed opaque #ccc3a5 (dim-parchment averaged with the v1.7035 7% teal tint) so the "waiting" visual signal still reads on deferred rows. The .staddrow input drops to rgba(217,207,176,.7), and the four button variants drop their alphas to blend rather than pop. Same rules mirror on .split-group.dim. (2) Pinned tasks on desktop now show their source parent. User report. On mobile (1-col), pinned sub-task rows displayed a tappable from chip that opens a toast with the full parent-task name (v1.6961). On desktop (multi-col), there was no surface for finding the source parent — you could see the pinned item but not where it lived. Root cause. The v1.6979 rule #tlist.cols-2/.cols-3 .strow-meta-from{display:none} hid the chip on wide because it competed with the original 5-button reorder drawer. But v1.6980 replaced three of those buttons with drag-to-reorder, leaving room. The hide rule was never revisited. Fix. Split the rule: .strow-meta-gp (the longer +N gp label) stays hidden in multi-col; .strow-meta-from is now visible in both modes. The chip is already a compact button with an on-click toast, so surfacing it doesn't regress layout. Scope boundaries. CSS-only. No JS change, no data-model change, no persistence change, no SQL change. Both fixes are additive overrides — removing the new block reverts to v1.7071 behavior exactly.
Version 1.7071 — Two unrelated changes. (1) Unique mode: teal tint on unique pills, mutually exclusive with Overlap. Per user spec. The v1.7069 Unique button filtered shared pills out but didn't color the remaining unique ones, so they rendered with the default parchment styling and looked indistinguishable from a normal pill. Now unique pills get a teal tint matching the active-state color of the Unique button itself: #c6dae2 fill (the saturated teal #1a6b8a blended 75% toward white), with the saturated hex used for border and text. Visual treatment parallels Overlap's palette pattern \u2014 saturated for border/text, washed for fill \u2014 so the two modes use a consistent color-design language. Mutual exclusion. Toggling Overlap on now forces Unique off, and vice versa. The previous "both can be on simultaneously" v1.7069 behavior produced a logically valid but visually quiet state (when Unique is on the shared pills are gone, so Overlap's coloring has nothing to do). Per user spec the toggles now behave as a one-of selector: either nothing, or Overlap, or Unique. The scope chip continues to govern both. (2) Recur modal: three new options. Added Every weekday (Mon\u2013Fri), Weekends only (Sat\u2013Sun), and Monthly on day\u2026. Schedule dropdown now reads: Tomorrow only / Every day / Every weekday / Weekends only / Every other day / Every N days / Weekly on / Monthly on day. The monthly mode reveals a number input for day-of-month (1\u201331). Why these specifically. Weekdays and weekends are the two most-requested cadence patterns missing from v1.7019's original list. Monthly was actually already supported by the engine since v1.7019 (the checkFavourites function had a freq==='monthly' branch that read autoMonthday; the archived_tasks table already had an auto_monthday column from the v1.7019 SQL migration) but was unreachable because no UI path set those fields. Exposing it in the dropdown was nearly free. Engine. Two new freq values added: weekdays (activates when dueAt.getDay() is 1\u20135) and weekends (activates when 0 or 6). Both stateless \u2014 no additional fields needed beyond the freq value itself. Dedupe. The v1.7060 recur-dedupe logic that neutralizes prior recurrence on duplicate archive entries now recognizes weekdays, weekends as recurring (alongside the existing daily/weekly/monthly/yearly). Without this update, setting a new recurrence on a task that previously had "weekdays" recurrence would have left both entries active in the archive, both reactivating on schedule. Why no "multiple weekdays (MWF, TTh)" option. Considered, but it requires multi-select UI, which is materially more complex than the existing single-day picker. Weekdays + Weekends covers the most common patterns; users with more specific needs can use weekly with a single day for now. Why no yearly option. Engine already supports it (freq==='yearly', autoYearlyDate) but the use case for a TASK contract that recurs annually is narrow enough I'd rather wait for a real ask before adding the date input. Scope boundaries. No SQL change \u2014 the auto_monthday column was added in v1.7019's migration. No data-model change. The new freq values round-trip through both regular-user (column-based) and code-session (blob-based) persistence as opaque strings. Existing recurring tasks with daily, weekly, etc. continue to work unchanged. The Reset Recurrence button in the archive editor (v1.7019) doesn't know about the new freq values yet \u2014 it clears all recurrence fields generically, which still works for weekdays/weekends since those don't use any extra fields. Editing existing recurrence on an archived task to switch between cadences is still not supported in the archive editor (matches v1.7019's deferred scope; would need a recurrence-editor modal on the archive row).
Version 1.7070 — Single-task merging: zero-sub tasks can now participate in merge. Why this was blocked. The merge feature was originally designed around combining two collections of subtasks into one collection. Three guards enforced that: countMergeCandidates only counted tasks with subs as candidates, _canMerge required the source itself to have subs, and the target dropdown filter excluded zero-sub tasks. So merging "License renewal finalize" (a flat task) into "Bugs and urgent features" (a task with subs) was impossible, even though the user's mental model for that operation is reasonable: "move this work item into that checklist as a new line item." What landed. Per user spec (Option A from the design discussion): when a participating task has zero subtasks, auto-promote its name to a synthesized subtask in the merge result. Three guards loosened: countMergeCandidates drops the subs filter (any other task is a valid candidate); _canMerge drops the subs.length >= 1 requirement on the source; the target dropdown includes zero-sub tasks (their option label honestly reads "0 sub-tasks" so the user can tell what they're selecting). The merge button title also updated to "Need another task to merge with" (was "Need another task with sub-tasks to merge"). How auto-promotion works. rebuildMergeOrderFromTarget synthesizes a subtask from the parent name when either side has no subs. The synthesized entry rides on the merge order with a promotedSub field carrying the data, so the source/target tasks themselves are NOT mutated until the user confirms (cancel-safe; no orphan subs left behind). The synthetic _sid is prefixed (__merge_promoted::) to avoid collisions, and is discarded on confirm in favor of ensureTaskIds assigning a real one when the merged task next renders. Promoted subs render identically to native subs in the picker (per user spec) — they look like subtasks because that's what they functionally become. The A/B origin tag on each row still indicates which parent the sub came from. Default merge title preserved. Still src.name + " + " + tgt.name for new merges, even when single tasks are involved (per user spec). The user can edit before confirming. Three merge scenarios now supported beyond the original two-multi-sub case: (1) zero-sub + zero-sub → result with two promoted subs (e.g. "Pay phone bill" + "Pay rent" → "Pay phone bill + Pay rent" with subs ["Pay phone bill", "Pay rent"]); (2) zero-sub + multi-sub → promoted parent name as one sub plus the multi's real subs; (3) multi-sub + zero-sub → multi's real subs plus the promoted target name. The original (4) multi + multi case is unchanged. Pin handling. If the source was a zero-sub task with a task-level pin (allowed under v1.6972's rule that no-sub tasks can be pinned at task level), the post-merge result has subs and the pin becomes invalid. ensureTaskIds already silently drops invalid task-level pins on next render (line 10867: (t.subtasks||[]).length===0) so no explicit cleanup needed in the merge confirm. The pin just disappears on the next render pass. Scope. Five small edits clustered in the merge subsystem: countMergeCandidates filter, _canMerge condition, button title text, target dropdown filter, rebuildMergeOrderFromTarget auto-promotion, renderMergeSubsList lookup fallback, merge confirm resolver fallback. No new state field, no schema change, no migration. The merge confirm's metadata-combine logic (count, pct, desc, note) is unchanged — it works correctly for zero-sub tasks since those fields default to safe zero/empty values.
Version 1.7069 — Two small changes. (1) Faith downtime: "adopted" \u2192 "researched". The unlock message for the faith downtime job now reads "X researched faith: Y" instead of "X adopted faith: Y" in two sites: the live downtime conclusion toast (when a faith job completes) and the Franchise Dashboard's Downtime Ledger entry (which echoes the same unlockMsg from history). Both sites use the same string template, both got the swap. (2) Party screen: new "\u2728 Unique" button, bounded panel for view-mode controls. New companion to the existing Overlap toggle. When Unique mode is on, the renderer FILTERS attribute pills to show only those unique to each character within the current scope (squad / territory / franchise) \u2014 not just colors them differently like Overlap does. Squad-composition tool: "what does each character bring that no one else in the {squad, territory, franchise} brings?" Composable with Overlap. Both modes can be on simultaneously. They share the same scope chip (which now appears when EITHER mode is on, not just when Overlap is on as before). When both are on, shared pills are filtered out by Unique, so Overlap's coloring has nothing left to color \u2014 a logically valid state, just visually quiet. Bounded panel. The three controls (Overlap, Unique, scope chip when visible) are now wrapped in a subtle bordered container with a slight background tint so the player reads them as one group governing the same axis-pill view system. Replaced the previous border-left separator that visually conflated them with the unrelated axis-toggles cluster to their left. Persistence. New S.partyUniqueOnly field plumbed through all six required surfaces per the v1.7041 self-test protocol: createDefaultState, regular-user sbPush cleanSettings._partyUniqueOnly, regular-user sbPull, code-session _keys array, FRANCHISE_FIELDS self-test, EXPECTED_CODE_KEYS self-test. The clean-strip list also picks up the new _partyUniqueOnly blob key so it doesn't leak into the cleaned settings copy. Render plumbing. The _pillOverlap overlap-lookup was previously built only when S.partyOverlapOn was true; expanded the build trigger to S.partyOverlapOn || S.partyUniqueOnly since unique mode needs the same shared/unshared classification. The pill render at line ~18645 gets a new early-return: when uniqueOnly is on AND colorFor returned a color (= shared in scope), return empty string from the map. Empty returns concat-vanish in the subsequent .join(''), so no row layout adjustment needed \u2014 unique pills just appear in their normal positions, shared pills just don't. Click handling. One new branch on the data-a dispatcher: toggle-unique flips S.partyUniqueOnly, saves, re-renders. Mirrors toggle-overlap's pattern. Scope. Pure additive UI feature. No SQL change, no schema change. The existing Overlap mode continues to work exactly as before when Unique is off; the existing pill-render logic is unmodified except for the early-return filter and the broader _pillOverlap build trigger. Why this is useful. Overlap answers "who else has this skill?" \u2014 helpful for understanding redundancy. Unique answers "what does each squadmate uniquely contribute?" \u2014 helpful when planning specialist coverage or deciding whether a recruit fills a real gap or just adds noise. The two modes complement: Overlap to spot redundancy, Unique to spot specialization.
Version 1.7068 — Quest-prereq Purchase button: route pets (and all party-asset items) through asset-mode commit so they end up in S.partyAssets instead of corrupted as strings in a character's inv. User report. After v1.7067 made the Purchase button render for pet prereqs, clicking it on the Rat slot did appear to complete a purchase, but the quest prereq slot remained unmet. Inspection of the affected character's gear screen showed Rat sitting in the regular Inventory list (alongside Adventurer's Pack, Mace, etc.) rather than in the "Pets & Mounts" section. Diagnosis. The qcs-store-link click handler at line ~25770 sets P5.assetMode=false and routes through a path that uses P5.item='__questitem__' + P5.customItem=shopItem. The buy commit (ov-bi-yes) reads P5.assetMode to decide between (a) push to S.partyAssets as a typed object (asset-mode path, lines 26583-26595) or (b) push to S.party[ci].inv as a flat string (inv path, line 26610). Pets — like vehicles, mounts, animals, and any item flagged party:true — require asset-mode because the prereq met-check at line ~34783 scans S.partyAssets for matching type:'pet' entries; a pet sitting as a string in inv is invisible to that check. The normal store-side opener at line ~26439 has an if(item.party){ P5.assetMode=true; ... } branch that flips the mode and sets up the asset-style owner picker. The qcs-store-link handler bypassed that branch entirely, so all party-asset items purchased via prereq Purchase buttons silently corrupted into inv strings. The bug existed for vehicles, animals, and mounts as well as pets — pets just made it newly visible because v1.7067 was the first version where pet prereqs even got a Purchase button. What landed. (1) qcs-store-link handler now branches on shopItem.party. When true, it runs the same asset-mode setup the regular store opener uses: P5.assetMode=true, ensureAssetMetas(), building / animal owner-picker variants, name row, party note, etc. P5.item is set to the pet's name (string) so the commit handler resolves it via the _allItems name lookup at line 26508 (which already includes PETS via v1.7067's SQL seed). When false, the original gear path runs unchanged. (2) Asset-mode commit now honors the quest-prereq re-open flag. The inv-path commit (_finalizeToChar at line ~26608) had the _reopen hook from v1.6924 that re-renders the quest modal after a successful prereq buy; the asset-mode commit at line 26594 closed the buy modal silently without the equivalent. Added a parallel _reopenAsset read so post-purchase the quest modal re-opens and the prereq slot lights up green. Notably the asset-mode re-open does NOT auto-assign characters to slots the way the inv-path does — pet ownership semantics are different (the user picks the owner explicitly via the asset-mode owner-picker, including unowned-franchise / squad / building / vehicle, so auto-assigning the "slotted character" based on a pet buy is ambiguous). The user sees the slot now met (the existing met-check correctly handles char-owned vs unowned pets) and proceeds. Migration for affected users. Anyone who purchased a pet via the prereq Purchase button after v1.7067 deployed will have the pet stuck as a string in their inv. Console one-liner to heal: (()=>{const targetCi=S.party.findIndex(c=>c&&c.name==='Bree Zephyrstone');if(targetCi===-1)return'not found';const c=S.party[targetCi];const ratIdx=(c.inv||[]).indexOf('Rat');if(ratIdx===-1)return'no Rat in inv';c.inv.splice(ratIdx,1);if(!S.partyAssets)S.partyAssets=[];S.partyAssets.push({name:'Rat',ic:'\ud83d\udc00',type:'pet',meta:{uid:'a'+Date.now().toString(36)+Math.random().toString(36).slice(2,8),customName:'',ownerType:'char',ownerRef:c.name}});return'Rat moved from '+c.name+'\u2019s inv to partyAssets as a pet (ownerType=char)';})() (replace the character name as needed). Then trigger a save by doing any small UI action. Scope. Two edits, both in the buy flow (handler routing + commit re-open). No SQL change, no data-model change, no schema change. The S.partyAssets shape is unchanged — this just routes purchased pets into it correctly instead of into inv.
Version 1.7067 — Pet prereqs now show a Purchase button, route to the Pets store tab. Companion to Quest Editor v1.693 + the pets-table seed migration. Context. Pets are now in the store as DB rows (seeded via the migration). The classification logic was already correct: isPetItemName('Rat') returns true once Rat exists in PETS, the slot label correctly renders as \ud83d\udc3e PET. But the Purchase button stayed suppressed because of an outdated !slot.isPet guard with the comment "No purchase button for pet prereqs \u2014 pets aren't in the store." That comment was true when written; isn't anymore. What landed. Three coordinated edits, all in renderParty's quest-prep modal render paths: (1) The qhole-render Purchase button now emits for pet slots too, routed to the pets store tab instead of adventuring. (2) The hireling-needed qhole render path got the same fix; both call sites used the same suppression pattern. (3) The info-prereq summary row builder (qddBuildInfoPrereqs) now classifies pet items via isPetItemName, sets isPet:true on the row + uses the \ud83d\udc3e icon, and the corresponding Purchase button in qddInfoPrereqHtml routes pet rows to the pets tab. Without (3), info-summary rows previously rendered every item with the gear icon and routed every Purchase button to the gear tab \u2014 broken for pets, but invisible because the suppression at the qhole level hid the broken button anyway. Fixing both guarantees consistency across the two prereq render surfaces. Met-check unchanged. The slot's "is this prereq satisfied" logic at line 34783 (looks for a matching pet in S.partyAssets) is correct as-is. After purchasing a Rat from the store, the pet ends up in partyAssets, the quest modal re-opens via _questModalOpenAfterBuy, the re-render runs the met-check, finds the pet, lights the slot green. Click handler unchanged. The qcs-store-link click handler at line 25781 already includes PETS in its name lookup, so emitting a button with data-tab="pets" for a pet name "just works" \u2014 buy modal opens with the right item, purchase completes, post-buy re-render does the rest. Scope. Three edits in three render paths; click handler + buy commit + met-check + post-purchase reopen all unchanged. No SQL change in this version (the pets table seed shipped separately as seed-pets.sql). No data-model change.
Version 1.7066 — Apply v1.7065's secondary-utility button sizing principle to Contracts and Adventure screens. Contracts: \u27f2 Undo, The Archive, \u2b50 Kudos action row dropped to inline font-size:.65em; padding:.25em .4em matching the Party screen treatment. Adventure: \ud83d\udcc5 Theme, \ud83d\uddfa Map, \ud83d\udcdc Log action row gets the same treatment. Principle being applied. Secondary utility buttons (modal openers, jump-to-section, collapse toggles, undo surfaces) in a sticky action bar above content should read as recessive relative to: (a) the bottom navigation, (b) the content itself (task cards, quest cards, character rows), and (c) the content's section headers. These five buttons all fit that category \u2014 none are the primary action the user came to the screen to take. Deliberately NOT touched. + Add on Contracts and + Group on Archive are primary CTAs and stay at their current size. Store screen has a different structural pattern (inline search / reroll / category tabs) and doesn't fit the "secondary utility above content" model. Settings screen buttons are all configurative and stay at their current size. Modal buttons (Save/Cancel/Yes/No/Close) use a different convention \u2014 their discoverability as primary-action-in-modal matters more than hierarchy recession, so they're left alone. Bottom nav and in-content action buttons (task card \u2715 / \ud83d\udccc / swipe drawer buttons) are the other side of the hierarchy and stay at their sizes. Scope. Two HTML inline-style edits, six buttons total. No CSS changes, no JS changes, no data-model change.
Version 1.7065 — Party-screen hierarchy fix: secondary utility buttons shrunk to match their role. Context. After v1.7063/v1.7064 fixed the roster-row text readability, the top-of-page buttons (ASSETS / STATS / RECRUIT trio + MANAGE SQUADS + COLLAPSE ALL) looked disproportionately massive relative to the newly-legible character rows below. Screenshot confirmed five big uppercase-serif-bold buttons consuming a substantial fraction of the visible viewport before the user saw any party content, and carrying more visual weight than the bottom nav (CONTRACTS / PARTY / QUEST / STORE) which is the actually primary navigation. Root cause. The trio at lines 3290\u201392 had inline min-height:36px with no font-size override, so they inherited the .btn.sm baseline of .68em but then got padded to 36px tall regardless. The two action buttons at line 18196\u201397 were already at an inline font-size:.78em override \u2014 which was up from the .sm baseline, not down \u2014 so they rendered even larger than .68em defaults. What landed. All five buttons (three top + two in the tagbar) dropped to inline font-size:.65em; padding:.25em .4em. The top trio also lost their min-height:36px floor in favor of min-height:30px. These are secondary utility actions \u2014 jumps within the page, modal opens, collapse toggles \u2014 not primary CTAs. They now read as such: visible, tappable, clearly-labeled, but visually recessive so the character roster content dominates the viewport. What this does NOT change. The chip row (Skills/Tools/Lang/Faith/Overlap) is unchanged \u2014 those are toggle chips at a different visual weight tier. Search field and Continue Downtime banner (when visible) also unchanged. Bottom nav unchanged. Territory and squad headers unchanged (those got their own proportional bump in v1.7063 and are now the dominant content-level signals). Scope. Pure CSS / inline-style change. Five buttons, one HTML template, one JS template string. No data-model change, no state change. Tap targets still meet the 30px minimum for touch (the trio sits at exactly 30px; the tagbar buttons use the default .btn padding which totals ~30px at the new font-size too).
Version 1.7064 — Party roster row: actually fix the readability this time. What went wrong in v1.7063. I bumped .prr-meta and .prr-status em values to lift the secondary text above the 15px floor. Didn't work. User screenshot confirmed the class/level row and countdown were still tiny after the deploy. Root cause I missed: (1) .party-roster-row itself has font-size: .8em on the parent (line 1804), so em-compounding was the real shrinker. The per-child bumps from .75em to .88em only moved things from ~10.8px to ~12.7px at 18px root \u2014 still below readable. (2) .prr-status uses font:inherit as a button-reset, and I placed my font-size:.88em declaration BEFORE that line. The font shorthand resets ALL font properties (including size), so my bump was silently overwritten on every render. Classic CSS gotcha \u2014 in my defense the shorthand subtleties aren't obvious until you trace the cascade, but I should have suspected it when the visual change didn't match the code change. What landed in v1.7064. (1) Removed the font-size: .8em from .party-roster-row entirely. The row now inherits cleanly from its grandparent, which inherits from --fs. Em-compounding was baked-in shrink with no obvious benefit \u2014 probably a relic from when the attribute pills were the only content in the row and needed to fit 4+ across. With v1.7044's full roster layout, shrinking the parent just punishes every child. (2) Moved the font-size:.88em declaration on .prr-status to AFTER the font:inherit line so the shorthand cascade doesn't swallow it. With (1)'s parent fix, this now produces ~15.8px at 18px root. Net effect at 18px root. Character name: 14.4px \u2192 18px. Class/level meta (.prr-meta, .92em): 13.2px \u2192 16.6px. Countdown (.prr-status, .88em): 10.8px \u2192 15.8px. Attribute pills (.prr-pill, .72em): 10.4px \u2192 13.0px \u2014 still below the floor but the pills are small by design and the territory/squad bumps from v1.7063 raised the neighboring context size so the hierarchy reads correctly now. Scope. One CSS rule edit plus one property reorder. All the v1.7063 inline-style bumps on territory / squad headers still apply \u2014 that part of v1.7063 was fine. This version fixes only the failing roster-row portion. Diagnostic note for the lesson file. When a CSS change doesn't seem to land: (a) check for font: shorthand AFTER your property in the same rule, (b) check the parent's font-size for em-compounding, (c) check for !important overrides, (d) check media queries at narrower breakpoints. In this case (a) and (b) were both true; (a) alone would have been enough to silently defeat the fix but (b) made the entire subtree too small regardless.
Version 1.7063 — Party screen readability pass + revert of v1.7062's global scale rebalance. What happened in v1.7062. Shipped a global accessibility-scale rebalance (default 18px → 24px, labels shifted Smaller/Small/Default/Large/X-Large). User feedback after deploy: the problem wasn't that everything was too small \u2014 it was that specific secondary metadata elements were too small relative to properly-sized primary elements. Bumping the root font size ballooned already-correct elements (axis chips, buttons, search, character names) while only marginally helping the targeted tiny-text offenders (territory headers, squad meta, class/level, countdowns). The net effect was a worse layout, not better readability. Reverted in this version. DEFAULT_SETTINGS restored to fontSize:18 / accessFontSize:18. normalizeSettings reverts to 22-maps-to-18. Accessibility panel buttons restored to the old labels (Default 18 / Large 20 / X-Large 22 / XX-Large 24 / Huge 26). Users who picked 24 or 26 in v1.7062 keep those values; they'll see XX-Large or Huge highlighted on the panel again. Retained from v1.7062: the latent bug fix. load()'s forced reassignment of S.settings.fontSize=18 and S.settings.accessFontSize=18 is still removed. That was independent of the scale-rebalance decision \u2014 it was silently resetting guest-user choices on every reload. Stays fixed. New targeted fix: Party-screen secondary metadata readability. Identified the actual pain points from the user report: (a) territory panel header with "N squads · M unassigned" subtext rendering at .82em × .78em ≈ 0.64em of root (~11.5px at 18px root, below iOS's 15px secondary-text floor). (b) Squad header "N members" at .72em and gold estimate badge \ud83d\udcb0 ~N at .66em. (c) Class/Level meta (.prr-meta) at .92em desktop / .85em mobile via media-query override. (d) Time-until-free countdown (.prr-status) at .75em. Surgical bumps: territory summary .82em→.95em, subtext .78em→.92em (net root of ~.87em); squad meta .72em→.88em; gold badge .66em→.82em; countdown .75em→.88em; mobile-override on prr-meta removed so the desktop .92em applies everywhere. All six targets now land at 14.8\u201317.1px at 18px root \u2014 at or above the 15px secondary-text floor \u2014 without scaling up any primary content or unintended elements. Scope. Party screen only. Other surfaces (Contracts, Quest, Store, Settings) likely have similar patterns but I didn't touch them to avoid layout regressions elsewhere. If they read as too-small as well, we can do the same surgical pass per-surface. The no HQ warning pill on territory headers (v1.7055) also got a proportional bump to match the new summary size. No SQL change. No data-model change. Pure CSS + one inline-style per surface.
Version 1.7061 — Edit Contract modal now preserves sub-task identity (_sid) and state fields (waiting, waitingNote) across saves. User report. Subtask pin status (\ud83d\udccc / \ud83d\udccd) silently disappeared whenever the user opened Edit Contract on a task with pinned subtasks and clicked Save \u2014 even if no subtask changes were made. The pin icons returned to their unpinned state and the Pinned Tasks zone lost the corresponding rows. Root cause. The save handler at line 14190 flattened t.subtasks into a newline-joined textarea of names, and the reconstruction loop at line 14199 built fresh subtask objects carrying only {name, done}. The _sid (stable subtask id, added v1.6924) was dropped. On the next render, ensureTaskIds() at line 10738 generated a brand-new _sid for each subtask. But the pin registry (S.pinned[]) had entries of the shape {kind:'sub', tid, sid} pointing at the OLD sids. The pin-prune pass at line 10801 then saw every pin's sid as orphaned (no subtask in the task had that sid anymore) and silently deleted the pin registry entries. Visible pins were gone, sid referential integrity was broken. Same regeneration also destroyed the v1.7032 s.waiting and s.waitingNote fields on every save \u2014 so a user who marked a subtask as \u23f3 waiting on someone and wrote a note, then opened Edit Contract to fix a typo in the task title, would lose both the waiting flag and the note on Save. What landed. Extended the existing v1.7019 done-preservation logic at line 14193. The logic matches old subtasks to new by name with FIFO handling for duplicate names \u2014 this correctly handles "subtasks A, B, A" where positional matching would mis-assign state across the two A rows. Retained that matching; just extended what gets preserved from {done} to {_sid, done, waiting, waitingNote}. Rebuilt subtasks stamp the old _sid back on (ensureTaskIds won't overwrite an existing _sid, so the value persists through save + rerender); waiting flag and note are preserved only when truthy/non-empty so we don't pollute subtask objects for the common non-waiting case. Known limitation: rename still loses state. The plain-textarea UI gives no way to distinguish "I renamed X to Y" from "I deleted X and added Y." So a subtask renamed via this modal gets a fresh _sid and resets done/waiting/waitingNote to defaults. Documented in the code comment. A per-row editable list with hidden _sid inputs and drag-handles would fix rename preservation and also give us a proper reorder affordance (currently reorder requires editing the textarea line-order manually); deferred to a follow-up because it's a larger UI refactor (est. 150-200 lines vs. this version's 15). In the meantime: to preserve pins on a rename, do the rename inline via the subtask row's \u270f edit affordance on the main Contracts screen (v1.6957 path) rather than via this modal, because the inline edit mutates name in place without touching _sid. What this covers: the 90% case. Reorder subtasks, delete subtasks, add subtasks, edit the task title or gold value, edit the task notes, archive via the modal, set the burner timer \u2014 all now preserve pin registry entries and waiting state across the save. Scope boundaries. Pure edit-handler change. No UI rewrite, no new state, no persistence plumbing. The archive-path subtask flattening at line 14232 still emits {name, done} only because archived tasks don't have pin UI and archiving a waiting task clears its waiting state by intent. No SQL change. No data-model change. 15-line edit in the save handler of ov-te-yes.
Version 1.7060 — Contracts: Recur action now dedupes prior recurring archive entries for the same task. User report. Completed "Morning Ritual" at ~9 AM, chose Recur → Tomorrow in the done modal. Two hours later the task had re-appeared in the active Contracts list. Diagnosis. Console dump showed four archive entries for "Morning Ritual": (1) the correct new one-shot-tomorrow entry just created (freq:"custom", autoIntervalDays:1, autoIntervalAnchor:"2026-04-24", _oneShot:true); (2) a stale plain-archive from an earlier archive action (inert, correct); (3) and (4) two older freq:"daily", fav:true entries left over from when the task had been set to recur daily at some prior point. checkFavourites runs on page load / focus / visibility-change, guarded by S.lastFavCheck===stamp. When it ran earlier today, the two leftover daily entries both passed the shouldActivate=!!t.fav check for the "daily" branch at line 30161 and pushed Morning Ritual back into S.active via the name-dedupe-guarded S.active.push at line 30196. The one-shot tomorrow entry was correct but irrelevant \u2014 the dailies out-fired it regardless of what the user chose in the Recur modal. Bug category. The v1.7019 Recur action writes a new archive entry but never deactivates older recurring entries for the same task. Every time a user chose Recur on a task that already had a recurring archive entry, the old cadence kept firing alongside the new one. Two-plus active cadences on the same task identity is never intended behavior; the Recur choice should be authoritative. What landed. Single targeted change in ov-done-yes at the Recur branch, inserted right before S.archived.unshift(archEntry). Scans S.archived for entries matching the current task's identity (primary match: _tid; fallback: name for pre-v1.6957 rows without _tid) AND carrying active recurrence signal (any of fav===true, autoIntervalDays>=1, freq==="daily|weekly|monthly|yearly"). Matched entries have their recurrence fields neutralized: fav=false, autoIntervalDays=null, autoIntervalAnchor=null, autoWeekday=null, autoMonthday=null, autoYearlyDate=null, _oneShot=false. The row itself is NOT deleted \u2014 preserved for undo history and for the user to manually re-activate from the Archive screen if desired. The newly-built archEntry below is then the sole active cadence source for that task. If any entries were cleared, logs "Cleared N prior recurrence entries for ‘X’ before applying new schedule" to the task log. Scope boundaries. Dedupe applies ONLY to the Recur path, not to plain Archive or Delete. Rationale: choosing Archive means "dismiss this completion, the schedule stays the same" \u2014 a user who completes one instance of a daily task and wants to just archive it without disturbing tomorrow's reactivation should be able to. Delete is identity-destructive and has no reason to touch the archive. Only Recur is an authoritative statement of "this cadence going forward," so only Recur dedupes. What this does NOT change. checkFavourites logic, archive undo, sbPush/sbPull shape, the 30-day archive rollup, or the Contracts UI. Recur modal options, field semantics, log messages for the Recur action, and the _oneShot burn-on-reactivation behavior all unchanged. Migration for existing users. Users already stuck with duplicate recurring archive entries won't self-heal automatically \u2014 the dedupe only fires when the user next chooses Recur on an affected task. For immediate cleanup, run in DevTools:

(()=>{const target='Morning Ritual';let n=0;(S.archived||[]).forEach(ae=>{if(!ae||(ae.name||'').trim()!==target)return;const r=(ae.fav===true)||(typeof ae.autoIntervalDays==='number'&&ae.autoIntervalDays>=1)||['daily','weekly','monthly','yearly'].includes(ae.freq);if(!r)return;ae.fav=false;ae.autoIntervalDays=null;ae.autoIntervalAnchor=null;ae.autoWeekday=null;ae.autoMonthday=null;ae.autoYearlyDate=null;ae._oneShot=false;n++;});save();renderArchive();return n+' entries cleared for "'+target+'"';})()

Change target string to the task name; the helper neutralizes every recurring archive entry with that name, then saves and re-renders. Non-recurring archive rows are left alone. This is optional \u2014 the user can also manually delete the duplicate archive rows from the Archive screen. No SQL change. No data-model change. Pure logic fix in one function, ~30 lines including the logging and scope-limiting guard.
Version 1.7059 — Quest Map: completed cycles no longer render at the top when no cycle is active. User report. After v1.7058's self-heal cleared the stuck R&B-as-active state, opening the Quest Map still showed "Tier I · Root & Branch" at the top of the incomplete-cycles list with a "\u2713 Completed" badge. It belonged under the "\u2713 Completed (4)" folder at the bottom with the other finished cycles. Root cause. Same activeQuestSet() fallback landmine that v1.7058 patched in renderAdv(), different symptom. renderCycleMapCards() at line ~9744 resolved its activeSet via activeQuestSet(), which falls back to QUESTSETS[0] (R&B) when qs.setId is null. The per-cycle routing block at line ~9866 then computed isActive=activeSet&&activeSet.id===set.id which resolved true for R&B in every no-active-cycle session. The completed-cycle routing (if(isCompletedCycle && !isActive)) excludes active cycles to preserve the "currently-playing" slot position \u2014 but a cycle falsely flagged active-by-fallback was being routed into incompleteCards instead of completedCards, putting it on top of the map. Fix. Resolve activeSet directly from S.questState.setId without the QUESTSETS[0] fallback. When the user has no active cycle, activeSet is null and every cycle's isActive is false. Completed cycles route correctly to the Completed folder; incomplete cycles still render at the top. When the user HAS an active cycle, activeSet resolves to that specific cycle via QUESTSETS.filter() by id \u2014 identical behaviour to activeQuestSet() except the null case is honored. Why not change activeQuestSet() itself. Same reason as v1.7058: the helper has 35 call sites and many rely on the fallback to avoid null-checks. Local fix is correct here: only two uses of activeSet inside renderCycleMapCards() (declaration + the null-safe isActive test) so scope is contained. What this does NOT change. Incomplete-cycle ordering, cycle card contents, Arcane Offerings rack, territory panels, Grind Missions sub-cards, or per-cycle completion badging. No SQL change. No data-model change. Pure render-path fix, 2 lines.
Version 1.7058 — Phantom-Root-&-Branch fix: closes the v1.6911 bug's remaining half. User report. After abandoning all cycles and grinds, the Quest screen kept surfacing Root & Branch with 0/10 progress and "ASSIGN & BEGIN" buttons on every quest, even though R&B had been completed weeks earlier. Diagnosis. Console dump showed qs.setId==="root-and-branch", qs.done===[], but setProgress['root-and-branch'].done===[0..9] and questCycleSquads==={}. The cycle was simultaneously active and archived-complete \u2014 the exact dual-state v1.6945 declared unreachable after adding the isSetCompleted() gate to the cycle-activate click handler. Something still reached it; empty questCycleSquads told us the normal squad-picker flow didn't create this state. Even with qs.setId=null (the clean post-abandon state), renderAdv() falls through to activeQuestSet() which returns QUESTSETS[0] (R&B) as a fallback, painting it fresh with all quest buttons clickable. That's the v1.6911 bug's remaining half: v1.6911 cleared the stale live arrays but didn't close the renderer fallback path. (1) renderAdv() self-heal at render time. When qs.setId points to a cycle whose archive reports full completion (setProgress[setId].done.length ≥ totalQuests) but live qs.done is smaller, the handler nulls qs.setId and clears every live working array (pending, done, repeatCounts, revealed, assigned, grindPending). The archive in setProgress[setId] is untouched \u2014 re-visiting the cycle via Quest Map → Quest Log still shows it as completed. save() fires so the corrected state persists. Console logs a [renderAdv] notice for forensics. Idempotent: re-running on clean state is a no-op. (2) renderAdv() no-active-cycle empty state. When qs.setId is null (post-abandon-all, or after the self-heal in (1)), #avcontent now renders “No active quest cycle — Open the Quest Map to pick one” with a \ud83d\uddfa Open Quest Map button instead of painting the QUESTSETS[0] fallback. The button wires a one-shot click to #btn-cycle-map, re-attached on every render because #avcontent gets innerHTML-replaced wholesale. THEME/MAP/LOG chrome sits outside #avcontent and stays visible \u2014 the existing MAP button in that row still works for anyone who looks up there first. Squad banner, auto-collapse detection, and calendar rollover checks all ran before the early-out, so nothing load-bearing is skipped. (3) ov-cycle-squad-yes completion gate. Defense-in-depth matching v1.6945's cycle-activate gate. The squad-picker confirm handler is the final commit point for activateQuestCycleSet(setId) and was previously un-gated. Added an isSetCompleted(setId) re-check at commit time \u2014 if the cycle finished while the picker was open (a race the v1.6945 gate can't catch at click time), route to the Quest Log instead of activating. Closes a secondary re-activation path that could have been the original corruption source. (4) navigateToNextQuest completion gate. Same pattern applied to the second un-gated caller of activateQuestCycleSet. This one fires from the cycle-activate handler when a squad is already mapped to the cycle (assignedSquadForSet(setId) returns truthy). The outer v1.6945 gate protects most of that flow, but since navigateToNextQuest is also a standalone helper reachable from other surfaces, the local gate is the correct place. Why all four together. (1) and (2) fix the user-visible symptom and self-heal existing corrupted saves on next load. (3) and (4) close the two remaining secondary activation paths that could have originally put the state into corruption \u2014 so even without knowing the exact trigger (the reporter's save had no log of the path that reached the bad state), future franchises can't reach it. Migration note. Users currently stuck (like the reporter) will see their save self-heal on first load after deploy \u2014 qs.setId nulls, live arrays clear, the Quest screen shows the empty state, and Supabase picks up the corrected state on next push. The R&B archive (setProgress['root-and-branch']) with its full done:[0..9] + repeatCounts is preserved intact, so Quest Log → Root & Branch still shows all ten quests as completed. isSetCompleted('root-and-branch') continues to return true, keeping grindsUnlocked() and any downstream gates satisfied. Scope boundaries. No SQL change. No data-model change. Four localized edits: two in renderAdv() (self-heal + empty state), one in ov-cycle-squad-yes, one in navigateToNextQuest. activeQuestSet() itself is not modified \u2014 it has 35 call sites and changing its fallback behavior would be a much bigger audit. Instead, the renderer detects the null-setId condition and routes around the fallback.
Version 1.7057 — Inbox routing fix (deferred from v1.7056). Resolves the long-standing "inbox links don't consistently work" report by making every inbox row land the user on the surface that can actually resolve the item. Problem recap. Since v1.7004's inbox shipped, every quest and grind row in the inbox modal had an onClick that just called navTo('adventure'). That routes to the currently-active cycle's Adventure screen, which fails three distinct ways: (a) quest rows for non-active-cycle quests were never even in the inbox to begin with \u2014 the collector only scanned qs.pending (live active-cycle) and missed setProgress[sid].pending for every other cycle, so any quest returned on a paused/switched-away cycle was invisible; (b) quest rows for active-cycle quests landed correctly but the user had no feedback about why it worked sometimes; (c) grind rows landed on the Adventure screen which doesn't show grinds at all since v1.6913 \u2014 grinds live in the Quest Map's Grinds modal (openGrindsList()), so the user had to close out and navigate manually. The v1.7056 changelog documented this as deferred; this version ships the fix. (1) Inbox now covers every cycle. _collectInboxItems unions qs.pending with every setProgress[sid].pending for non-active cycles (matching the v1.6905 pattern for party status / busy-scan). Each item tracks the source setId. For non-active rows, the subtitle appends \u201c\u00b7 on <CycleName>\u201d so the user knows they're about to context-switch. Grinds continue to read only from qs.grindPending because v1.7017 explicitly clears setProgress[sid].grindPending for non-active cycles \u2014 grinds are territory-scoped, not cycle-scoped (v1.7013), so there's no archive path to add. (2) Quest row click activates the right cycle. If the target row's setId differs from the current active cycle, the onClick calls activateQuestCycleSet(setId) before navTo('adventure'). That helper is the same path the Quest Map's Activate button uses \u2014 it calls saveActiveSetProgress() for the outgoing cycle, then loadSetProgress() for the new one, then renderAdv(). The user lands on the Adventure screen where the just-ready quest is visible and collectable. Idempotent when the target cycle is already active (round-trips through save/load on the same setId). (3) Grind row click opens the Grinds modal. Each grind row carries its territory (v1.7013) and the onClick now calls openGrindsList(territory) which is the authoritative entry point for the Grinds modal, scoped to the specified territory (Minsaka vs Gojimura shifts). Subtitle gains \u201c\u00b7 <territory> shift\u201d so multi-territory franchises see which shift the grind belongs to. Falls back to navTo('adventure') if openGrindsList is somehow unavailable. (4) Clear Inbox extended to non-active-cycle pendings. The \u2709 Clear Inbox button's dismiss loop now walks setProgress[sid].pending for every non-active cycle and sets _inboxDismissed on each ready entry. Without this extension, users with returned quests from a paused cycle could never clear their inbox badge \u2014 the button would report \u201cNothing to clear\u201d even though (1) was surfacing non-active items in the visible list. Grinds are skipped in the archive walk for the same reason (1) skips them on the collection side: v1.7017 clears non-active grindPending arrays. (5) Collect All: gracefully surfaces non-active-cycle items it can't touch. The \u2713 Collect All button continues to only process active-cycle qs.pending and qs.grindPending \u2014 collectQuest() needs a live qs reference + matching set object to award rewards correctly, and batch-swapping cycles mid-collection is risky. Instead, the button now counts ready pendings on non-active cycles and (a) when those are the only ready items, alerts \u201cN item(s) are ready on other cycles. Tap each inbox row individually to switch cycles and collect.\u201d; (b) when it successfully collects active-cycle items but non-active ones remain, logs a matching hint to the adventure log so the user knows why the inbox isn't empty. Per-item click (from fix #2) gets them to each non-active cycle with one tap each. A future version could refactor collectQuest to accept explicit (qs, set) arguments and auto-loop through cycles inside Collect All, but that's a larger change than warranted for this report. What this does NOT change. Downtime rows (already correct \u2014 they open openDowntimePendingModal directly). Polling cadence, red-dot animation, z-index, button positions, the v1.7056 header layout, the v1.7055 \u201c\u26a0 no HQ\u201d badge, persistence paths. Collect All reward-distribution logic is untouched; it just has better empty-state and post-completion feedback. Scope boundaries. One function rewritten (_collectInboxItems), one handler extended (ov-inbox-dismiss / Clear Inbox), one handler adjusted (ov-inbox-collect / Collect All, two small additions \u2014 alert branch + log line). No new state fields, no new DOM elements, no CSS changes, no migration. No SQL change. No data-model change.
Version 1.7056 — Notification system consolidation + header compaction. Four user-driven changes shipping together. (1) Downtime ribbon retired. The standalone #downtime-ribbon bar (v1.6904) between #hdr and the dev bar is removed entirely. Pending downtime rolls continue to surface as \ud83d\udd6f entries in the inbox modal (that path has worked since v1.7004's _collectInboxItems); the bar was duplicative. renderDowntimeRibbon() is left as a no-op rather than deleted because it has ~8 call sites scattered through load, roll-resolution, and downtime-completion paths, and the function exits cleanly when the DOM element is absent (existing pre-check at line ~15853). Removing the function would have required five more hunks; leaving it harmless costs nothing. The #downtime-ribbon HTML and its comment block are gone. (2) Roll button hides after click in pending-downtime modal. Previously the \ud83c\udfb2 Roll All button flipped to \u201c\u2713 Done\u201d after the animation completed, but that state had no remaining function and added visual weight after the results list was already visible. The button now sets style.display='none' post-animation. openDowntimePendingModal() resets style.display='' on entry so if a second batch of downtimes arrives and the modal reopens, the button reappears. (3) Inbox button moved into #hdr, title compressed to "RQ", sub-nav bar removed. The v1.7004 #subnav bar is deleted. Its sole child (the \u2709 envelope button with red-dot notification badge) moves inline into #hdr-row's grid, inserted between the gold/QP display and the \u2699 settings gear. The grid template expands from 44px 1fr auto 44px to 44px 1fr auto 36px 44px to accommodate the new cell; the inbox button uses the standard btn.bink.sm header-button styling and hosts an absolutely-positioned .inbox-dot span (new CSS class, renamed from .subnav-dot to match its new home). The \u2709 glyph and dot state / polling / click-to-open-inbox behavior are unchanged \u2014 only the DOM location moved, and the click handler still targets #btn-inbox so no wiring change was needed. To make room on the header bar for the new button, the #hdr-title H1 always displays the fixed short label "RQ \u2694" in applySettings(), regardless of the user's custom appName setting. The user's full appName still drives the browser tab title (via document.title) and the subtitle below the main title (via #hdr-sub), so renaming the franchise remains supported \u2014 only the top-bar label is fixed. --sub-h CSS custom property is retired and #app's padding-top simplifies from calc(var(--hdr-h) + var(--sub-h) + 14px) back to calc(var(--hdr-h) + 14px). Net reclaim on mobile iOS: ~36px of vertical screen content area (32px in compact-hdr mode). (4) Tavern / named-building inclusion in origin HQ label + latent field-name bug fix. The Party screen's \ud83e\udded HQ panel for the origin territory now reads "Headquarters: The Golden Stag\u2009\u00b7\u2009Minsaka" when the player has purchased and named a qualifying \u22651000 gp cycle-owned building in origin. When no qualifying building exists (common for franchises that haven't bought any real estate yet), the label stays the plain "Headquarters\u2009\u00b7\u2009Minsaka" it was before. Pre-existing latent bug (since v1.7018) fixed in the same edit. The territoryHQName() helper read m.name off the asset-meta object, but the purchase flow saves the user-chosen name into m.customName (see finishRealEstateNaming at line ~9387; same convention used by assetDisplayName at line 7631, the Party Assets renderer at line 18771, and the Animal naming field at line 18719). m.name is never populated on the meta object. So non-origin territory HQ panels have been showing the base building name (e.g. "Tavern\u2009\u00b7\u2009Gojimura") instead of the player's custom name (e.g. "The Golden Stag\u2009\u00b7\u2009Gojimura") since v1.7018 shipped. Fixed by reading m.customName. Non-origin panels now correctly surface the player-chosen name for the first time; the fix composes with (3)'s origin extension so the two behaviors are consistent \u2014 both territories honor named buildings, origin additionally prefixes with "Headquarters:" to keep the HQ anchor legible. Scope boundaries. No change to: squad creation gating, Relocate modal, cycle-owned real estate catalog rendering, Party Assets section (it already reads m.customName correctly), grind clock-in rules, inbox polling / dot badge state, persistence paths, or the v1.7055 "\u26a0 no HQ" badge behavior. Item 3 from the user report (inbox entries don't consistently activate the specific quest/grind/cycle) was investigated \u2014 confirmed as real (inbox onClicks call navTo('adventure') which lands on the currently-active cycle, not the cycle that the inbox entry belongs to; grinds have no cycle-scoped view at all so their inbox links land on an unrelated screen) \u2014 but deliberately NOT fixed in this version. Reserved for a follow-up because the right fix requires each _collectInboxItems entry to carry cycle/grind context in its onClick, which is a larger plumbing change. No SQL change. No data-model change. Four coordinated display/UI changes plus one latent bug fix.
Version 1.7055 — Party screen: surface pre-rules-era legacy territory state with a "\u26A0 no HQ" badge. User-reported symptom. The Gojimura territory zone was rendering on the Party screen with a "Headquarters \u00B7 Gojimura" HQ panel for Torinn and the Solarians squad \u2014 but the user had never completed a Gojimura cycle, so they had never had the chance to purchase a 1000+ gp building to unlock the territory under the v1.7006 rules. Root cause. Diagnostic console dump showed S.unlockedTerritories contained only ["Minsaka"], but 11 characters and one squad carried homeTerritory:"Gojimura" stamps. The Party screen's render loop (line 17992) defensively appends any bucket territory not in S.unlockedTerritories to the ordered render list \u2014 so the zone surfaced anyway, driven by the homeTerritory stamps. territoryHQName() correctly returned its "Headquarters" fallback string because no qualifying building existed, producing the misleading label "Headquarters \u00B7 Gojimura" that read as if a real HQ existed. How the legacy state arose. Pre-v1.7039, S.unlockedTerritories was never persisted to Supabase \u2014 every cross-device pull reset it to origin-only. This franchise likely had Gojimura in the unlocked list transiently during earlier testing (before v1.7039's franchise-settings-blob persistence fix), created Solarians there via the v1.7006 squad creation picker while the territory was unlocked in-memory, and then a subsequent pull stripped the unlocked-list. The squad + character homeTerritory stamps survived (those fields ride on their own persistence paths) but the unlocked-list authority fell back to origin-only. Since then, recomputeUnlockedTerritories() has never re-added Gojimura because no qualifying building exists to regenerate the unlock. Design decision: no auto-heal. The three available fix paths were (a) re-add Gojimura to unlocked via a migration scanning homeTerritory stamps, (b) strip the stamps and relocate everyone to origin, or (c) keep the data as-is and fix the UI honesty. Option (c) was chosen because new franchises created after v1.7039 cannot reach this state \u2014 the quest cycles were begun before the territory-rule change was in effect for this specific save. No other user on the system should have the same orphaned-stamps condition. Auto-healing the unlocked list would silently contradict the v1.7006 gating contract for anyone whose state happens to match this pattern for a non-legacy reason. What landed. (1) New helper territoryHasHQBuilding(territory). Sibling to territoryHQName. Returns true for origin (always has HQ by definition, no building required) and for any non-origin territory with at least one \u22651000 gp ownerType:'cycle' asset in S.partyAssets whose owning cycle's territory matches. Returns false otherwise \u2014 the signal the UI uses to surface the warning badge. (2) HQ unassigned-bucket panel header. When the bucket's homeTerritory is non-origin AND territoryHasHQBuilding returns false, prepend a small crimson-pilled "\u26A0 no HQ" badge to the existing (N / 10) cap indicator. Tooltip: "No qualifying 1000+ gp building in this territory \u2014 legacy state from before the territory rules." (3) Territory zone wrapper header. Same badge, same gating, rendered between the territory name and the existing "N / 4 squads \u00B7 M unassigned" count readout. Two places to land the badge because the wrapper is the most prominent claim of territory legitimacy; the HQ panel is the player's direct destination for unassigned-character actions. Both need to read honestly. Scope deliberately limited. Real squad panels (e.g. the Solarians banner itself) do NOT get the badge \u2014 the warning applies to the territory as a whole and showing it on every squad panel inside an already-badged zone would be redundant. The badge is purely a display signal; no data change, no state field, no migration, no re-assignment logic. Every existing code path around S.unlockedTerritories, sq.homeTerritory, c.homeTerritory, territoryHQName, charHomeTerritory, and the Party screen render loop continues to work unchanged. The zone renders, squads operate, the HQ panel accepts unassigned characters as before \u2014 the UI just now reads the truth about whether a real headquarters backs the territory. Scope boundaries. Does not touch: squad creation gating (still requires a territory to be in S.unlockedTerritories), Relocate modal (still reads from S.unlockedTerritories), grind clock-in rules, per-territory bucket caps, or any persistence path. Does not add a migration. Does not alter territoryHQName's fallback behavior \u2014 the label still reads "Headquarters \u00B7 Gojimura" when no building exists; the badge is additive signal alongside that label. No SQL change. No data-model change. One new helper function, two new emit-time badge insertions, all reading existing state.
Version 1.7054 — Recovery release: corrected push-before-pull logic after v1.7053 caused data loss. v1.7053 is skipped. Not deployed widely. The v1.7053 fix for the iPhone ghost-pending-grinds bug introduced a worse bug: the "unpushed changes" flag was set by saveLocalState(), which fires not only on user actions but also on boot normalization (initSession line ~32493), migration healers, and various post-pull cleanup paths. Result: every boot had the flag set, so push-first always fired, sometimes with partial or stale localStorage state that overwrote good server state. At least one user lost progress to this. The correct fix (this version). Move the flag-set up one level to save() instead of saveLocalState(). Only save() \u2014 the genuine user-action write path \u2014 marks the local state as having unpushed changes. Boot normalization, pull hydration, migrations, and dedupes all call saveLocalState() directly without going through save(), so they no longer flag work as unpushed. Push-before-pull gating. At boot, initSession() checks _hasUnpushedChanges(). If true, there's genuine pending work from the previous session that never made it to Supabase (most commonly: user did an action, then the 4-second auto-push debounce never fired before the tab was closed or reloaded) \u2014 push first to get it to the server, then pull. If false, the local blob is known to match a prior successful push \u2014 pull directly and trust the server. Resurrection safety net. The pre-v1.7053 restore-local-pending-if-server-empty block is KEPT but now gated on the same _hasUnpushedChanges() flag. When the flag is clear (no unpushed work), resurrection does NOT fire \u2014 the iPhone ghost-pending bug from v1.7052's trace is killed. When the flag is set and the push-before-pull path fires but somehow doesn't make it (e.g. network flapped between push and pull), the resurrection block provides a last-chance restore for local work. Belt-and-braces. Apology + lessons. I should have recognized during v1.7053 authoring that saveLocalState() is called from many non-user paths, including the mandatory boot-time saveLocalState() at initSession line ~32493 that fires on every startup. The flag was effectively always set. The fix for the ghost-pending bug made the push-first path fire every time, which made a second class of bug visible (stale push overwrites good server state). Moving the flag up to save() is the minimum change that correctly isolates user-driven writes from boot-time bookkeeping writes. Scope boundaries. No new features. No new UI. No new Supabase columns, no migrations. The v1.7052 diagnostic logger is retained. The boot_sync event now also records the snapshot lengths of localPending and localGrind for forensic continuity. If a user sees data loss or ghost return AGAIN after this release, the log will show exactly which path fired and with what values. No SQL change. No data-model change. No changes to any feature behavior beyond the startup sync decision tree.
Version 1.7052 — Sync forensic logger + concurrent-tab detector. Context. User reported four distinct sync symptoms over 4 hours: duplicate quest/downtime completions re-appearing, a spent QP voucher whose balance credit didn't land on the redeemer's account, contracts deferred or set to recur returning to the active screen, and lost Pinned Spotlight entries. Console had zero errors. The v1.7041 persistence self-test reported 1499 pass / 0 fail on regular-user round-trip and 41 pass / 0 fail / 5 skip on code-session coverage. All required Supabase table columns were confirmed present. By elimination, the bugs are not in the plumbing (fields correctly round-trip when tested in isolation) — they arise from interactions between sessions that the self-test cannot simulate. Leading hypothesis: multi-tab / multi-device concurrent sessions where a stale tab's debounced push overwrites fresh server state from another tab. The 4-second autoPushTimer debounce + Promise.all(ops) parallel push architecture + lack of server-side version checking means "last write wins," and the loser silently loses data. This version ships diagnostic instrumentation to confirm (or refute) that theory. (1) RQ_SYNCDIAG module. A new IIFE at line ~7138 provides an in-memory ring buffer (200-entry cap) that captures every push/pull/save event with a compact state fingerprint. The fingerprint includes: gold, qpBalance, qpEarnedToday, activeLen, archivedLen, pinnedLen, partyLen, squadsLen, partyAssetsLen, qsPendingLen, qsGrindPendingLen, qsDoneLen, qsSetId, qsCycleNum, qpLedgerLen, qpPendingLen, plus compact hashes of task ids, first-5 archive titles, and pinned-item digest. The buffer persists to localStorage['rq_syncdiag:<uid>'] so a browser crash or refresh doesn't lose forensic history. API: log(type, payload), fingerprint(), diff(a, b), detectConcurrent(), getAll(), clear(), getTabId(), startHeartbeat(). Exposed as window.RQ_SYNCDIAG for console access. Lightweight when dev mode is off (only heartbeat writes, no fingerprint capture); full detail only when dev mode is on. (2) Heartbeat-based multi-tab detection. Each tab writes its tabId + timestamp to localStorage['rq_heartbeat:<uid>'] every 10 seconds under a sub-key keyed on the tabId. Heartbeats older than 30 seconds are considered stale. When detectConcurrent() reads the map, it returns every fresh heartbeat from a tab OTHER than our own — a non-empty result means "someone else is running this account right now." A beforeunload handler removes our own heartbeat on page close so other tabs see us go away promptly. A storage-event listener fires whenever another tab writes to either the heartbeat key or the account's acqv7: save key; both write a concurrent log entry so the forensic trace shows exactly when a cross-tab write happened. (3) Instrumentation points. Five sites hooked: saveLocalState logs save events; sbPush entry logs push_start + completion logs push_ok / push_fail; sbPull captures a BEFORE fingerprint at entry, logs pull_start, then at success logs pull_ok with a diff report showing exactly which fingerprint keys changed between what we had and what the server returned. pull_fail logs on catch. Both regular-user and code-session paths are instrumented. A visibilitychange handler logs every foreground/background transition so the trace captures when a tab was actually being used. (4) UI: 🔬 Sync Diagnostic Log modal. New button in Settings → Developer Tools section, right below 🧪 Run Persistence Self-Test. Opens ov-sync-diag modal showing: (a) a live concurrent-tab indicator at the top — green "✓ No other tabs detected" or crimson "⚠ N other tabs active on this account" with their ages; (b) a color-coded reverse-chronological event list with type, timestamp, tabId, payload summary, fingerprint one-liner, and concurrent-tab markers for any event logged during a multi-tab moment; (c) footer buttons 📋 Copy (serializes the full buffer as formatted JSON to the clipboard — for pasting back into a support conversation), ↻ Refresh (re-renders without re-opening), 🗑 Clear (wipes the buffer after confirm). (5) Console export. RQ_SYNCDIAG.getAll() returns the buffer; JSON.stringify(RQ_SYNCDIAG.getAll(),null,2) gives a copy-pasteable dump. On dev-mode boot the console prints "🔬 RQ_SYNCDIAG active (tabId …). Open Settings → Dev → 🔬 Sync Diagnostic Log to view" so users know the tool is armed. Scope boundaries. Pure observation. Never mutates S, never makes network calls, never interferes with the existing push/pull pipeline. If a logging call throws (e.g. localStorage quota exhausted), the try/catch eats it silently — instrumentation never affects real behavior. Performance cost: a single fingerprint computation (~50μs) per push or pull; heartbeat writes a small JSON blob every 10 seconds. Negligible. Planned follow-ups. Once we have traces showing the actual failure mode, the v1.7053+ work will likely need: (a) cross-tab coordination via BroadcastChannel or the storage event, so a push in Tab A can notify Tab B to pull before its own next push; (b) server-side version/etag column on franchise + key tables so stale pushes get rejected by RLS rather than silently overwriting; (c) or — simplest — a "foreground tab owns writes" lock where only the currently-visible tab of a given account runs sbPush. Scope TBD based on what the forensic traces show. Data privacy note. The log stores only the fingerprint numbers (counts, flags, ids) — never raw task names, character names, or content text. Safe to paste the copy-exported JSON into a support conversation. No SQL change. No data-model change. No changes to any existing feature behavior.
Version 1.7051 — Dev-bar access for two test accounts. User need. Routine testing happens on stable@stable.com and restart1@restart.com rather than on the primary admin account (566fc455-3cd5-4a93-8663-9b361296e5a9), because those are the accounts that exercise regular-user flows the way a student would experience them. But the v1.6959 admin lockdown gated dev-mode access to a single UID, so reaching the dev bar from inside a test account required logging out, logging in as admin, enabling dev mode, logging out, logging back in as the test account \\u2014 and then dev mode wouldn't carry over anyway because isDevModeActive() re-checks isAdmin() on every call. Net effect: Justin could not get to the Ledger, QP Manager, +1 Day, or sync-diagnostic tools from inside the accounts where he most needed them to diagnose user-reported sync issues. What landed. Added both test-account UIDs to ADMIN_UIDS at line ~27579: 972f5001-ca4f-4388-9e03-3e9d9dcfa1f1 (stable@stable.com) and 57e9c9c9-c37b-4d4e-9744-100dca070d08 (restart1@restart.com). Each now has full admin access to the dev bar and the admin-only buttons btn-enable-dev, btn-gen-code, btn-gen-bulk. Inline comment clarifies the provenance (developer-controlled test accounts, not student accounts). Security posture unchanged. The v1.6959 model is preserved: isAdmin() is a UID-allowlist check, isDevModeActive() requires both isAdmin() AND S.settings.devModeOverride. A student whose UID is not on the list cannot enter dev mode even with devModeOverride=true in their blob. The ?dev=1 URL parameter (the actual credential-leak vector fixed in v1.6959) stays disabled \\u2014 it was the auto-login-with-hardcoded-password that was dangerous, not the param itself. Adding explicit UIDs to the allowlist is the precedent v1.6959 set: \"Add more UUIDs here to grant admin.\" RLS note. The client-side allowlist is hide-UI-only. Real server-side authorization for login_codes, content_items (official scope), and any other admin-only tables is (or should be) enforced by Supabase RLS policies keyed to the actual authenticated UID \\u2014 not by a client flag. If RLS is not already restricting admin writes to the primary admin UID, the two new test UIDs will now ALSO be able to write admin-restricted data. Audit recommended before treating this as production-ready. For the current development deployment context (single developer, controlled accounts, no student access to these UIDs or their passwords), the risk is negligible. Scope boundaries. No other changes. No new features, no UI edits, no persistence plumbing, no cost tuning. Pure four-line allowlist addition plus a comment block explaining why. No SQL change. No data-model change. Verification. node --check on the extracted script body passes clean.
Version 1.7050 — QP Manager audit + downtime-cost display bug fix. User-reported symptom. Changed Advance Downtime cost from 5 to 2 in the QP Manager, saved, and pushed to Supabase \u2014 but the Party screen's \ud83d\udd6f Continue Downtime bar kept multiplying by 5. Root cause. The QP Manager Save handler at line ~23254 called save() and updateQpDisplay() but never re-triggered renderParty() or renderAdv(). Every QP-cost-labeled button on those screens (Continue Downtime, Auto-Assign Grind, Recall/Rest/Early-Complete, Rebalance, the v1.7047 \u21c4 transfer row labels) is drawn on renderParty/renderAdv ticks, so saving a new cost value didn't refresh any of them until something else happened to trigger a re-render. Persistence itself was correct \u2014 the value was in S.settings.qp.costDowntime, rode the franchise settings jsonb column cleanly, and read back correctly on pull. Audit context. The bug inspired a full audit of every QP-spend option to verify each reads cost dynamically from qpCfg() at render/click time. Four unrelated issues surfaced and are fixed together here. Fix 1 \u2014 QP Manager Save now re-renders. Save handler now calls renderParty(), renderAdv(), updateSqmgrFocusToolbar(), and (if a quest-assignment modal is currently open) qddRender(). Every cost-labeled button picks up the new value within one paint frame. No more stale labels. Fix 2 \u2014 Back-to-HQ plumbed through qpCfg().costBackToHQ. The character-peek "\ud83c\udfe0 Back to HQ" button had 10 hardcoded in six places (button label at line ~16636, two alerts at ~19121/19122, confirm dialog at ~19132, the actual qpSpend(10,...) at ~19173, and the adventure log entry at ~19181). This was the last remaining hardcoded qpSpend literal in the entire codebase \u2014 every other qpSpend call already read from qpCfg(). Added costBackToHQ to normalizeSettings defaults (default 10, matching prior behavior) and replaced all six hardcoded values with qpCfg().costBackToHQ||10 reads. The v1.6943 and v1.7047 changelogs both explicitly noted "character peek's 10 QP Back to HQ button is unchanged" \u2014 it was never plumbed. It is now. Fix 3 \u2014 costAssignToSquad exposed in QP Manager UI. This field (added by v1.6943, default 5 QP, drives the "\u2694 join" open-seat cost in the v1.6942/6943 Assign-to-Squad modal) was defaulted in settings but had no input surface in the manager. The v1.6943 changelog flagged this as a follow-up: "Not yet exposed in the QP Manager UI \u2014 Adding a dedicated manager field is a 3-line patch." Shipped.
