New input field labeled "Assign to squad (open seat join)" sits between Auto-assign and Territory squad swap in the cost-knobs grid. The Territory-squad-swap label itself was also clarified to "Territory squad swap / full-squad seat trade" since that single knob governs both the v1.572 Territory Swap Zone and the v1.6943 full-squad bump trade. Fix 4 \u2014 Back-to-HQ input field added to QP Manager. New "Back to HQ (character peek)" row under the squad-swap row, paired with Fix 2 so the user can tune the cost without editing via console. Fix 5 \u2014 costAutoAssign save-fallback typo. The QP Manager Save handler had parseInt(...)||5 for costAutoAssign, but the normalizeSettings default and the openQpManager read both use 1. A user who cleared the field and clicked Save would get 5 instead of 1 \u2014 subtle inconsistency, corrected to ||1. Fix 6 \u2014 Stale "Auto-assign (1 QP)" tooltip at line ~25071. updateQuestModal wrote a hardcoded tooltip string even though the button TEXT at qddRender (line ~34085) correctly read qpCfg().costAutoAssign. The tooltip now uses the same dynamic cost so hover text matches button label. What the audit did NOT change. Four "dead-knob" cost fields in the QP Manager (costQuestSplit, costMagicItem, costRename, costHomebrew) are editable in the UI but have zero read sites \u2014 they're UI-only today. User explicitly opted to KEEP these for now rather than strip them, on the chance the features land later. Flagged in the audit report for future cleanup or activation. No data-model change, no SQL change. All five new user-editable fields ride the same franchise settings jsonb column that holds every other QP cost. Existing saves without costBackToHQ or costAssignToSquad fall through to defaults (10 and 5) and get the fields stamped on next save. Scope boundaries. Pure audit + plumbing. No gameplay rules changed, no rebalance, no new features \u2014 every cost defaults to its prior value. The only user-visible difference is (a) the Continue Downtime bar now reflects Manager changes without a reload, (b) three new editable cost fields in the Manager, (c) Back-to-HQ alerts and confirm dialog now cite the current cost. Verification. node --check on the extracted script body passes clean. Grep for qpSpend\\([0-9] (hardcoded literal args) returns zero matches \u2014 confirming every QP spend in the app is now parameterized.
Version 1.7049 — Party/squad consolidation plan, final step: Squad Manager \u2194 Quest Preview direct link + UX polish. Closes out the 5-version sequence (v1.7044 attribute columns, v1.7045 overlap coloring, v1.7046 shopping-mode narrow, v1.7047 transfer buttons, v1.7048 readability). (1) Neutralized axis pill colors. The v1.7044 per-axis tints (teal skills / brown tools / slate lang / gold faith) conflated two signals: axis identity and overlap coloring. The axis is already labelled by the column header; the same info on the pill background was noise. Collapsed all four prr-pill-* variants to a single neutral parchment-tinted style. Overlap coloring (v1.7045) remains the only source of pill color, so seeing a colored pill now unambiguously means \"shared with another character in the current scope.\" The axis-variant CSS classes (prr-pill-tools, prr-pill-lang, prr-pill-rel) are still emitted by the renderer but have no CSS rules targeting them \u2014 kept as no-ops in case we ever want axis-distinction back without a render-path edit. (2) \u201C\uD83D\uDED2 Shop for this squad\u201D CTA in Quest Preview. New gold button in the Quest Preview list\u2019s squad banner, rendered only when the current _qpvSquad has at least one shopping-list entry. Clicking closes the preview list and opens the Squad Manager pre-entered into shopping mode for this exact squad \u2014 skipping the three-step old navigation (close preview \u2192 navTo party \u2192 Manage Squads \u2192 \uD83D\uDED2 Shopping \u2192 squad picker). Mirrors the tail of _openShoppingMode() without the picker stage since we already know the target. Stashes window._qpvReturnAnchor = {setId, squadName} so the Squad Manager can offer a reverse jump. (3) \u201C\u2190 Back to Quest Preview\u201D button in the shopping banner. The Squad Manager shopping-mode banner (the gold-dashed strip above the Fill Role donor list) now renders a teal \u201C\u2190 Back to Quest Preview\u201D button whenever window._qpvReturnAnchor is set. Clicking consumes the anchor (one-shot, cleared immediately so later reopens don\u2019t re-enter shopping-mode unexpectedly), closes the Squad Manager, re-opens the Quest Preview list for the remembered setId, and restores _qpvSquad to the anchor\u2019s squad name. The player sees updated coverage percentages on every quest row immediately \u2014 which is the whole point of shopping: see what\u2019s missing, swap people in, check what\u2019s now covered. Button absent when there\u2019s no return anchor (i.e. player entered Squad Manager via the normal Manage Squads toolbar rather than via the Quest Preview CTA), so existing workflows are unchanged. (4) Action-oriented line on each donor row. The Fill Role donor cards previously showed name / class / level / source squad / matched tags / delta %. Easy to miss the actual effect of a click. Added a third line between the meta line and the tags: Move: [source] \u2192 [target] in teal bold. For cycle-locked donors the same line flips to \uD83D\uDD12 Locked to current cycle in crimson so the refusal reason reads as prominently as the action would have. Mirrors the \u201Cremove X, transfer Y in for cost Z\u201D framing from the consolidation-plan spec, adapted to the Squad Manager context where moves are free and cost disclosure isn\u2019t part of the story. Design note on cost. Squad Manager\u2019s Fill Role has always moved characters for free via sqmgrAssignChar; the openAssignSquadModal path that\u2019s used by the v1.7047 row-level \u21C4 button charges 5\u201310 QP. That asymmetry is pre-existing and intentional \u2014 Squad Manager is positioned as the free composition tool for deliberate planning; the row-level \u21C4 is the quick-and-costed ad-hoc path. This version doesn\u2019t change either cost behavior; it just makes the Squad Manager path faster to enter and clearer to read. (5) Drag handles + swipe drawers: still not shipping. Same rationale as v1.7048\u2019s \u201Cwhat this version does NOT include\u201D: the \u21C4 row button reaches every transfer scenario, HTML5 drag on iOS Safari needs substantial polyfill work, \u201Cdrag to HQ\u201D has no natural drop target. The action-line addition in (4) above closes the remaining UX gap where it was hard to see what would happen when clicking a donor \u2014 which was the real value of drag-to-reorder anyway. Scope boundaries. Pure UI + dispatch. No data-model change, no new state fields (the return anchor is a transient window._qpvReturnAnchor, one-shot, never persisted). The shopping-list data itself (S._shoppingList) is untouched. The existing \uD83C\uDFAF Fill Role and \u2696 Rebalance flows are unchanged \u2014 only the shopping-mode banner and the donor-card markup inside Fill Role got touched. Self-test should pass unchanged. No SQL change. No data-model change. Consolidation plan summary (v1.7044\u20131.7049). Party screen: attribute pill columns with four axis toggles, overlap coloring with squad/territory/franchise scope, per-character \u21C4 transfer button, per-squad \u21C4 Relocate button, shopping-mode auto-narrow. Quest Preview: direct \u201C\uD83D\uDED2 Shop for this squad\u201D jump to Squad Manager. Squad Manager: \u201C\u2190 Back to Quest Preview\u201D reverse jump, clearer donor action line. Persistence: four new fields on S (partyAttrAxes, partyOverlapOn, partyOverlapScope, partyShoppingOverride) plumbed through all eight surfaces per the v1.7041 self-test protocol. Ten stacked UX improvements to how the player analyzes squad composition without a single database migration.
Version 1.7048 — Party-screen overlap readability: opaque pill fills + partner names in tooltips. Two small but meaningful fixes to v1.7045 overlap coloring based on live user feedback. (1) Opaque pill fills. v1.7045 used an 18%-alpha rgba() fill on colored pills. This meant the stripe-dark vs stripe-light row alternation from v1.7045's readability pass modulated the pill colors \u2014 the same \u201CAthletics\u201D pill appeared as two different shades depending on which row it sat on, making it genuinely hard to confirm that two pills were \u201Cthe same color\u201D across alternating stripes. Fix: compute a blended opaque fill \u2014 take the saturated palette hex and blend it 75% toward white (r\u2019 = r + 0.75*(255-r), same for g and b). The result is a washed pastel version of the palette color that reads consistently on any background because it has no alpha. The saturated hex continues to drive the border and the pill text, so pills still have a visible color identity at a glance. Side effect: pills now read stronger overall because the fill no longer gets diluted by the row background. (2) Partner names in tooltips. v1.7045's tooltip read generically \u201CShared with others in squad scope.\u201D That told the player a pill was shared but not with whom. Hovering a pill now shows the actual other-character names: \u201CShared with Alice, Bob (territory scope)\u201D. Extended computePartyPillOverlap's return shape with a new partnersFor(ci, axis, value) method that walks the same scope-bucket counts map colorFor uses, filters out the querying ci itself, and resolves remaining cis to c.name strings (with #ci fallback for defensive cases). The empty-stub fallback path (used when S.partyOverlapOn is false) also got a partnersFor stub returning an empty array so the roster emit path doesn't need a guard \u2014 both methods are safe to call in both states. Implementation notes. Blend math runs per-pill at render time; the cost is trivial (three Math.rounds per overlapping pill, skipped entirely for non-overlapping pills since they hit the plain-pill fast path). The partnersFor lookup is O(members-in-scope-bucket), same as the existing colorFor lookup; both reuse the same counts map built once per render. What this version does NOT include. Desktop drag handles on roster rows for within-territory squad swaps. After review I decided against shipping drag: (a) the v1.7047 \u21C4 transfer button already reaches every squad + territory move, including open-seat joins (5 QP) and full-squad seat trades (10 QP); (b) HTML5 drag on iOS Safari (the primary test device) needs substantial polyfill work to feel native, which would dwarf the actual drag-to-swap logic; (c) \u201Cdrag to send to HQ\u201D would need a drop target that doesn't exist (the per-territory HQ virtual panel), adding visual complexity. The row-level \u21C4 button is already one click, is keyboard-accessible, works on iOS with no compromise, and goes through a well-tested modal that handles every cost and refusal path. Drag would save half a tap in a rare flow at the cost of another ~300 lines of iOS-robust drag code. Deferred permanently unless a specific workflow surfaces that needs it. Scope boundaries. Pure visual + tooltip change. No data-model change, no new state, no persistence work. Self-test should pass unchanged. The v1.7047 transfer buttons, v1.7046 shopping-mode, v1.7045 overlap-toggle + scope cycle, and v1.7044 attribute columns are all unchanged below the pill-emit layer. No SQL change. No data-model change.
Version 1.7047 — Party-screen transfer buttons: per-character (\u21C4) and per-squad (\u21C4 Relocate). Fourth step of the party/squad consolidation plan (v1.7044\u20131.7046 preceded). This is the \u201Ctransfer actions\u201D sub-slice of the planned drag/swipe work; drag handles and swipe drawers are deferred to v1.7047b to keep this ship small. (1) Per-character transfer button. Each thin roster row grows a small teal \u21C4 button between the axis cells and the status pill. Clicking opens the existing v1.6942/6943 Assign-to-Squad modal (openAssignSquadModal(ci)), which handles the entire price ladder the app already enforces: costAssignToSquad (5 QP, open-seat join), costSquadSwap (10 QP, full-squad seat trade), cycle-lock refusal, busy-set awareness, and the \u201C\u2295 Create new squad\u201D fallback. Because the existing modal already supports cross-territory moves (no territory gate in that flow), the \u201Cfree within-territory / 10 QP cross-territory\u201D distinction you described is handled by the destination squad\u2019s homeTerritory, not by the button itself. (2) Locked state for busy characters. Any character whose _msUntilFree(ci).ms > 0 (on a quest, grind, or downtime) shows a muted \uD83D\uDD12 button instead of \u21C4, disabled with a hover tooltip describing the activity kind and time-until-free (\u201COn a quest \u2014 cannot transfer until 1 hr 42 min from now\u201D). This is a UI hint \u2014 openAssignSquadModal itself already refuses busy chars via _buildBusySet, so the button state is belt-and-braces. New CSS: .prr-xfer (teal pill, hover darkens), .prr-xfer.is-locked (muted grey, not-allowed cursor), .prr-status-cell (flex wrapper that holds button + status pill side-by-side). (3) Per-squad Relocate button. The squad-panel header grows a \u21C4 Relocate button next to the existing \u270F Edit button, shown only for real squads (not virtual HQ panels) and only when S.unlockedTerritories.length >= 2. Clicking opens a new lightweight modal ov-sq-relocate that lists every other unlocked territory as a button with its current squad-count fraction (N / SQUADS_PER_TERRITORY_MAX). Each button is disabled if: (a) the target territory is at squad capacity (N/4 squads full), or (b) the franchise can\u2019t afford the cost. Cost: costSquadSwap \u00d7 member count (so a 5-character squad costs 50 QP to relocate; a 10-character squad costs 100 QP). The cost + balance are shown in the modal message row. (4) Relocate implementation. openSquadRelocateModal(sqi) handles the full flow: cycle-lock gate (an alert() refusal with \u201Cfinish or abandon current cycle first\u201D text), unlocked-territories count check, confirm dialog, qpSpend call, then the atomic update \u2014 sets sq.homeTerritory = target AND walks sq.memberIdxs setting each character\u2019s c.homeTerritory = target. Logs a \uD83D\uDDFA entry to the hire/adventure log: Squad "X" relocated: OldTerr \u2192 NewTerr (N QP). Re-renders party + squad manager after apply. If QP is globally disabled (!qpEnabled()) the cost step is skipped and the message reads \u201CQP system off \u2014 relocation is free.\u201D (5) Event handling. Both data-a=\"char-transfer\" and data-a=\"sq-relocate\" live in the #plist click delegator. The squad-relocate handler also calls e.preventDefault() + e.stopPropagation() because the button lives inside the squad\u2019s <summary> element, which would otherwise toggle the panel collapsed/expanded state on the same click. Integration with the shopping-mode / attribute / overlap columns from v1.7044\u20131.7046. The transfer button is a new column that grows the status area; the grid template was already auto-sized via minmax(60px,auto) for the status column so the extra button fits without a grid-template rewrite. On mobile the entire status cell stacks to its own row (via the existing @media (max-width:640px) rule on .party-roster-row), which means on narrow viewports the transfer button is on its own row below the status \u2014 acceptable since narrow rows can\u2019t fit a tight side-by-side layout anyway. What this version does NOT include. Drag handles on rows for within-territory squad swapping. Swipe-drawer on rows for a secondary action menu (e.g. \u201C\uD83C\uDFE0 Back to HQ\u201D, \u201C\u2695 Start downtime\u201D directly from the row). Those two pieces (mirror of v1.6980 desktop drag + v1.6925 mobile swipe from Contracts) are the bigger lift and ship as v1.7047b. Persistence. No new state fields \u2014 this version is pure UI + dispatch. Every modification routes through existing persisted paths (c.homeTerritory from v1.7040, sq.homeTerritory from v1.7006, QP ledger from v1.6858, etc.). Self-test expected to pass unchanged. Scope boundaries. The Squad Manager\u2019s Territory Swap Zone (v1.572) is unchanged. The character peek modal\u2019s \u201C\uD83C\uDFE0 Back to HQ\u201D button (v1.6900, 10 QP) is unchanged. The v1.6942 \u201Cunassigned \uD83E\uDDED\u201D chip on the charpeek modal still routes to the same openAssignSquadModal \u2014 now that same modal is reachable directly from the party roster without opening charpeek first. No SQL change. No data-model change.
Version 1.7046 — Party-screen shopping-mode integration (Plan A, step 3 of 5). What landed. When a squad has a non-empty entry in S._shoppingList (the per-squad needed-attribute list authored by Quest Preview + Squad Manager from v1.6941), that squad's party-screen roster auto-narrows to display only the pills for attributes on its list. The global S.partyAttrAxes axis toggles (v1.7044) are ignored for that squad; instead, the roster shows one column per axis that has at least one shopping-list item, and inside each column only the list-whitelisted values are rendered. Characters without a matching pill on a column show the em-dash placeholder, so the player can immediately see which squadmates cover which needed attributes. Per-squad banner. Each squad in shopping mode grows a small gold-dashed banner above its roster: \uD83D\uDED2 Shopping mode \u00b7 narrowed to N list items \u00b7 \u2190 Manual. The Manual button opts that one squad OUT of shopping mode (stored in a new S.partyShoppingOverride[squadName]=true flag). The squad then displays with the normal v1.7044 axis-toggle-driven layout, and the banner flips to a muted Manual display \u00b7 shopping list present (N) \u00b7 \uD83D\uDED2 Shopping with a button to re-engage shopping mode. The override is per-squad and persistent, so the player can have one squad in shopping mode while a sibling squad in the same territory is in manual display. Column header prefix. In shopping mode, each column label gets a \uD83D\uDED2 prefix (e.g. \uD83D\uDED2 \u2B50 Skills) so the reader can tell at a glance that the column is narrowed rather than showing every skill the character has. On mobile the column headers hide and the per-cell labels handle that role; the \uD83D\uDED2 prefix propagates into the mobile ::before treatment naturally since the label source is the same string. Overlap coloring compatibility. v1.7045's overlap toggle continues to work unmodified. In shopping mode, pills are filtered before overlap coloring is applied, so a pill that paints means \u201Cthis attribute is on the shopping list AND shared with at least one other character in scope.\u201D That's actually the ideal signal for shopping decisions: it tells you \u201Canother squadmate could cover this so you don't strictly need to recruit for it\u201D without the visual noise of coloring attributes that aren't on the list. Unassigned panels. Virtual per-territory HQ panels never enter shopping mode (they have no shopping list of their own). Their display continues to follow the global axis toggles. Rename + disband migrations. The v1.6955 sqSave rename branch now migrates S.partyShoppingOverride[oldName] \u2192 [newName] alongside _shoppingList and _sqmgrPins. Disband explicitly removes the disbanded squad's override entry so it doesn't linger as an orphan keyed by a name that no longer exists. The shopping list itself is intentionally preserved on disband (matches v1.6941's _sqmgrPins treatment \u2014 the list survives in case the player re-creates a squad with the same name). Click path. The \u2190 Manual / \uD83D\uDED2 Shopping button dispatches through the existing #plist click delegator as data-a=\"party-shop-override\" with data-sqname. The handler toggles the squad's entry in S.partyShoppingOverride and re-renders. No new modal, no new UI surface beyond the banner button. Persistence. S.partyShoppingOverride plumbed through all eight persistence surfaces per the v1.7041 self-test protocol: createDefaultState seed (empty object), regular-user sbPush (cleanSettings._partyShoppingOverride), regular-user sbPull (read-back + strip from cleanSettings), code-session _keys array, self-test EXPECTED_CODE_KEYS, self-test FRANCHISE_FIELDS, self-test _serializeFranchiseRow, self-test _deserializeFranchise. Self-test would fail loudly on a miss. What's still deferred. Drag handles + swipe transfer on rows (v1.7047), Squad Manager wizard refactor (v1.7048). Scope boundaries. Pure additive. The v1.7044 manual display is unchanged when no shopping list exists for a squad (same render path, same axis-toggle resolution). Shopping-list editing still happens in Squad Manager as before \u2014 this version only adds a read surface for the list on the party screen. No changes to Quest Preview, Squad Manager, the shopping-list data model, or any adjacent feature. No SQL change. No data-model change beyond the additive S.partyShoppingOverride field + additive _partyShoppingOverride key in the settings blob.
Version 1.7045 — Party-screen attribute pills: readability pass + overlap coloring with scope toggle. Readability. First user report on v1.7044 was that the parchment-with-lines background was bleeding through between roster rows and making it hard to tell where one character's cells ended and the next began. Added alternating-stripe row backgrounds: odd rows get a creamy opaque fill (rgba(255,255,255,.55)), even rows get a subtle warm grey (rgba(44,26,14,.05)), each row has a border-radius:4px and a 2px gap separating it from its neighbors. The parchment pattern no longer interferes. Old dotted-line separator removed — the color contrast is doing that job now. Overlap coloring. New toggle chip \uD83D\uDD17 Overlap in the party tagbar; when on, a second chip appears showing the current scope (Squad / Territory / Franchise) with a \u21BB indicator that cycles on tap. When overlap is on, any attribute pill whose (axis, value) is shared with at least one other character inside the current scope paints with a color keyed off that value. All \u201CAthletics\u201D pills across the page share one hue, all \u201CPerception\u201D pills share another, etc. Colors come from an 8-hue saturated palette (red / orange / gold / olive / forest / teal / slate / purple) spread around the wheel for maximum mutual contrast against both row backgrounds; each color applies as border (saturated), fill (18% alpha), and text (saturated) simultaneously so even small pills read their color. Non-shared pills stay in the neutral axis tint from v1.7044. Scope semantics. Squad scope \u2014 for a character in a named squad, the bucket is their squadmates; for an unassigned character, the bucket is others in the same territory's HQ panel (so a Minsaka hireling and a Gojimura hireling don't cross-color just because both have Arcana). Territory scope \u2014 bucket is every character in the same charHomeTerritory, squadded or not. Franchise scope \u2014 one big bucket, the whole party. Implementation. A new helper computePartyPillOverlap(scope) builds the scope-bucketing once per render and returns a { colorFor(ci, axis, value) } API that the roster emit path calls per pill. The helper is deliberately different from v1.6936's computeOverlapCiSet (which answers a pair-wise question for the Squad Manager's card overlay) \u2014 this one answers the simpler question \u201Cdoes this pill's (axis,value) appear in \u2265 2 characters within my scope bucket?\u201D When overlap is off, the render path substitutes a stub {colorFor:function(){return null;}} so no conditional is needed at each pill. Palette color choice. Value-\u2192-color mapping uses a cheap stable string hash of axis+\u2019:\u2019+value into the 8-color palette, so every franchise sees the same color for \u201CAthletics\u201D and nobody has to learn a new key. Collisions are expected (there are \u226440 total axis-values in typical play but only 8 colors) \u2014 the goal is \u201Csame attribute = same color within the session,\u201D not globally distinct. Persistence. Two new fields added: S.partyOverlapOn (boolean) and S.partyOverlapScope (string enum: squad / territory / franchise). Plumbed through all eight surfaces per the v1.7041 self-test protocol: createDefaultState, regular-user sbPush (_partyOverlapOn / _partyOverlapScope), regular-user sbPull + strip, code-session _keys, self-test EXPECTED_CODE_KEYS, self-test FRANCHISE_FIELDS, self-test _serializeFranchiseRow, self-test _deserializeFranchise. The self-test would fail loudly on a miss. What's still deferred. Shopping-mode auto-narrow of the pill set to the shopping list (v1.7046), drag handles + swipe transfer on rows (v1.7047), Squad Manager wizard refactor (v1.7048). Scope boundaries. Pure additive. v1.7044 roster layout is unchanged except for the per-pill inline-style override when overlap is active. No existing code paths altered. No SQL change. No data-model change beyond the two additive S fields + two additive _partyOverlap* keys in the settings blob.
Version 1.7044 — Party-screen attribute columns (Plan A, step 1 of a multi-part party/squad consolidation). User goal. The Squad Manager has accumulated composition-analysis functionality (coverage %, Focus Mode, Overlap insignia) that's most useful when the player is already looking at the party roster. This version begins moving that analysis onto the Party screen itself, so the player can see what each squad covers without opening a separate modal. What landed in this first pass. (1) Four axis chips in the #party-tagbar: \u2B50 Skills, \uD83D\uDD27 Tools, \uD83D\uDDE3 Lang, \uD83D\uDE4F Faith. Each chip is an independent on/off toggle; active chips glow teal, inactive read as muted parchment pills. Default: only Skills on. Tap any chip to toggle its column across every squad panel on the page. (2) Columnized thin-roster rows. The existing thin-roster grid (name \u00B7 class/level \u00B7 status) now grows per-axis pill columns between class/level and status whenever the corresponding axis chip is on. The layout is a true CSS grid (.party-roster-row), not a flex row, so pills line up column-by-column down the roster \u2014 who has Athletics reads at a glance without eye-jumping across members' individual pill lists. Members who have no attributes on a given axis render a thin em-dash (\u2014) placeholder in that cell so column alignment holds. A column-header strip above the roster labels each axis column. (3) Pill color coding by axis. Skills pills are teal, Tools pills are wood-brown, Lang pills are slate-blue, Faith pills are gold. Subtle tints (~10% alpha fills with matching 1px borders) so the columns read as distinct without shouting. (4) Mobile responsive layout. Below 640px the grid collapses via a @media rule: name/meta/status keep their positions on row 1, and each active axis cell takes a full-width row below with its emoji label (\u201C\u2B50 Skills\u201D) prepended via a CSS ::before. Column headers hide on mobile since the per-cell labels replace them. Reading down a squad on a phone still scans cleanly by axis. (5) Persistence. The toggle map lives on S.partyAttrAxes as {skills, tools, lang, rel} booleans. Plumbed through all four persistence surfaces at authoring time per the v1.7041 self-test protocol (ninth field to ride in the settings blob via underscore-prefix piggyback): createDefaultState seed, regular-user sbPush (cleanSettings._partyAttrAxes), regular-user sbPull (read-back + strip from cleanSettings), code-session _keys array, self-test EXPECTED_CODE_KEYS, self-test FRANCHISE_FIELDS, self-test _serializeFranchiseRow and _deserializeFranchise. The self-test would fail loudly if any of those eight sites were missed. What's deliberately NOT in this version. No overlap coloring yet \u2014 pills are neutral in color except for axis-distinction tints. Overlap detection with a three-way scope toggle (squad / territory / franchise) ships in v1.7045. No shopping-mode auto-narrow yet \u2014 this ships in v1.7046. No drag handles / swipe transfer on rows yet \u2014 ships in v1.7047, reusing the v1.6980 desktop-drag + v1.6925 mobile-swipe patterns from Contracts. No Squad Manager wizard refactor yet \u2014 ships in v1.7048. Why this ordering. The attribute-pill component is the shared foundation both overlap coloring and shopping-mode narrowing need to read; building it in isolation first means v1.7045 and v1.7046 are small additive edits rather than parallel reworks. Scope boundaries. Pure additive. No existing roster logic changed beyond the outer emit (name/meta/status buttons still have the same data-a attributes, same click handlers, same charpeek and char-status dispatch). The grid template is computed per-row but all the data flows through unchanged. Single-col roster (all axes off) renders identically to pre-v1.7044 except for the CSS class rename (.party-roster-row instead of the old inline flex). Reading the axis flags takes one _charAttrsOnAxis call per member per active axis \u2014 cheap (existing helper, used by Squad Manager's Focus Mode). No SQL change. No data-model change beyond the additive S.partyAttrAxes field + the additive _partyAttrAxes key in the settings blob.
Version 1.7043 — Tutorial & Reference zone (Settings) — scaffold + first topic. What landed. New #settings-tutorial-sec between Contract Preferences and Change Log on the Settings screen. Data-driven architecture: a single TUTORIAL_TOPICS array defines every topic (id, category, icon, title, summary, body HTML, optional admin-only spec details, optional relatedScreen deep-link target). A single renderTutorialSection() function builds category tabs + a filtered topic list; _buildTutorialTopicCard() expands one entry into a <details> element. Adding a new topic = one object in the array; no new DOM or render-function work per topic. Categories. Five categories defined: Basics, Workflow, Squads & Quests, Advanced (all public), and Reference (admin-only via the existing isAdmin() allowlist — same gate as class codes and dev tools; non-admin accounts never see the tab or its topics). Active category tracked in module-local _tutorialActiveCat, defaulting to 'basics'; if a non-admin somehow holds a 'reference' selection from a previous session it snaps back to basics on next render. First topic: The Split action (Workflow). Explains the cutline-modal split flow on task groups in Contracts with a 4-step how-to, a note about metadata preservation on both halves, and a "Try it on the tasks screen →" deep-link button. Admin users also see a collapsed "🔧 Spec / Reference" sub-section with implementation notes (undo integration, metadata inheritance, mobile-vs-desktop cutline UX). Deep-link mechanism. Topics with a relatedScreen property matching a SCRMAP key render a "Try it" button that calls navTo() with that screen id — piggybacks entirely on the existing navigation infrastructure. Guard: only renders when navTo is a function AND the screen id exists in SCRMAP, so topics referencing screens that were removed or renamed degrade gracefully (no button, no error). Scope boundaries. Pure additive scaffold — no existing render paths changed. renderSettings() gained one new line (renderTutorialSection();) at its end; no other function altered. No changes to data model, persistence, or any other feature. Future increments will add topics to TUTORIAL_TOPICS (Getting Started, Waiting Hourglass, Pinned Spotlight, QP Economy, Inbox, Projects, Quest Preview & Coverage, etc.). The admin-only Reference category is intended for spec-level notes useful when working on the app itself — round-trip-strip pattern catalogue, persistence plumbing conventions, the __meta channel packing rules — without cluttering the student-facing view. No SQL change. No data-model change. No persistence change.
Version 1.7042 — Downtime Ledger persistence fix (eighth occurrence of the round-trip-strip pattern) + self-test whitelist completeness pass. User-reported symptom. The Franchise Dashboard's 🕯 Downtime Ledger tile has been showing "0 rolls · 0 unlocks · 0 pending" for a long time, even on franchises with extensive downtime history. User confirmed the issue pre-dates v1.7039 — it's a long-standing bug, not a recent regression. Root cause. S.downtimeRollHistory (capped at 200 entries) and S.pendingDowntimeRolls, both added by v1.6904 to drive the Downtime Ledger tile + the "🎲 Downtime completed ×N — tap to roll" ribbon, were never plumbed through sbPush or sbPull for regular users. The in-memory state was populated correctly via resolveDowntimeRoll() and runDowntimeCompletion(), and survived within a session via saveLocalState (which JSON-stringifies the whole S), but every Supabase pull wiped both arrays back to []. The Ledger read from S.downtimeRollHistory at getDashboardCards() line ~34399 and at the detail-modal render, so immediately after any pull the tile reverted to zeros regardless of actual history. Same pattern as v1.7039 (territory), v1.7040 (roles, qpPending, qpLedger, qpLoginStreak, qpLastLoginDate, c.homeTerritory, c.statsClass). Why the v1.7041 self-test didn't catch this. The self-test's FRANCHISE_FIELDS whitelist was itself incomplete — it covered the seven fields fixed by v1.7040 but didn't include downtimeRollHistory or pendingDowntimeRolls. The v1.7040 audit report correctly categorised these as "regular-user push/pull missing" but the fix wasn't scoped into that version (v1.7040 Category A focused on five specific fields and deferred the downtime arrays as session-local-looking even though they're not). The self-test faithfully reported a clean pass for every field in its whitelist, but the whitelist had a blind spot. This is exactly the "self-test whitelist drift" failure mode flagged in the v1.7041 changelog as a known limitation. Fixes (three coordinated changes): (1) Franchise sbPush now writes _downtimeRollHistory and _pendingDowntimeRolls into the settings blob. downtimeRollHistory is capped at 200 entries on write, matching the in-memory cap enforced by resolveDowntimeRoll. pendingDowntimeRolls is typically small (queued completions between trigger and dice animation, usually 0–5 entries) so no cap is needed. Both keys join the underscore-prefixed blob-key pattern established by _qpPending, _qpLedger, _originTerritory, etc. (2) Franchise sbPull now reads both keys back and strips them from cleanSettings. Fallback behavior: only overwrites in-memory values when the blob value is a valid Array, so pulls from pre-v1.7042 rows don't wipe values from localStorage. First save after upgrade writes the new keys; subsequent pulls restore authoritatively. (3) Persistence self-test whitelist + serialize/deserialize updated. FRANCHISE_FIELDS gained downtimeRollHistory and pendingDowntimeRolls; _serializeFranchiseRow and _deserializeFranchise helpers updated to cover the same blob keys. The tool now validates these fields round-trip correctly, and future round-trip-strip regressions on either field will be caught at authoring time. Deployment behavior. On first load after deploy: the in-memory S.downtimeRollHistory and S.pendingDowntimeRolls are still whatever survived from localStorage (possibly empty if the user has recently pulled from another device). On the next debounced push after any state change, both arrays write to the franchise.settings blob. From that save forward, the Dashboard tile will correctly display the accumulated history and any subsequent pull restores from Supabase. Consequence for pre-existing test data. Saves that have been through multiple cross-device pulls since the Downtime Ledger launched (v1.6904) may have permanently lost history — once sbPull stripped the array and a subsequent sbPush overwrote the blob, the historical rolls aren't recoverable from Supabase. Going forward, every new roll is preserved. Eighth occurrence of the round-trip-strip pattern. Catalogue: (1) v1.543 pct, (2) v1.6995 bagOfHolding/effect, (3) v1.6930 pinned/split_group_meta, (4) v1.7001 _col, (5) v1.7033 done/waiting/waitingNote, (6) v1.7039 homeTerritory/originTerritory/unlockedTerritories, (7) v1.7040 roles/homeTerritory(char)/statsClass/qpPending/qpLedger/qpLoginStreak/qpLastLoginDate, (8) v1.7042 downtimeRollHistory/pendingDowntimeRolls. Meta-lesson. The v1.7041 self-test is only as good as its whitelist. When adding the tool, I correctly flagged this limitation but didn't force a completeness audit of the whitelist itself. Going forward: (a) when shipping a fix for a round-trip-strip bug, ALSO extend the self-test whitelist to cover that field so future regressions on it are caught automatically; (b) periodically re-run the grep-audit of all S/sq/c/meta fields against the self-test whitelist to catch blind spots like this one. Scope boundaries. Read-only bug fix — no UI/gameplay/migration changes. The Dashboard tile and Ribbon rendering logic is unchanged; they correctly read from S.downtimeRollHistory and S.pendingDowntimeRolls and will display accurate counts the moment the underlying arrays are correctly populated. No SQL change. No data-model change beyond the two additive blob keys.
Version 1.7041 — Persistence round-trip self-test (dev tool). Context. Seven versions (v1.543, v1.6930, v1.6995, v1.7001, v1.7033, v1.7039, v1.7040) have shipped fixes for the same class of bug: an additive state field added to the in-memory model without the corresponding plumbing through sbPush and sbPull. Each time, the bug escaped local testing because saveLocalState JSON-stringifies the whole S and works fine. Only cross-device pulls surface the regression, often weeks or months after the field was introduced. The v1.7040 changelog flagged a pending dev-tool to catch this pattern at authoring time; this version ships that tool. What landed. A new dev-mode button 🧪 Run Persistence Self-Test in Settings > Dev section, plus a console entry point window.rqPersistenceTest(). The tool runs a full serialize→deserialize cycle in memory (no Supabase network calls) against the current S and reports which fields round-trip cleanly. How it works. Two independent checks: (1) Regular-user round-trip. The tool contains duplicated copies of the sbPush row-construction logic (franchise, party, squads) and the sbPull row-extraction logic (same three tables). It builds the rows that sbPush would insert, feeds those rows into its sbPull-style rebuilders, and compares field-by-field against the deep-cloned original S. Any field that doesn't match is a round-trip failure. The duplication is deliberate — if the real sbPush drifts from this reference (e.g. someone adds a field to the real code but not the tool, or vice versa), the tool will flag a divergence failure, which is exactly the signal we want. When intentionally updating persistence, the developer must update both the real sbPush AND the tool's _serializeFor*/_deserialize* helpers; the tool then confirms the new field round-trips. (2) Code-session coverage. For the class-code login path (where all state is serialized as a blob via a hand-maintained _keys array), the tool checks that every S-level field currently set is either (a) in an EXPECTED_CODE_KEYS whitelist that mirrors the real _keys array, or (b) on a SESSION_LOCAL_EXPECTED allow-list of fields intentionally excluded (stock, stockDate, doneEnc, encAssigned, pendingQuests, partyTagFilter). Any S-field that's neither in the whitelist nor explicitly session-local is flagged as a potential round-trip-strip bug. Whitelist maintenance. Two hand-maintained lists drive the tool: EXPECTED_CODE_KEYS (mirrors the real _keys) and SESSION_LOCAL_EXPECTED (fields intentionally not persisted). When adding a new persistent field, update both the real code AND the tool's whitelists. When adding a new session-local field, add it only to SESSION_LOCAL_EXPECTED. The tool's failure on an unwhitelisted field is the prompt to decide which bucket the new field belongs in. Output. Results render as a summary alert (pass/fail counts per path) with full field-by-field detail logged to the console. Failures include the before-value and after-value side by side so the exact drift is visible. The full results object is also returned from rqPersistenceTest() for programmatic inspection or unit-test integration. Field coverage. The regular-user round-trip currently exercises: franchise — gold, franchiseStorage, downtimeJobs, questLog, splitGroupMeta, pinned, kudos, qp* (balance, earnedToday, earnDate, pending, ledger, loginStreak, lastLoginDate), originTerritory, unlockedTerritories; party — 32 character fields including the v1.7040 additions (homeTerritory, statsClass); squads — name, emoji, color, memberIdxs, homeTerritory, roles (the v1.7040 addition). The code-session check covers all S-level fields present in the current createDefaultState output plus any dynamically-added fields. What the tool doesn't catch. Fields inside complex substructures (e.g. partyAssets[].meta, S.questState.setProgress, subtask.__meta) aren't individually enumerated — the tool treats them as opaque blobs. If a new field is added INSIDE one of those and the serialize path strips it at a sub-level, the tool won't see it. This is a limitation worth flagging for the next audit: a future version could recurse into known-complex substructures with per-sub-structure whitelists. For now, top-level field coverage catches the most common pattern (every shipped round-trip-strip bug so far has been at the top level of S, sq, c, or meta — none have been deeper). Access control. The button appears only in Dev Mode (behind the existing passcode gate at btn-enable-dev). The window.rqPersistenceTest() console function works regardless of dev mode since it's read-only (no state mutation) and only useful to someone with console access anyway. Recommended usage. Run the self-test (a) before releasing any version that adds a new field to createDefaultState, S.party.push(...) sites, or S.squads.push(...) sites; (b) after any modification to the sbPush/sbPull row-construction blocks; (c) as a periodic sanity check when investigating a cross-device-sync bug report. A clean pass is a reasonable assurance (but not a proof) that the persistence layer is healthy for the fields currently in the whitelist. Scope boundaries. Read-only: the tool never mutates S, never calls save(), and never touches Supabase. Safe to run at any time. Execution is fast (a few hundred ms for a typical save of ~30 characters + ~5 squads + ~15 assets). Future enhancements flagged but not shipped: (1) recursive coverage of known-complex substructures (partyAssets meta, questState.setProgress); (2) an automated assertion that EXPECTED_CODE_KEYS matches the real _keys by extracting both at runtime via source parsing — would eliminate the hand-maintained duplication; (3) integration into a CI-style pre-save hook that runs the test before every sbPush and warns if failures are detected. No SQL change. No data-model change. No changes to any existing functionality. Pure additive dev tool.
Version 1.7040 — Systematic persistence audit + five Category A fixes + code-session completeness pass (seventh occurrence of the round-trip-strip pattern, plus deliberate audit-driven hygiene pass). Context. v1.7039's note flagged that the territory-persistence fix was the sixth occurrence of an additive state field being introduced but not plumbed through sbPush/sbPull, and that a systematic audit was overdue. This version is that audit. Method. Grep-enumerated every S.X, sq.X, c.X, and meta.X field in the codebase; cross-checked each against the regular-user sbPush column lists + settings-blob keys, the sbPull extractions, and the code-session _keys array. Every field not covered by one of those three persistence surfaces is a latent bug. Five active bugs (Category A — silently corrupting user data on cross-device pulls) plus sixteen code-session gaps (Category B — class-code login users losing state on multi-device use) were catalogued and fixed together. Category A fixes (user-visible data loss): (A1) sq.roles round-trip strip. The Captain/Quartermaster/Patrol Leader/Field Medic/Scout squad-role assignments from v1.574 were never in the squads sbPush column list. Every cross-device pull wiped role assignments; squad banners on the adventure page lost their Captain's buff calculations, kit-ownership badges on the party screen disappeared, and the squad-edit modal's role dropdowns returned to all-unassigned. The in-memory shape {roleId: ci} uses party indices, which aren't stable across devices (S.party rebuilds fresh on every pull), so persistence needs name-based translation. Fix: extended the v1.7039 __sqmeta__ pack on the squads.name column to also carry a rolesByName map — {roleId: characterName}. sbPull extracts rolesByName from both new-schema and legacy branches, then translates back to {roleId: ci} by looking each name up in the freshly-rebuilt S.party. Characters no longer present in S.party (renamed, removed, or data drift) drop silently from the roles map — matches how memberNames→memberIdxs handles the same situation. Empty maps are normalized to null on the rebuilt squad object to match the in-memory "roles never assigned" shape. (A2) c.homeTerritory round-trip strip. v1.7006 added a per-character homeTerritory field with a render-time backfill in renderParty (line ~16720) that reassigns missing values from squad.homeTerritory || S.originTerritory. For origin-territory-only franchises this invisibly masks the bug — the backfill resolves to origin (Minsaka) every render, which happens to be correct. The bug surfaces as soon as a franchise unlocks a second territory: characters stamped with homeTerritory='Gojimura' survive locally via saveLocalState (localStorage JSON-stringifies the whole S) but are stripped on every Supabase pull; the render-time backfill then silently relocates them back to Minsaka. Fix: piggyback c.homeTerritory and c.statsClass (A3, below) into the existing stats jsonb column under reserved underscore-prefixed keys _homeTerritory and _statsClass. The real stat keys (str/dex/con/int/wis/cha) are three letters with no underscores, so the reserved keys can't collide. sbPush constructs _statsWithMeta=Object.assign({},c.stats||{}) and stamps the reserved keys; sbPull deep-clones stats, lifts the reserved keys off, and deletes them from the clean stats object so c.stats ends up pure ability scores again. (A3) c.statsClass round-trip strip. Added by v1.6955 as an ASI pre-req — e.g. The Founder's statsClass:'Bard' overrides cls:'Expert' for CLASS_STATS table lookup at ability-score-improvement levels (4/8/12/16/19). Stripped on pull, The Founder's ASI falls back to CLASS_STATS.default, silently applying wrong stat bonuses. Low-severity (affects only The Founder, hireling graduates, and any custom class-stat-override characters, and only at five specific levels), but real. Fix: piggybacked alongside homeTerritory in the stats jsonb pack, as described above. (A4) S.qpPending and S.qpLedger actively stripped. The franchise sbPush had an over-aggressive delete loop at line ~26903 that stripped _qpPending, _qpLedger, and _qpBalance/_qpEarnedToday/_qpEarnDate from cleanSettings before the upsert. Three of those five legitimately moved to dedicated columns — but qpPending (QP vesting queue) and qpLedger (recent history, capped 100) never got columns and were only ever meant to ride in the settings blob. The sbPull path at line ~27490 reads rawSettings._qpPending and rawSettings._qpLedger back, but the blob never contained them — every cross-device pull reset both to []. Pending QP rewards (downtime-completion vests, e.g.) silently lost; QP Manager history tab showed empty. Fix: removed _qpPending and _qpLedger from the delete loop, explicitly write them into cleanSettings from S.qpPending / S.qpLedger before the upsert (with the same 100-entry cap on ledger). (A5) S.qpLoginStreak and S.qpLastLoginDate never plumbed. Added by v1.6858 for the daily QP login bonus. The values are set by checkLoginLoyalty() but were never written to or read from Supabase. Streak reset to 0 on every cross-device pull; the QP Manager displayed "Login streak: 0 QP" / "last: never" until the user logged in again on that device. Fix: piggybacked on the settings blob as _qpLoginStreak / _qpLastLoginDate, matching the same pattern used for qpPending and qpLedger. Category B fixes (code-session blob completeness pass): sixteen top-level S fields that were top-level on S but absent from the code-session _keys array. Code-session users (class-code login path) save their entire state as a blob to login_codes.player_data; any field not in _keys is silently dropped. Added: QP state (qpPending, qpLedger, qpLoginStreak, qpLastLoginDate — mirror A4+A5 for the code-session path). UI preferences (archCollapsed, squadCollapsed, assetsCollapsed, assetsAnimalEditorCollapsed — panel open/close states that users expect to persist across devices). Squad-manager state (_shoppingList from v1.6941, _sqmgrPins from v1.6936 — both changelogs claimed these rode in the settings blob, but they're actually top-level S fields; the claim referred to intent, not implementation). Downtime roll machinery (pendingDowntimeRolls + downtimeRollHistory from v1.6904 — the ribbon-prompted queue and the 200-entry log that feeds the Downtime Ledger dashboard tile; without persistence, a mid-roll crash or device switch silently discards completed downtimes before they're resolved). Undo log (v1.6957 undoLog, 20-entry ring buffer — the changelog explicitly claimed it "rides inside the existing settings blob" but it's a top-level S field not in _keys, so code-session users lost their undo history on every pull). Daily-reset trackers (lastFavCheck, lastFavCheckTs, lastRerollReset — date-string and timestamp markers used by the favorite-recompute and store-reroll schedulers; without persistence, a mid-day cross-device pull would reset the day-marker and cause double-applies of already-done daily operations). Deliberately NOT added (session-local by design): stock/stockDate (rotating daily store stock regenerates from seed each day), doneEnc/encAssigned/pendingQuests (transient or dead UI state), partyTagFilter (UI filter, resets each session by design). Migration behavior. All Category A fixes are backwards-compatible with rows saved under prior versions — every read path falls back to '' / 0 / [] / the in-memory localStorage value when the blob key is absent. No one loses state on upgrade. First save after deploy writes the new fields into place; subsequent pulls restore them. Category B additions follow the same pattern: code-session _keys forEach only writes _blob[k]=S[k] when S[k]!==undefined, so missing fields on older blobs stay missing in S (which then gets filled by createDefaultState defaults) rather than clobbering downstream reads. No SQL change. All five Category A fixes use piggyback patterns on existing columns (squads.name's __sqmeta__ got rolesByName; party.stats got _homeTerritory/_statsClass; franchise.settings got _qpPending/_qpLedger/_qpLoginStreak/_qpLastLoginDate). Code-session uses a single jsonb column so no schema consideration there. If we later want dedicated columns for any of these fields (e.g. home_territory text on party for query performance), the read paths can prefer the dedicated column over the pack with a trivial additive migration. Seventh occurrence of the round-trip-strip pattern. Catalogue: (1) v1.543 pct, (2) v1.6995 bagOfHolding/effect, (3) v1.6930 pinned/split_group_meta, (4) v1.7001 _col, (5) v1.7033 done/waiting/waitingNote, (6) v1.7039 homeTerritory/originTerritory/unlockedTerritories, (7) v1.7040 roles/homeTerritory (char) /statsClass/qpPending/qpLedger/qpLoginStreak/qpLastLoginDate. The pattern: additive field added to the in-memory data model, tested locally via saveLocalState (which JSON-stringifies the whole S), changelog says "no data-model change" — but the Supabase push/pull paths use hand-maintained column lists that weren't updated. The local test works; the cross-device sync silently regresses; the bug surfaces weeks or months later as mysterious data loss. Mitigation going forward. Every future addition of a top-level field on S, sq, c, or meta should include a three-surface check: (a) is it in an appropriate sbPush column or settings-blob key?, (b) is it extracted by sbPull?, (c) is it in the code-session _keys array? A unit-test-style check at save time (e.g. round-trip a sample S through serialize→deserialize and assert equality on known-persistent fields) would catch these at authoring time rather than user-report time. Not shipped in this version — flagged for a future dev tool. Scope boundaries. No changes to UI, gameplay rules, migration logic, or any feature behavior. Every edit in this version is in a sbPush or sbPull code path — pure plumbing, no semantic changes. All Category A and Category B changes are independently guarded (missing values fall back to defaults), so the partial-apply case (save in progress, crash mid-push) is no worse than current behavior. No SQL change. No data-model change beyond the documented additive reserved keys inside existing jsonb columns.
Version 1.7039 — Territory persistence fix (round-trip strip, sixth occurrence). User report: after unclocking all squads and quest cycles to re-sort squads, then re-activating Solarians to the active cycle and assigning characters, all four squads landed in a collapsed "Unassigned Territory 0 / 4 squads · 0 unassigned" zone and none were offered in the grind clock-in picker. Screenshot confirmed Solarians, Stellani, Terrans, and Bloodhawks all stacked in the empty-territory bucket while the Headquarters (8/10) virtual unassigned panel correctly reported its Minsaka characters — so characters and squads had gotten out of sync on the territory field. Root cause. sq.homeTerritory (added by v1.7006) was never persisted to Supabase. The squads table sbPush (line ~27067) wrote {user_id, name, squad_name, emoji, color, member_names, member_ids, collapsed} and the sbPull rebuild (line ~27674) constructed {_sbId, name, emoji, color, memberIdxs, collapsed} — homeTerritory appeared in neither. After a push+pull cycle, every squad returned with homeTerritory undefined; migrateToTerritoryV1_7006 (called later in the sbPull .then chain) tried to backfill via the !sq.homeTerritory guard, but on a fresh S=createDefaultState() with originTerritory:'', its fallback was sq.homeTerritory=S.originTerritory||'' — itself empty-string when computeOriginTerritory() fired before QUESTSETS/mergeCustomContent had stabilized the territory stamps. Net result: squads reliably landed with homeTerritory='' on every pull, and the Party screen's bucket key sq.homeTerritory||S.originTerritory||'' (line ~17289) resolved to the empty bucket. The grind clock-in helper availableSquadsForGrindClockIn(territory) (v1.7013, line ~8188) then filtered to squads whose homeTerritory matched the target territory — with empty-string on every squad, nothing matched Minsaka, and the picker was empty. Also broken. S.originTerritory and S.unlockedTerritories (added by v1.7006) were also never persisted — they rode on the franchise row's settings jsonb column but no _originTerritory / _unlockedTerritories keys were being written or read. The migration's re-seed from QUESTSETS[0] covered originTerritory for the default (Minsaka) case, but any later territories the player unlocked by buying a 1000+ gp building in a non-origin cycle would silently vanish on the next pull, reverting the franchise to origin-only. This violates the v1.7006 monotonic-unlock invariant. What landed. (1) squads.name column now carries a __sqmeta__ pack. Extends the v1.495 pattern that originally packed emoji and color into the name column before they got dedicated columns. sbPush now writes name:sq.name+'__sqmeta__'+JSON.stringify({homeTerritory:...}) when homeTerritory is set; squad_name stays clean as the authoritative display-name source. The only live consumer of the name column is the sbPull legacy branch (line ~27640), which already strips __sqmeta__ before __members__ — so back-compat is by construction. No ALTER TABLE required; fifth time this "piggyback on an existing channel" pattern has shipped (v1.495 emoji/color in name, v1.6995 _boh/_fx in type, v1.7001 _col in __meta, v1.7033 _done/_waiting/_waitingNote in __meta, now homeTerritory in __sqmeta__). (2) Both sbPull branches (new-schema and legacy) parse __sqmeta__ for homeTerritory. The new-schema branch extracts homeTerritory from the name column's pack before using squad_name as the clean sqName — the extraction is shared across branches via a pre-check that runs before the if(sq.squad_name) split. Stamped onto the rebuilt squad object alongside _sbId, name, emoji, color, memberIdxs, collapsed. Empty string when the pack is absent; migrateToTerritoryV1_7006 later in the .then chain still backfills from S.originTerritory for any squad that arrived without a pack (pre-v1.7039 rows, or fresh franchises). (3) franchise.settings blob now carries _originTerritory and _unlockedTerritories. Matches the existing _qpPending / _qpLedger pattern for franchise-level state that hasn't been promoted to a dedicated column. sbPush writes them into cleanSettings before the upsert; sbPull reads them back and strips them (they're removed from the cleanSettings that ends up on S.settings so there's no source-of-truth ambiguity between S.originTerritory and S.settings._originTerritory). Fallback behavior on missing values: sbPull only overwrites S.originTerritory / S.unlockedTerritories when the blob value is non-empty, so a pull from a pre-v1.7039 row doesn't wipe values the local in-memory state had (which typically came from localStorage or the migration). (4) Code-session _keys updated. The _keys array at sbPush (line ~26851, used by code-session accounts that save the whole S object as a single blob to login_codes.player_data) added originTerritory, unlockedTerritories, and kudos. kudos was another silent omission (added by v1.6996) — code-session users would've lost their kudos log on cross-device pulls. Several other fields remain absent from _keys (archCollapsed, squadCollapsed, qpPending, qpLedger, qpLoginStreak, qpLastLoginDate, lastFavCheck, plus the intentionally-session-local stock/stockDate/doneEnc/encAssigned/pendingQuests); these are flagged in an inline comment for the pending grep-audit that v1.7001 and v1.7033 already called for. Not addressed in this version to keep the fix narrowly scoped. Deployment note: first-pull behavior on existing saves. On first load after deploy, migrateToTerritoryV1_7006 runs as usual and backfills homeTerritory on every squad via the fallback path (foundTerr||S.originTerritory||''). On the next save (debounced push), the new pack writes homeTerritory into squads.name.__sqmeta__ and _originTerritory/_unlockedTerritories into franchise.settings. From that save forward the state is authoritative; subsequent pulls restore the field from the blob rather than re-running the QUESTSETS-seeded fallback. Workaround for users mid-session before the save completes: a console one-liner S.squads.forEach(sq=>sq.homeTerritory=sq.homeTerritory||'Minsaka');save();renderParty(); forces an immediate backfill + push without waiting for the auto-save debounce. Per-user territory string varies if they've renamed the origin; default Minsaka per v1.7007's hardcoded stamps on the four built-in cycles. Sixth occurrence of the "JSON-rides-but-wasn't-plumbed" pattern. Prior five instances catalogued in v1.7033's audit note (pct v1.543, bagOfHolding/effect v1.6995, pinned/split_group_meta v1.6930, _col v1.7001, done/waiting/waitingNote v1.7033). Adding a systematic grep-audit to v1.7040's backlog — each additive field added to S/sq/c/item since v1.6900 should be cross-checked against the sbPush column lists and the code-session _keys array. If anything else is missing, it's a latent time-bomb. Scope boundaries. (i) No SQL change. Piggyback pattern avoids ALTER TABLE on both squads (homeTerritory rides in name.__sqmeta__) and franchise (origin/unlocked ride in settings.jsonb). If later we want a dedicated home_territory column for query performance or reporting, it's a trivial additive migration — the read path would just prefer the column over the pack. (ii) c.homeTerritory persistence was already correct. Character rows use dedicated columns, and c.homeTerritory was plumbed in the v1.7006 party sbPush/sbPull via the broader field-preservation... (audit needed to verify; noted as a follow-up). The symptom Justin reported focused on squads so they're fixed first; characters appear to be working per the rendered "Headquarters (8/10)" bucket showing correct Minsaka membership. (iii) Existing __sqmeta__ legacy pack behavior unchanged. The legacy branch still parses emoji/color from __sqmeta__ for pre-v1.495 rows; the v1.7039 addition of homeTerritory to the pack is additive — old rows still work, new rows carry the new field. (iv) Character homeTerritory backfill on load unchanged. Line 16719's per-render loop in renderParty (v1.7006) still catches characters created by any of the six S.party.push(…) sites without homeTerritory; this patch doesn't disturb that path. Unchanged behaviors. Grind clock-in rules (one-territory-per-squad, v1.7013), territory unlock via 1000+ gp building purchase (v1.7006), per-territory unassigned bucket cap (v1.7006), squad creation territory picker (v1.7006). Those all continue to work; they just now have a reliable persistent homeTerritory to read. No SQL change. No data-model change beyond the additive _originTerritory/_unlockedTerritories keys in the franchise settings blob and the additive homeTerritory field inside squads.name's __sqmeta__ pack.
Version 1.7038 — Encumbrance-on-purchase: three-way choice modal + refund-on-cancel. User report: when a store purchase would encumber the assigned character, the Franchise Storage flow already offered a drop-an-existing-item picker (encumbranceRelinquishPrompt / ov-enc-drop, v1.575/576), but the store-buy and quest-prereq-slot-buy flows did not — they showed only a simple yes/no "Send to Franchise Storage instead?" confirm. Investigation finding: the store-buy and quest-prereq-slot-buy flows are actually the same code path — both open ov-buyitem and commit through the ov-bi-yes handler, which at line ~24772 ran a confirm() for the encumbrance branch. The quest-prereq-slot entry point (qcs-store-link) sets window._questModalOpenAfterBuy=true so the quest modal re-opens after a successful buy, but that hook was bypassed by an early return; in the encumbrance branch. Also noticed: clicking "No" (cancel) on the yes/no confirm already kept the user's gold while delivering nothing — a pre-existing latent gold-leak bug since gold is spent at line 24752 (goldSpend) before reaching the encumbrance check. What landed: (1) New modal ov-enc-choice. Three-button layout (Drop & Deliver / Send to Franchise Storage / Cancel & Refund) with an inline dropdown of relinquishable items (weight ≥ incoming, sourced from destC.inv with the same __meta__-exclusion filter as encumbranceRelinquishPrompt). Distinct from the existing ov-enc-drop because Cancel here has to refund gold — the storage→char transfer flow that uses ov-enc-drop is free, so its Cancel can just close. (2) New helper encumbranceChoicePrompt(destC, destCi, incomingItem, onDrop, onStorage, onCancel). Three callbacks match the three outcomes. If the character has no items ≥ incoming weight, the Drop button renders grayed out (opacity .45, disabled=true, explanatory title) and the dropdown shows a "(no items of equal or greater weight)" placeholder with a secondary italic note row; the user can still choose Send to Storage or Cancel. Clone-and-replace pattern on all three buttons to clear any listeners from a prior invocation, mirroring encumbranceRelinquishPrompt's approach. Fallback path if the modal DOM is missing: degrade to a confirm() with Storage vs. Cancel (no drop path in the fallback — there's no simple prompt equivalent for a weight-filtered dropdown). (3) ov-bi-yes handler rewired. The encumbrance branch now calls encumbranceChoicePrompt and returns; the three outcomes run through three new local helpers defined just above the check: _finalizeToChar (drop → put incoming in char.inv + full _reopen auto-assign for the quest-prereq flow), _finalizeToStorage (storage → push to franchiseStorage, clear _questModalOpenAfterBuy since the char isn't carrying it so the prereq slot stays unmet), _cancelAndRefund (refund via goldAdd(total, 'Refund: ... (encumbrance-cancel)', 'buy'), clear _questModalOpenAfterBuy, close buy modal). The refund log entry is symmetric with the original goldSpend charge so the ledger shows both the debit and the credit. (4) Quest prereq behavior pinned per user spec. When the chosen outcome is Send-to-Storage and the flow was initiated from a quest-prereq-slot Purchase button, the quest modal does NOT re-open / auto-assign — the purchased prereq item is in franchise storage, not in the slotted character's inventory, so the slot remains unmet (matches questPrereqCheck's rule that item prereqs count only items carried by non-busy peg-slot chars, v1.616/v1.620). If the user wants to satisfy the slot, they can Give the item from storage to the character via the existing Franchise Storage row's drop-picker flow. Scope boundaries: (i) from-storage (franchise storage → char Give) path unchanged. Still uses encumbranceRelinquishPrompt / ov-enc-drop; no behavior change. The two flows live on different modals so the user can have one open and not risk button/listener state clash with the other. (ii) Quantity handling. The encumbrance check is wouldEncumber(c, item.name) which considers one unit of weight — if the user buys qty=3 and drops one existing ≥-weight item to make room, the resulting state may still leave the character encumbered (they receive 3 copies, dropped only 1). This matches how wouldEncumber and the encumbrance system generally work (encumbrance is not blocked — only warned-on-would-trigger — and a character CAN be encumbered). No qty-per-drop logic added; keeps the fix narrow. If needed, a v1.7039 could iterate: require dropping enough items to cover qty × incomingWt, or force qty=1 for encumbering buys. (iii) Non-character destinations unchanged. Buying to storage, buildings, vehicles, or squads bypasses the encumbrance check as before — no character carries those items. (iv) Fallback path behavior. If the new modal DOM somehow isn't found (should never happen on a correctly-loaded page), the fallback confirm() offers Storage vs. Cancel-with-refund. Losing the Drop option in fallback is a conservative degradation; the two destination paths still work. No SQL change. No data-model change. Pure UI + handler-logic fix. One new modal (ov-enc-choice, ~20 lines HTML), one new helper function (encumbranceChoicePrompt, ~75 lines), three new local finalization closures in the ov-bi-yes handler (~55 lines), replacing the previous ~8-line encumbrance branch. Net add ~140 lines.
Version 1.7037 — Contracts screen layout reshuffle. User ask: move the Task Search box to the top of the page, and move the "new contract" composer (name field + gp value + + Add button) between the Undo/Archive/Kudos action row and the Pinned Tasks zone at the top of #tlist. New top-to-bottom order on the Contracts screen: (1) title "✦ Active Contracts", (2) 🔍 search bar, (3) Undo / Archive / Kudos action row (+ desktop column-count toggle + Rebalance), (4) "New contract…" composer row, (5) gp-per-tick hint + ℹ help icon, (6) daily task-budget readout, (7) #tlist containing Pinned Tasks zone and main task list. Rationale: search-first positioning lets the user locate any existing contract before deciding whether to add a new one — a common pattern when re-engaging with a long backlog. The new-contract composer sits directly above the task list (Pinned zone is the first visible item inside #tlist), which creates a natural add → see-appear-below flow. Previously the composer was at the top of the screen and the budget readout separated it from the pin zone, so a newly added contract was visually far from its appearance point. Purely HTML reordering — no JS changes. Every element keeps its existing id, so every event listener (#btn-addtask, #new-tname, #new-tgp, #contract-search-input, #btn-undo, #btn-nav-archive, #btn-kudos, #cols-toggle, #btn-cols-rebalance, #btn-contracts-help, #task-budget-readout) continues to bind and fire unchanged. bindContractSearch() still finds #contract-search-wrap. renderTasks() still writes into #tlist. The hidden legacy stubs (#tsort, #btn-newgrp, #btn-nav-projects) are preserved in their guard positions just before #tlist. No data-model change. No SQL change. CSS classes (.addrow, .stitle, .hint) unchanged, so existing per-element styling (border colors, spacing, typography) reads identically in the new position.
Version 1.7036 — Pinned-row drawer spacing fixes. User report: after v1.7034/7035 added the ⏳ waiting button to the pinned-row left drawer (making it 3 buttons: 📍 + ✕ + ⏳), swiping a pinned row right on mobile showed the drawer plus partial ghosting of the right-drawer buttons on the same row. The reveal ate most of the visible row width on narrow pinned sub-columns. Three coordinated fixes: (1) Tighter pinned drawer buttons. New rule .strow.strow-pin .strow-drawer-btn{min-width:38px;padding:0 5px} scopes pinned-row drawer buttons to 38px instead of the main-list 44px. Scoped specifically to .strow-pin so main-list sub-rows keep comfort-size hit boxes; pinned rows are inherently more compact (narrower sub-columns, smaller content) so 38px still comfortably fits a thumb but gives 6px per button × 3 = 18px savings across the drawer. (2) Reduced pinned reveal distance. .strow.strow-pin.strow-show-del .strow-main translate reduced from 170px → 140px. Budget: 3 × 38 + 2 × 8 padding + small gaps ≈ 130px; 140 gives buffer. Main-list rows keep the 120px reveal from v1.7034 (they have the full 44px buttons). (3) Right drawer opacity gating. Added .strow.strow-pin .strow-actions{opacity:0;pointer-events:none} at rest and .strow.strow-pin.strow-show-actions .strow-actions{opacity:1;pointer-events:auto} on swipe-left. This is a scoped revival of the v1.6965 drawer-hiding treatment (which was rolled back in v1.6966 along with a broader translucent-row experiment); here it only affects pinned rows and only hides the right drawer, so the artifacts the v1.6966 rollback was addressing don't recur. Effect: when the user swipes a pinned row right (to reveal the left drawer's 📍/✕/⏳ buttons), the right drawer's +gp label and ⇈/↑/↓ reorder buttons stay fully hidden instead of potentially bleeding through at the row's right edge. When the user swipes left (to reveal the right drawer), the right drawer fades in with a 180ms ease matching the row's translate animation. The pointer-events:none on the hidden state means accidental taps near the row's right edge don't trigger the reorder buttons — only when the drawer is actually revealed. No changes to main-list rows, which keep their always-visible drawers (both at rest and during swipe) since they've always had opaque parchment and no bleed-through issues. No SQL change. No data-model change. CSS-only fix, three rules changed.
Version 1.7035 — Two fixes on the v1.7034 waiting-state UX. (1) Drawer bleed-through on waiting rows. User report: after marking a sub-task as waiting on mobile, both the left drawer (✕ / ⏳) AND the right drawer (⇈ / ↑ / ↓ / ⇄ / 📌) were visible underneath the row content at rest — not just when swiped. Looked like the row was stuck in "both drawers revealed" mode, cluttering the layout. Root cause: v1.7032's waiting tint was written as .strow.strow-waiting .strow-main{background:rgba(38,122,130,.07)} — but .strow-main needs to be OPAQUE to hide the absolutely-positioned drawers sitting behind it (that's how the v1.6924 swipe system works: the drawers are permanently behind the main row, revealed by transforming the main row left/right on swipe). Setting .strow-main's background to a 7%-alpha teal replaced the opaque parchment with a near-transparent tint, so the drawers bled through. Fix: apply the tint to the outer .strow wrapper (harmless — it's behind everything) AND set .strow-main to a pre-mixed opaque color #e6e0bc — that's the RGB average of parchment #f4e8c1 and 7% teal, so it reads as "parchment with teal tint" while staying fully opaque. Drawers stay hidden at rest; waiting rows still read as tinted. (2) No mobile path to clear waiting flag. User observation: the v1.7034 drawer ⏳ opens the note editor (for setting/editing), but the only way to CLEAR the waiting flag was tapping the inline ⏳ on the row — which is a small target and not obvious. Right-click works on desktop; mobile had no equivalent. Fix: the note editor modal (ov-waiting-note) gains a "✓ Done waiting" button that only appears when the target item is currently waiting. Tapping it flips item.waiting to false, preserves the note (so re-flagging later doesn't lose context), and closes the modal. The existing "Clear note" button also now hides when there's no note to clear, reducing visual noise. A new _syncWaitingNoteButtons() helper runs on every modal open (drawer path, badge path, right-click auto-open path — all three call sites updated) to reflect the current target's state onto the button visibility. Interaction model is now: to mark waiting — tap ⏳ in drawer (sub-task) or action row (task), fill in note if desired, Save. to edit note — same path; the editor opens with existing note pre-filled. to clear waiting — tap ⏳ and then "✓ Done waiting" (mobile) or tap the inline ⏳ on the row (quick shortcut) or right-click the checkbox (desktop). to just edit the note without changing flag — Save always keeps waiting=true. Symmetric and discoverable. No SQL change. No data-model change.
Version 1.7034 — Mobile waiting-state UX: swap the long-press for explicit buttons. User ask: long-press on the checkbox was a hidden feature — easy to miss and occasionally triggered accidentally. Put an hourglass ⏳ button directly in the swipe-reveal drawer (right of the ✕ on the left drawer, so drawer order reads ✕ → ⏳), and replace the task action row's 💰 claim-gold button with an ⏳ button that opens the note editor. Remove the long-press behavior entirely; keep right-click on desktop as a power-user shortcut. What landed: (1) Sub-task drawer button. A new ⏳ drawer button (data-a="stwopen") renders in .strow-del immediately after the ✕ delete button on both main-list sub-rows and pinned sub-rows. Tapping it opens the existing ov-waiting-note modal with the current note pre-filled. Saving from that modal flips s.waiting to true (if not already) and stores the note. When the item is already waiting, the button gets an .is-waiting class that tints it filled-teal so the same button reads as "edit note" instead of "mark waiting". (2) Drawer reveal widened. Main-list sub-row drawer translation bumped from translateX(70px) to 120px to fit the second button. Pinned sub-row drawer translation bumped from 120px to 170px for its now-three buttons (📍 unpin + ✕ delete + ⏳ wait). Budget math: 3 × 44px min-width + 2 × 8px padding + gaps ≈ 150px; 170px gives buffer. (3) Task action row: 💰 → ⏳. The 💰 Claim Gold button (data-a="tick") on the per-task action row was removed in favor of a ⏳ waiting button (data-a="ttwopen"). Tapping it opens the note editor directly — same behavior as the sub-task drawer button. The button is amber-on-parchment when not waiting and teal-filled when waiting, so the color state communicates the current flag at a glance. (4) Task-level checkbox now claims gold. Since 💰 was the only surface for claiming gold on tasks without sub-tasks, that mechanic is migrated to the v1.7032 task-level checkbox. The ttck change handler now mirrors the old tick flow: if the checkbox goes from unchecked → checked AND the daily task-gold budget has room, it increments t.count and calls claimTaskGold(t.gp, ...). Budget-exhausted case: the checkbox still flips to done but no gold is awarded (matching how the old disabled 💰 button behaved). The ttck undo entry now also captures prevCount, gpEarned, and prevBudget so reverting a check-on rolls back BOTH the done state AND the gold claim atomically. (5) Long-press removed. The v1.7032 touch-hold IIFE on #tlist is deleted; the contextmenu handler for right-click on a checkbox remains as a desktop power-user shortcut (instant flag toggle without opening the editor). Mobile users now have only explicit tap targets — no more accidental waiting flags from pressing too long on a checkbox. (6) Note editor: Save flips the flag. Saving from ov-waiting-note sets item.waiting = true unconditionally. This was already the behavior (dating to v1.7032's defensive-mode code), but is now the primary entry path for marking-as-waiting. Drawer tap → editor opens → user types note → Save → waiting:true + waitingNote set. If the user cancels, nothing changes. If the user wants to clear the flag from a waiting row, they tap the inline ⏳ button on the row itself (already present from v1.7032), not the drawer button. (7) Fixed pre-existing undo bug. v1.7032's stwnote / ttwnote undo entries captured prevWaiting AFTER the Save handler set item.waiting = true, so the "revert" target was always the post-state, meaning revert never actually flipped the flag back. Corrected to snapshot the PRE-save state; undo of a "mark as waiting" action now correctly restores the un-waiting state, and undo of a "edit note on already-waiting" keeps the flag set (matching the previous reality). Help modal updated. The "Waiting (⏳)" paragraph describes the new drawer+button flow and mentions right-click as the desktop shortcut. No SQL change. No data-model change. All persistence (v1.7033) continues to apply unchanged.
Version 1.7033 — Supabase persistence for v1.7032's waiting state. The miss. v1.7032 added five new task/sub-task fields (t.done, t.waiting, t.waitingNote, s.waiting, s.waitingNote) and claimed "no SQL change — rides inside existing active_tasks JSON blob." That's partially true (no SQL change IS correct) but the claim glossed over the same round-trip-strip trap as v1.6930 (_splitGroup), v1.6995 (bag-of-holding flags), and v1.7001 (_col): sbPush pushes specific named fields per row, and any field not listed gets silently dropped on the next pull. v1.7032 saved the flags to localStorage (full-S blob) but a cross-device pull would reset every waiting flag to false and every waitingNote to empty. The flags would appear to work within a single session (localStorage reads are fast) and then vanish on any device switch or post-push pull. What landed. (1) Task-level fields packed into __meta. The existing __meta sentinel subtask (which already carries _tid, note, desc, _col) now also carries _done, _waiting, and _waitingNote. One-line addition to the push packer in sbPush, one-line addition to the pull rebuilder in sbPull. Back-compat: rows saved pre-v1.7033 have no meta._done etc. — fallbacks return false/'', same as the bug. First save+push from v1.7033 forward upgrades each task. (2) Sub-task fields travel naturally. s.waiting and s.waitingNote live directly on sub-task objects inside the subtasks JSONB array. The existing push identity-map (t.subtasks||[]).map(function(st){return st;}) preserves all sub-task properties as-is; the existing pull filter pushes non-__meta entries into cleanSubtasks unchanged.
