So waiting flags on sub-tasks ride through without any plumbing work. Verified by tracing PostgREST JSONB serialization: arbitrary object properties on array elements round-trip intact. (3) Code-session accounts unaffected. The login_codes.player_data blob path saves S.active whole via the _keys array at sbPush; waiting flags ride inside the blob naturally. No code-session changes needed (same as v1.7001 — only the regular active_tasks table path needed the repair). (4) No SQL change. No new columns, no migration. The subtasks JSONB column is the transport for all five new fields. Deployment order. Just deploy — there's no SQL prereq. Users on v1.7032 who flagged items as waiting will see their flags reset once on the first post-deploy pull (because the v1.7032 pushes wrote rows without the new meta keys), but from that point forward the state persists. If anyone has accumulated important waiting-state context, flag this as a known one-time reset — but in practice the user count affected is one (the developer) and the field is young enough that a reset is acceptable. Audit note. Fifth occurrence of the "rides-inside-JSON-but-wasn't-plumbed" pattern (pct v1.543, bagOfHolding/effect v1.6995, pinned/split_group_meta v1.6930, _col v1.7001, now done/waiting/waitingNote v1.7033). Worth scheduling the grep-audit the v1.7001 changelog called for. No SQL change. No data-model change.
Version 1.7032 — Waiting state for tasks and sub-tasks + task-level checkboxes. User ask: mark an item as "I'm waiting on someone else to act before I can do anything here" via long-press or right-click, swapping the checkbox for an hourglass and showing an optional free-text "waiting on whom?" note. Also, tasks without sub-tasks should have a checkbox (they only had a progress pie before). (1) Task-level checkboxes. Tasks without sub-tasks now render a crimson-accent checkbox alongside the existing progress pie inside the task name row. The pie is still there for partial progress (0–100% in 16.6% increments); the checkbox is a clean binary "done" signal. Checking the box sets t.done=true via a new data-a="ttck" handler. Does NOT auto-open the Archive/Delete/Recur modal — the user archives manually via the ✕ button when ready, per spec. Tasks WITH sub-tasks continue to route completion through the all-subtasks-done trigger (v1.7019) and don't get a separate task-level checkbox (would duplicate signal). (2) Waiting flag. New optional item.waiting:boolean on both tasks and sub-tasks. When true, the checkbox glyph is swapped for a ⏳ hourglass button (styled teal), the row picks up a teal tint (.strow-waiting / .card-waiting), and a waiting-note badge appears next to the item name. The flag is orthogonal to done and pct — an item can be "waiting on someone" AND have partial progress on your own end. Checking an item off auto-clears waiting (you're done; you're no longer waiting) — the auto-clear is captured in undo so revert restores the prior waiting state cleanly. The note (waitingNote:string) persists across waiting-flag toggles, so clearing and re-flagging preserves "Waiting on Sarah's approval" without re-typing. (3) Interaction model. Long-press (500ms hold, 8px move-slop cancel) on a checkbox toggles waiting. Right-click (desktop contextmenu) does the same instantly, no hold needed. The existing split-group long-press IIFE already ignored input and button targets so the two IIFEs don't race. Haptic nudge (navigator.vibrate(12)) fires on supported devices when the hold fires, matching the v1.6928 split-group long-press pattern. Click suppression via capture-phase listener prevents the checkbox from also toggling done on the release tap after a successful long-press. (4) Note editor. New ov-waiting-note modal — tiny single-purpose: a maxlength=120 text input with Save / Clear note / Cancel. Tap the note badge on any waiting item to open it; tap ⏳ to just clear the flag. When a new waiting flag is first set and no note yet exists, the editor auto-opens so the user can type "waiting on whom?" without a second tap — they can still cancel/leave empty and the flag stays set. Enter submits, Escape cancels. (5) Render changes. Two new helpers (renderCheckboxOrWaiting, renderWaitingBadge) emit the ⏳-vs-checkbox swap and the note badge. Called from three sites: main-list sub-row render in taskCardHtml, renderPinnedSubRow (so pinned waiting items also show ⏳ and tint), and the task card header (for tasks without sub-tasks). (6) Five new undo kinds. ttck (task-level checkbox toggle, includes prevWaiting so check-auto-clear is reversible), stwait + ttwait (waiting-flag toggles, include prevNote for consistency), stwnote + ttwnote (note-string edits). stck extended to capture prevWaiting so the existing check/uncheck undo also restores waiting state cleanly. All five reverse operations use _tid/_sid lookup so they survive structural rearrangement of S.active. (7) Scope boundaries. The filter-out-waiting-items option (discussed but scoped out) is not included — visual tint + ⏳ is the whole signal. Waiting is not flagged as a completion state: it doesn't write kudos, doesn't affect gold claims, doesn't interact with recurrence. It's purely a note-to-self. Pinned Tasks zone, Archive, Kudos log, and the done modal all treat a waiting item as a normal incomplete item. (8) Help modal updated. Contract-help modal gains a "Waiting (⏳)" paragraph describing the long-press/right-click interaction. Action-row blurb updated to reflect the v1.7031 ✕ archive icon swap. No SQL change. No data-model change requiring migration. t.done, t.waiting, t.waitingNote, s.waiting, s.waitingNote are all additive optional fields that ride inside the existing active_tasks JSON blob. Pre-existing saves continue to work (all five fields default to false / '' / undefined, rendering as normal non-waiting state). Correction (v1.7033): this version's persistence claim was incomplete — the fields save to localStorage but the sbPush / sbPull path in active_tasks was not updated, so cross-device sync would strip t.done / t.waiting / t.waitingNote on every pull. See v1.7033 for the fix.
Version 1.7031 — Contracts-screen polish ahead of a stable cut. (1) Split-task modal: unified row coloring. Previously the sub-tasks below the scissors cutline rendered with a gold-tinted background (rgba(201,146,42,.08)) and a warmer border, intended as a "destination preview" for the Move bucket. The tint muddied the read on mobile — users were parsing it as "highlighted = completed" or "highlighted = error". The scissors handle + dashed crimson cutline already communicate the split position unambiguously, so the tint was redundant signal. Both .split-stay-row and .split-move-row now render with the same parchment background and parchment-deep border. The .split-move-row class is retained so the template can keep its Stay/Move distinction for any future rule (e.g. drag-to-toggle), but it's visually identical to Stay now. (2) Split-task modal: "new task name" field relocated. The name input used to sit at the top of the modal under the header, forcing the user to name the new task before they'd even chosen where to split. Now it sits at the bottom, between the sub-task list and the Cancel/Split action row. Narrative order is: preview the split → name it → confirm. The top section keeps the header + instructional hint ("Drag the ✂ cutline up or down…"); the name field sits inside its own bordered band immediately above the footer buttons. (3) Contract Archive button: icon. The 🗂 folder glyph on the task-action-row Archive button (data-a="done") swapped to a crimson ✕ so the button reads as "dismiss this contract" at a glance. The underlying .archive-a styling (muted amber fill, parchment-deep border) is unchanged, so the button still reads as a distinct action from the destructive sub-task ✕ delete. Tooltip and aria-label updated to "Archive, delete, or recur contract" since the button opens a three-way chooser, not a direct archive. Sub-task ✕ buttons (data-a="stdl") remain immediate-delete — scope of the icon swap is task-level only. (4) Done modal: pills replace the Action dropdown. The ov-done modal's Action chooser was a <select> with three options (Archive / Recur / Delete). Replaced with three tappable pills of the same three choices. Each pill carries a data-done-pill value; tapping one updates a hidden #ov-done-action input (preserving the existing confirm handler's actionSel.value read path) and flips the active-pill class. Pills are color-coded by action family: Archive pill tinted amber when active, Delete pill tinted crimson, Recur pill tinted teal. Inactive pills render as muted parchment chips. Active state uses bold weight so the choice reads clearly on a fast glance. The recur sub-panel (Schedule / N / Weekday) still opens when the Recur pill is active, via the same _syncDoneRecurUI machinery — just fired from the pill click now instead of a select change event. Both auto-open and manual-open paths of the modal continue to reset to the Archive pill on open since they were already setting doneSel.value='archive' then calling _syncDoneRecurUI(), and the sync function now also reflects that value onto the pills. No SQL change. No data-model change. All four changes are presentation-layer only — the hidden ov-done-action input with the exact same values archive|delete|recur keeps the downstream archive/delete/recur branch in ov-done-yes byte-identical to v1.7030.
Version 1.7030 — Content Editor UI removed. Its authoring function has been shunted to the standalone quest-editor file, so the in-app modal is now dead weight. What was removed. The #ov-editor modal HTML (header toolbar with ☁ Push / ⬇ Pull / 📤📥 CSV / ✕ Close, the two-pane #ed-list-panel + #ed-form-panel body, the six tab buttons for Quests/Grind/Gear/Assets/Cards/Flow, and the mobile ‹ Back to list button) is gone. The Settings screen's "✏️ Content Editor" section (#btn-open-editor) and the Dev Bar's ✏️ Editor button (#dev-bar-editor) are gone. All editor-only CSS (#ov-editor, #ed-list-panel, #ed-form-panel, #ov-editor-header-tabs, #ov-editor-header-toolbar, .ed-tab, #ed-mobile-back, and the 900px/600px responsive overrides including the .ed-form-active slide transitions) is gone. All editor JS is gone: openEditor, closeEditor, edRenderTabs, edRenderList, edShowPlaceholder, edShowCycleForm, edShowQuestForm, edShowGrindForm, edShowGearForm, edShowAssetForm, edShowDupeCycleForm, edQuestObj, edScopeRow, edScopeFlash, edFormScope, renderDashboardEditor, renderCycleFlowChart. The CSV import/export helpers (csvEsc, csvList, parseCsvText, boolFromCsv, numFromCsv, csvToList, exportEditorCsv, importEditorCsv, QUEST_CSV_COLUMNS) are gone — the quest-editor file owns CSV now. The macro panel (getMacros, saveMacros, insertAtCursor, edTrackFocus, renderMacroPanel, wireMacroPanel, edLastFocused, edMacroManageOpen, MACRO_CATS) was only used by edShowQuestForm/edShowGrindForm, so it went too. The dead editorScopeNote stub was deleted. Four button wirings were deleted: btn-open-editor, btn-close-editor, dev-bar-editor, and the full editor toolbar block (push/pull/csv/tab/dupe/new/scope/back-button listeners). The dev-mode visibility toggle setSectionVisibility('btn-open-editor', true) was removed. What stayed — shared machinery audit. loadContentLibrary and mergeCustomContent are called from 10+ non-editor sites (boot path at ~26703, sbPull at ~27157, main load at ~29675, initSession at ~29697, persistContentScope at ~30358) — both retained. saveCustom retained (used by the surviving _autoCreateDashCard). All scope helpers retained: normalizeContentScope, currentContentScope, contentBucket, clone, editableContentItem (still called inside loadContentLibrary at line ~30718), isAppAdmin, isTestAccount, contentItemsForScope, getCustomQuestSets/Quests/Grind/Gear/Vehicles/Buildings/Pets, contentRow, persistContentScope, migrateLegacyContentIntoPrivateScope, loadPetsFromDB, loadItemsFromDB, isAssetItemName, isPetItemName, applyDBItemsToArrays. refreshEditorScopeOptions kept as a no-op stub since it's still called once from the startup hygiene pass at ~34828 (cheaper than hunting the caller). currentContentScope still has the sel?sel.value:'private' fallback, so with #ed-content-scope gone it simply always returns 'private' — matches the exact default the removed hidden input had, so behavior is identical. The whole qdd*/qcs*/qcar* block (quest-assignment modal machinery — qddRender, qddBindEvents, qddAutoAssign, qcsApply, qcarBuildEntries, etc., owned by ov-quest, confirmed via v1.6913/v1.6923/v1.6974 changelogs) is untouched. _autoCreateDashCard is production code that fires when a capstone quest resolves with a non-random reward — it was retained, but its two calls to renderDashboardEditor() (used to refresh the editor's Cards tab if it happened to be open) were stripped. The dashboard viewer (renderDashboard, getDashboardCards, cardGradient, cardTextColor, _dashCardClick, _capstoneCardEarned) is view-side, not editor-side, and was retained. Verification. node --check on the extracted <script> body passes clean. The remaining getElementById('ed-content-scope') call inside currentContentScope and getElementById('ed-save-flash') call inside saveCustom are both null-guarded (sel?sel.value:... and if(fl){...}), so the missing elements are handled gracefully. The Dev Bar description in Settings was updated to match the new button set (Ledger, QP Manager, +1 Day, Exit Dev). Net change. File shrunk from 35,446 lines to ~33,077 lines — roughly 2,370 lines of editor-specific HTML/CSS/JS removed with zero impact on shared content-library plumbing or quest-assignment flow. No SQL change. No data-model change.
Version 1.7028 — Character modal restructure: Traits inline on Overview, new Progress tab with downtime-as-XP readouts. User ask: (1) move Traits content (Faith, Languages, Skills, Tools) from its own tab onto the Overview page; (2) move the XP / downtime progress indicators to a new second tab; (3) expand row heights to be more readable; (4) show downtime progress bars and goal counts inline (in the Progress tab AND inside the dt-badge modal) so downtime visibly mirrors the XP system's presentation. New tab structure. Tab count stays at 3: Overview (0) now holds background + coin purse + role chips + all the Traits content; Progress (1, renamed from Traits) now holds XP tracks + active downtime tracks + earned milestone badges; Gear (2) unchanged. Swipe navigation bound _charpeekTab<2 at line ~20203 stayed correct since the max index didn't move. Traits inline on Overview. The four Traits sections — Faith (with ptag pill), Languages (🗣 pills), Skills (⭐ pills), Tools (🔧 pills, only shown when non-empty) — now render directly after the role-badge chip row inside _cpkOverviewHtml. Same markup they used to have in _cpkTraitsHtml, just relocated. _cpkTraitsHtml itself is left in place as dead code rather than deleted; the dispatch at line 17584 no longer routes to it, so it can be pruned in a future cleanup without affecting behavior. New _cpkProgressHtml function. Two sections with sectional "Cinzel" headers. (a) Experience Tracks — iterates XP_TRACKS, renders any track with XP > 0 as a button-row with track icon (1.15em, bumped from 1em), label column (.82em, bumped from .72em, min-width 90px vs 80px), current badge name, "N / threshold" readout with tabular-nums, and an 8px progress bar (was 4px) with 4px border-radius (was 2px). Row padding is 10px 12px (was 6px 9px). Uninitiated tracks (0 XP) collapse into a single "Not yet started: 🗡 🤝 🔍 ✨" footer row as before but with 1.05em icons vs the previous dim-at-full-opacity. (b) Downtime Tracks — NEW inline progress bars matching the XP rail. Walks c.dtProgress, groups keys by their canonical (prefix-stripped) form so v1.6866-era duplicate-pollution tracks collapse into one visible line; threshold logic matches the dt-badge modal (contacts=6, intel=5, everything else =8). Each active track renders as a teal-bordered button-row with 🕯 icon, "Downtime" label column, the track's readable label from downtimeTrackLabel(type,target), "N / threshold" progress readout, and an 8px teal progress bar. Duplicate tracks show a crimson "(duplicate)" note — the user can open the modal and use its existing ⇄ Merge button. Below active tracks, an "Earned Milestones" subsection lists c.downtimeBadges entries as compact display-only rows with a green ✓, the badge name, and "earned" label — tinted with the same teal background as the Downtime rail so they read as crossed-threshold achievements. Empty states: "No experience earned yet. Complete quests to earn ranks in Combat, Diplomacy, Exploration, or Arcana." and "No downtime progress yet. Assign this character to a downtime job (via the Party screen or a character card) to begin earning milestones." Click routing preserved. XP rows still carry data-a="xp-badge" + data-track=<key> and open the existing xp-badge modal (z:510 above charpeek). Active downtime rows and earned-milestone rows both carry data-a="dt-badge" and open the existing dt-badge modal, which already had full progress UI from v1.6869 / v1.6866 and includes the ⇄ Merge button when duplicate tracks are detected. So no handler changes were needed; the delegated click handler inside the charpeek body listener (line ~20238) already routes both actions correctly. Layout rationale. The Progress tab is now visually "dense with information" in a way the old Overview wasn't — the XP rail and the downtime rail share the same row shape (icon + label + value + bar) so the eye can scan both tracks as a single progress system. This was the main user point: downtime shouldn't be a silent tally that you only see in a modal; it should be as visible as XP. The 8px bars + 1.15em icons + 10/12px padding give each row about 54px of vertical real estate vs the old ~38px, which trades some density for readability on mobile. Works within the v1.7025 fixed-height charpeek modal (86vh) since the body is flex:1; overflow-y:auto, so tall Progress content scrolls internally without affecting the modal frame or the prev/next squadmate arrows. What didn't change. The xp-badge modal (ov-xpbadge) and its dt-badge render path are byte-identical — no changes needed because they already supplied the detailed per-track view. The ov-charpeek-tabs swipe bound, the header nav arrows, the footer Level Up / Back to HQ buttons, and _refreshCharpeekFooterBtns are untouched. No SQL change. No data-model change.
Version 1.7027 — Close the auto-shopping back door for Bag of Holding. Follow-up to v1.7026. Per user direction: no recruited character should ever start with OR auto-purchase a Bag of Holding, because the bag has a special in-game purpose (squad-formation artifact routed through the 500 gp enrollment path, capstone quest rewards, or store purchases paid from franchise gold). What changed. (1) ITEM_AFFINITY cleaned. The auto-shopping affinity table — consumed by pickItemForChar when a character's coin purse from a completed grind/quest is disbursed — previously listed Bag of Holding under five entries: History (skill), Wizard, Sorcerer, Runesmith (classes), and wonder (quest theme). A character with any of those class/skill identifiers and a fat enough coin purse could auto-buy a bag as a post-hire "discretionary" purchase, bypassing the squad economy. All five entries had Bag of Holding removed. The History entry collapsed to an empty array (it had only that one item); the other four kept their remaining item lists intact. (2) Defensive guard in pickItemForChar. The function has two candidate-gathering branches: an affinity-matched pass (items specifically called out for the character's class/skills) and a fallback pass that walks the full GEAR table when affinity yields nothing affordable. The fallback would still surface Bag of Holding from the GEAR definitions since it's a 400 gp type:'magic' item — cleaning ITEM_AFFINITY alone wasn't sufficient. Added _isBoHGear(g) helper at the top of pickItemForChar that filters out any GEAR entry where g.bagOfHolding===true OR g.effect.squadFormation===true. Those flags are authoritative — they're set on the built-in Bag of Holding at line ~7222 and propagated onto reskinned variants (e.g. "Longinus Carpetbag of Holding") by the v1.6968 capstone-reskin code path. So the filter catches every bag variant, not just the one exact name. Applied to both the affinity-matched filter and the fallback filter so neither path can emit a bag into a character's coin-purse shopping result. Scope. Only the coin-purse auto-shopping path is affected. The squad-enrollment path (500 gp, btn-sq-enroll), capstone-reward awards (asset-give), and the store purchase path (franchise gold, buyItem) all remain open — those are the three intended acquisition paths and they route the bag through the correct economy. A player who wants a character-level bag via capstone-specific narrative reward still gets one; a player who wants to enroll a squad with a bag still can; a player who wants to spend franchise gold at the store still can. What's closed is only the bypass — characters no longer accidentally accumulate bags from their individual coin purses. Retroactive cleanup. Not shipped in-app; the user requested a direct SQL query to strip existing Bag of Holding entries from the party table's inv and owned_items columns. Delivered alongside this version. The SQL matches any item whose name contains "bag" + "holding" or "carpetbag" (same fuzzy criterion the codebase's _isBagOfHolding helper uses since v1.544) so it catches reskinned variants like "Longinus Carpetbag of Holding" as well as plain Bag of Holding. Preview query + cleanup query provided; cleanup is wrapped in a transaction so a misfire rolls back cleanly. No SQL migration is required from the app side — the code changes in v1.7027 stand alone; the SQL is a separate one-time admin operation. No data-model change.
Version 1.7026 — Remove Bag of Holding from recruitment loadouts. User ask: no recruited character (mercenary, standard adventurer, Risen hireling, starter-guide companion) should arrive with a Bag of Holding in their starter inventory. Bags should be acquired through the squad-level economy — via the 500 gp squad-formation enrollment, capstone quest rewards, or store purchases paid from franchise gold — not bundled into individual character kits as a side effect of hire cost. Where it was. recruitLoadoutByClassBgAndCost(cls, bg, hireCost) is the single shared loadout builder. All three hire paths (⚔ Recruit Adventurer via recruitCandidate, ⭐ Custom Mercenary via the direct call, and the starter-guide Founder companion via makeStarterAdventurer) route through it. Its tierMap table assigned the elite tier — triggered when hireCost ≥ 425 per recruitCostTier — a Bag of Holding alongside two Potions of Healing. So a 425+ gp adventurer candidate, a 400+ gp custom merc, or any elite-priced hire walked in with a free bag. Fix. elite tier updated to ['Potion of Healing','Potion of Healing'] — retains the premium healing allowance (reflects the price point) but drops the bag. Veteran and starter tiers were already bag-free. Scope. The fix is forward-looking only — no retroactive migration. Existing save data with elite-tier characters already carrying a Bag of Holding keep their bags; only newly-hired recruits from this version forward are affected. Rationale: retroactively stripping items from a player's party without compensation is hostile; re-routing to franchise storage would silently restructure existing economies. If you want retroactive cleanup, let me know — I can build a migration that relocates bags from recruit inventories into franchiseStorage so the player can enroll a squad with them or sell them back. What's unaffected. (i) The squad enrollment path (500 gp + Bag of Holding routed into partyAssets with ownerType:'squad') is unchanged. (ii) Capstone reward awards of bags are unchanged. (iii) The store Bag of Holding purchase is unchanged (400 gp, type:magic, party:true). (iv) Dev-mode test adventurer "Selene Ashdown" still carries a Bag of Holding in her scripted inventory — she's a dev fixture not a player-recruited character, so outside the scope of "recruited character loadout." (v) The ITEM_AFFINITY table used by pickItemForChar during downtime coin-purse auto-shopping still lists Bag of Holding under History skill, Wizard class, Sorcerer class, Runesmith class, and the "wonder" theme — a character with a fat coin purse from a completed grind can still auto-buy a bag as a post-hire discretionary purchase. This is a separate economy from hire-time loadout (it consumes the character's own coin earnings, not franchise gold), but if you'd prefer bags to route exclusively through squad enrollment + capstone rewards + franchise-gold store buys (never auto-bought with character coin), that's a trivial additional edit — five lines in ITEM_AFFINITY. Flag this and I'll ship it in a follow-up. (vi) Encumbrance calculations (charCarriedWeight, wouldEncumber) continue to recognize Bag of Holding in a character's inv and treat items past it as weightless — needed because characters can still legitimately end up with bags via the other paths above. No SQL change. No data-model change. Single-line tier-map edit.
Version 1.7025 — Character modal: anchor to a fixed vertical position. User report: the ◀ / ▶ squadmate-navigation arrows on the character peek modal shift up and down when the user changes tabs (Overview / Traits / Gear) or navigates between squadmates, making them hard to hit. Root cause: the global .ov overlay uses align-items:center on its flex container, centering every modal vertically in the viewport. The #ov-charpeek modal has max-height:92vh but NO fixed height, so its actual rendered height shrinks to fit short content (e.g. a Founder with zero inventory and one faith line) and grows to 92vh on characters with lots of gear. As the modal's height changes, the centered-alignment math re-computes its top edge — and because the prev/next arrows are absolutely positioned at top:50% of #ov-charpeek-header (which sits at the top of the modal), the arrows physically move up or down by whatever amount the modal's height just changed. Changing tabs alone was enough to make a click-target dance 30–60 pixels. Fix: two CSS rules added to both of the codebase's duplicate stylesheet blocks (lines ~593 and ~2668). #ov-charpeek.on{align-items:flex-start;} overrides the inherited center alignment for this one modal, pinning its top edge to the overlay's top padding zone. #ov-charpeek > .modal{margin-top:6vh;height:86vh;max-height:86vh;} gives the modal a fixed height (86vh, matching the existing fallback) and a consistent 6vh offset from the top. Net result: the modal's top edge now sits at exactly 14px + 6vh below the viewport top regardless of content, and the header (with its nav arrows) never moves. Why not just pin the arrows to the viewport: considered option — moving the buttons from position:absolute inside the header to position:fixed relative to the viewport — but rejected because it decouples the arrows from the modal's visual frame. If the modal is scrollable and the user scrolls the body, the arrows would stay pinned to the viewport while the modal's chrome moves, which reads as a bug. The fixed-modal-height approach keeps the arrows positioned relative to the modal's header (the correct parent) while making sure that header itself doesn't move. Why not align-items:flex-start for all modals: considered this too but it's unnecessary for the smaller modals (confirmation dialogs, picker modals) that benefit visually from being vertically centered. The charpeek modal is uniquely affected because it's the only one with (a) content that varies dramatically in height between states, (b) a header with persistent nav controls that the user needs to target repeatedly. Other modals either have stable content or don't have controls the user hunts for. Scope: only the character peek modal (#ov-charpeek). The downtime reveal modal (ov-dt-roll), quest assignment (ov-quest), inventory options (ov-cpk-inv), and every other .ov-based overlay keep their current centered behavior. The inline style="max-height:92vh" on the modal element remains (no edit there) — it's harmless since the CSS height:86vh takes precedence for actual sizing, and inline max-height:92vh specificity-wins over the external max-height:86vh without causing any observable difference (86 ≤ 92). Expected visual change: short-content characters will now show a small patch of empty parchment at the bottom of the body tab (where previously the modal hugged the content). This is the intended trade-off. Users who are hunting for the nav arrows will find them exactly where they left them. No SQL change. No data-model change. Pure CSS.
Version 1.7024 — Mercenary equipment is now personal. User ask: in the merc recruitment space, merc equipment was listed as modifiable / transferable / sellable — but all mercenary equipment at hire should be marked as personal (display-only, locked to that character). Rationale: a mercenary is paid a premium (⭐ custom hire, 500+ gp base) specifically because they come with their own professional-grade gear. The franchise rents the merc's services; the gear travels with them. Pre-v1.7024 the starter kit landed in c.inv without any corresponding c.ownedItems entries, so the character modal's Gear tab rendered it under "Shared" where Sell and Move-to-Storage buttons were active — which contradicted the hire-contract narrative. (1) Hire-time marking. The Mercenary hire handler (around line 22455) now captures the generated starter inventory in a local _mercInv and assigns both inv:_mercInv and ownedItems:_mercInv.slice() on the new party object. The standard recruitLoadoutByClassBgAndCost(cls, bg, cost) kit — class weapons, armor, kits, background items, and tier-appropriate consumables — all land in the Personal bucket from the moment the merc joins. Items the merc later acquires via downtime coin-purse shopping (already personal via the existing v1.6886-era rule at the downtime-purchase site) or via capstone-reward Give (already personal via the v1.6924 rule at the asset-give site) continue to be personal. So every route by which a merc gains gear ends up in ownedItems; the gap at hire time is the only one that needed closing. (2) Back-fill migration for existing saves. New migrateMercPersonalV1_7024() scans S.party, finds every character with c.premium===true (the mercenary flag set at hire), and ensures every non-sentinel string in their c.inv is also present in c.ownedItems. Idempotent — a merc whose ownedItems is already fully synced is a no-op on the next run. If the migration actually adds any items, it logs "⭐ Marked N mercenary-kit items as personal across M mercs." to the hire log so the state change is auditable. Items a merc has already lost pre-v1.7024 (sold before this migration ran, or moved to franchise storage) are NOT restored — the migration only syncs the personal flag against currently-held inventory. Wired into all three load paths (load(), code-login post-pull, main sbPull) alongside the existing migration chain; runs after migrateGrindPendingDedupV1_7017 so it has stable party state to walk. (3) Render filter already respects personal. The character-modal Gear tab (around line 17717) splits c.inv into personalItems (items also in ownedItems) and sharedItems. Personal items render as display-only rows with the 🔒 lock icon and a "personal" label — no Sell button, no Move-to-Storage button. Shared items render as clickable buttons opening the Sell / Move-to-Storage drawer. So once the migration flags a merc's gear, the UI correctly hides the management affordances with no further rendering changes. (4) Handler guards were already in place. Belt-and-braces check: the sell-inv handler at line 19314+ already guards with if((c3.ownedItems||[]).indexOf(itemName)!==-1){alert('Personal items cannot be sold.');return;} and the to-storage handler at line 19254+ already guards with if((c2.ownedItems||[]).indexOf(itemName)!==-1){alert('… personal property — it cannot be moved to Franchise Storage.');return;}. These protect against direct handler invocation bypassing the render-side filter (e.g. DOM manipulation or stale button references). No changes needed here — the existing guards do exactly what's required. Scope boundaries: (i) This patch touches ONLY mercenaries (c.premium===true). Standard recruits (⚔ Recruit Adventurer) and hirelings (🪓 Hire a Hireling) keep their current semantics — their starter gear remains Shared inventory, which matches their narrative of "franchise employees whose kit belongs to the franchise." If you want either of those flipped to personal-by-default, say the word. (ii) Capstone NPC contacts (c.contact) have had their full inventory treated as personal since v1.6901 via a separate backfill in sbPull; this patch doesn't disturb that path. (iii) The Founder's initial loadout and hireling graduation kits are untouched — they're not mercenary hires and the personal-vs-shared framing doesn't apply the same way. (iv) A merc who picks something up during an adventure via the capstone-reward Give flow already gets that item marked personal (v1.6924 rule), so post-hire acquisitions are covered. Undo / reversal: there's no UI to manually un-flag a merc's gear as personal. If you truly need a merc to transfer gear (e.g. a legacy save where a merc's Chain Shirt should move to a standard recruit who lost their armor), the cleanest recovery is via the dev-mode editor or by clearing the specific item from c.ownedItems via console. A future version could add a per-item "✎ Unlock" admin control if this turns out to be a common need; for now the contract is "merc gear is merc gear for the character's lifetime." No SQL change. No data-model change beyond the additive ownedItems entries, which ride inside the existing party jsonb blob.
Version 1.7023 — Split-group polish: add-a-new-split button + fix drag handles on member cards. (1) ➕ New split footer button. User ask: a button at the bottom of every split-group that creates a new "split" without first having to split an existing task's sub-tasks. The new task appears beneath existing members with its own sub-task add field, like any other member. Landed: renderSplitGroupHtml footer now emits a btn bg sm "➕ New split" button alongside the existing Defer/Return and ↕ Reorder buttons, keyed by data-a="sgaddmember". The click handler in the #tlist delegator finds the last S.active index occupied by a member of the target group, splices a new task object in right after it. New task inherits the first member's gp and _col (multi-col column assignment), starts with name "New split" (user can rename via ✎), no sub-tasks (user adds via the normal sub-task add field on the card), and a fresh _tid. Scrolls the new card into view with scrollIntoView({behavior:'smooth',block:'center'}) and focuses its ✎ edit button after an 80ms render tick so the user can rename immediately without hunting. Writes a dedicated sgaddmember undo entry; revert splices the new task out and dissolves any lone-member residue (matches the extraction dissolve pattern below). (2) Drag handles on member cards now work on desktop. User-reported bug: the ⋮⋮ drag handle renders on task cards inside a split-group but doesn't function when grabbed. Root cause: the dragstart validity check at the card branch required src.parentElement===#tlist OR src.parentElement.classList.contains('tcol'). A split-group member's parentElement is .split-group (which is itself inside .tcol or #tlist), so neither condition matched. The handler called e.preventDefault() and the drag aborted silently. Fix: extended the validity check to accept .split-group as a third valid parent. The existing task-drop logic then handles the rest via _tid-based splice-and-reinsert, which works identically whether the source is inside a group or not. Intra-group reorder: if you drag a member and drop on a sibling member within the same group, the drop handler detects srcTask._splitGroup === dstTask._splitGroup and keeps _splitGroup set — it's just a positional shuffle within S.active, and renderSplitGroupHtml filters members by gid in whatever order they appear. Cross-group / extraction: dropping a member onto a card outside its group, onto a different group's member, or onto an empty column clears srcTask._splitGroup — the card is now a plain top-level task. If extraction leaves the source group with only one remaining member, that lone member's _splitGroup is also cleared (the meta entry gets reaped by ensureTaskIds on the next render pass). Matches the codebase's existing "≥2 members = group" rule. Dragover: cardValid also extended to accept .split-group parent so member cards are valid drop targets, and a small rule added to suppress treating the source member's own group wrapper as a drop target — if you hover over a sibling inside your own group, the drop bar lands at the sibling's position (intra-group reorder), not at the whole group's edge. Undo: the dragTask undo entry now captures prevSg (the source's _splitGroup at drag time). On revert, if prevSg is a string, _splitGroup is restored; if null/undefined, it's deleted. The corresponding extract-partner (the sibling whose group was dissolved as collateral) is not restored because it's a separate task — if the user wants the partner back in the group they can undo-revert the extraction AND re-associate manually. One-level undo was deemed sufficient; capturing the full residue state for revert adds complexity disproportionate to the use case. Scope boundaries: (i) Only desktop multi-col (#tlist.cols-2 / .cols-3), matching the rest of the drag system. 1-col mode uses the ↕ Reorder modal. (ii) The ⋮⋮ handle on the group WRAPPER (header row, v1.6983) is unaffected — that one was already working. (iii) Dropping a member onto its own group's wrapper edge (above/below the header) is now treated as a cancel (no-op), since we explicitly suppress sg-valid when the source is inside that group. Drop onto a sibling member inside the group is the reorder path. Authoring workflow: to start a split group with three splits without typing sub-tasks first, split any task once (creates 2 members), then tap ➕ New split once to get the third. Rename each via ✎. Add sub-tasks per member at your own pace. No SQL change. No data-model change beyond the additive prevSg field on dragTask undo entries (optional, handled by !==undefined guard).
Version 1.7022 — Inbox three-button footer: Close / Clear Inbox / Collect All. (1) Close unchanged — just dismisses the modal. (2) Clear Inbox (new): marks every ready quest and ready grind entry with a non-persistent-but-synced _inboxDismissed:true flag. _collectInboxItems filters these out, so the inbox list empties and the red badge dot goes dark. The rewards remain in state — the user can still collect them via the Quest Map's Collect All or by tapping individual grind cards in the Grinds list. This answers the user's ask of "let them clear the inbox without collecting." Downtime rolls are deliberately not dismissible — they carry game-state side effects (d20 stat math) that shouldn't be buried. Logs "✉ Inbox cleared: N notification(s) hidden. Rewards are unclaimed — collect via the Quest Map when you're ready." so the action is reversible-looking even though the flag isn't exposed for manual re-lighting (the user would just wait for the next new return to re-light the badge naturally). (3) Collect All now mirrors the Quest Map's Fast-Forward semantics exactly: same QP cost via qpCfg().costFastForward (default 50), same confirmation dialog, same inline reward-distribution logic copied from the qmap fast-forward handler. No more fake-click dispatch — the v1.7021 workaround (splice-siblings-then-click) is replaced with a direct implementation that resolves each grindPending entry by object reference via indexOf(entry), sidestepping v1.7013's same-id-collision entirely. The button label now reads "✓ Collect All (50 QP)" when QP mode is enabled and the user isn't in dev mode, or plain "✓ Collect All" otherwise. Disabled with 50% opacity and a tooltip when the user can't afford the cost. Visibility: both action buttons (Clear Inbox + Collect All) are hidden when the only inbox contents are downtime rolls — dismissing them isn't supported and collecting them can't happen via this path (the downtime d20 flow is the single legal route). Close remains visible in all states. Persistence: _inboxDismissed lives on the pq/gp objects inside S.questState. That state rides as a blob inside the franchise row's settings jsonb (main auth) or the login_codes.player_data blob (code-login), both of which serialize objects whole — so the flag round-trips across devices without any schema change. When the entry is eventually collected, it's spliced out of the array entirely; the flag goes with it. No SQL change. No data-model change.
Version 1.7021 — Inbox "Collect All" fix: same-id collision with v1.7013's per-territory grinds. User report: three ready items in the inbox, Collect All yields a "Not ready yet!" alert and doesn't clear them. Root cause: the batch handler dispatches one hidden click per ready grind, carrying data-gid="<id>". The document-level gcollect handler resolves the click by walking qs.grindPending with forEach and assigning gpIdx every time gp.id===gid matches — so gpIdx ends up pointing at the LAST matching entry, regardless of its readiness. With v1.7013's per-territory grinds, the same template id can appear in multiple concurrent instances (three different char sets on "g0 Patrol the District," say). If the last-indexed sibling is still running (doneAt > now), the handler alerts "Not ready yet!" and returns without touching the actually-ready earlier entry. The batch loop then moves to the next iteration, repeats, and the user sees three misleading alerts while their ready rewards remain uncollected. Fix: before each dispatched click, temporarily splice out all non-ready siblings sharing the target id so the handler's last-match-wins lookup finds only the ready entry. Siblings are sorted by descending index (so earlier splices don't shift the indices we care about) and are pushed back to qs.grindPending after the click completes. Ordering within grindPending isn't load-bearing elsewhere in the codebase — the runtime scans the array in every call site — so restoring via push rather than splice at the original position is fine. Empty-action feedback. When a user clicks Collect All but only downtime rolls were in the inbox (which this path deliberately skips — they need the d20 reveal modal), the adventure log now reports "✉ Inbox: N downtime roll(s) pending — tap the row to reveal the d20." rather than going silent. Pre-fix the user would click the button, see no change, and have no feedback about why. Not changed: the handler's resolution strategy is unchanged — we work around it from the batch caller. Re-architecting the handler to resolve by a compound key (id + doneAt + chars) would be cleaner but would require touching every data-gid emit site (currently only the single one inside the Grinds list per-card render) and would be a broader refactor than warranted for this fix. The localized workaround captures the correctness without rippling. Design question for follow-up: whether "Collect All" should dismiss the inbox notification without collecting rewards — i.e. let the user clear the visual badge while leaving the pending rewards in place for later manual collection. Current behaviour (and v1.7021) keeps the inbox as a derived view of the reward backlog: if you don't want to see the dot, you collect. Decided to keep this semantic for now since an explicit dismiss layer would risk users losing track of owed rewards; we can revisit if the collect path continues to feel cumbersome. No SQL change. No data-model change.
Version 1.7020 — Auto-prompt toggle for contract completion. User ask: after shipping v1.7019's auto-prompt (Done modal opens automatically when every sub-task is checked), surface a "don't ask me again" checkbox inside the modal itself, with a reminder that the preference can be flipped back on from Settings. What landed: (1) Settings field. New boolean S.settings.autoPromptSubtaskComplete in DEFAULT_SETTINGS (default true) plus a coercion line in normalizeSettings so existing saves without the field get the default on load. (2) Settings UI. A new "Contract Preferences" section placed right after "Daily Reset" holds a single labelled checkbox ("Auto-prompt on completion") with a hint line explaining what it controls. Populated on applySettings; change handler wired once via a _wired sentinel so repeat boot (post-auth refresh) doesn't stack listeners. Writes S.settings.autoPromptSubtaskComplete and calls save() on every flip — no explicit save button needed, consistent with the theme/access toggles elsewhere. (3) Auto-prompt gate. The subtask-toggle auto-open path from v1.7019 now reads the setting via (!S.settings || S.settings.autoPromptSubtaskComplete!==false) — turning the checkbox off silently suppresses the modal. The manual "Done" button path is unchanged (it always opens the modal). (4) In-modal checkbox. A new dashed-top-border section appended to the Done modal: "Don't auto-open this when I finish all sub-tasks" with a smaller hint "You can turn it back on in Settings → Contract Preferences." Section is display:none by default and revealed only when the modal was auto-opened. A new module flag window._doneModalAutoOpened distinguishes the two entry paths: the subtask-toggle handler sets it to true and shows the section; the manual Done-button handler sets it to false and hides the section. (5) Apply-on-dismiss helper. New _applyDoneDontAskIfChecked() reads both the flag and the checkbox on exit; when both are truthy it writes S.settings.autoPromptSubtaskComplete=false, calls save(), and logs "⚙ Auto-prompt on contract completion turned off. Re-enable in Settings → Contract Preferences." to the adventure log (so the change is discoverable and the log entry itself tells the user where to find the reversal control). Called from both the OK button handler (top of function, before any other work) and the Cancel button handler. The flag is cleared after each apply so the next modal open starts fresh. Why a flag instead of checking the checkbox's visibility directly: CSS display state isn't a reliable signal for "was this auto-opened" — a future refactor could reveal the section for other reasons, and the intent of the toggle is specifically "I saw this appear without my asking." A boolean state flag captures that intent cleanly. Persistence: the field lives in S.settings which rides inside the existing franchise row's settings jsonb — no new column needed, no SQL migration. Code-session accounts (which save the whole S blob to login_codes.player_data) pick up the field for free. Not in scope: a granular preference for "ask me but only for tasks with N+ sub-tasks" or "ask once per task per day rather than once per 30 seconds" — the current 30-second anti-thrash window is fine for the primary use case, and users who find the automatic trigger too eager can turn it off entirely and use the manual Done button as the single opt-in path. No SQL change. No data-model change beyond the additive settings field.
Version 1.7019 — Inbox button render fix + recurring contracts feature. (A) Inbox Collect All label fix. The v1.7018 button label was written as the escape sequence \\u2713 inside a static HTML string literal — JS escape processing only happens inside JS source, not in HTML attributes/text, so the literal backslash-u-2-7-1-3 rendered in the DOM. Replaced with the actual ✓ character. (B) Recurring contracts. User ask: when a contract (or all its subtasks) has been completed, prompt to delete OR recur with cadence options — "tomorrow," "daily," "every other day," "every N days," "weekly on [weekday]" — and route the recurring choice to the archive so the existing morning-reset loop reactivates it at the scheduled time. What landed: (1) Done modal expanded. The ov-done modal's Action select now has three options: Archive (one-shot, default), Recur…, Delete. Choosing Recur reveals a sub-panel with a Schedule select and two conditional sub-fields: an N-days input (shown only for "Every N days") and a Weekday select (shown only for "Weekly on"). Toggle logic lives in _syncDoneRecurUI() wired to the change events of both selects. (2) Recurrence cadence engine extended. checkFavourites (the morning-reset loop that reactivates archived tasks) already supported daily, weekly, monthly, yearly. Added a new sub-mode inside the existing custom freq: when an archive entry has both autoIntervalDays (integer ≥ 1) and autoIntervalAnchor (yyyy-mm-dd), the task reactivates when (today − anchor) is a positive multiple of the interval. Powers "every other day" (interval=2), "every N days" (interval=N), and "tomorrow only" (interval=1 with one-shot). Legacy custom-freq tasks (no interval fields) fall back to the pre-existing fav-driven behavior. (3) Tomorrow is one-shot. Marked via _oneShot:true on the archive entry. When the reactivation loop activates a one-shot, it then clears fav/autoIntervalDays/autoIntervalAnchor/_oneShot so the entry becomes a plain archived task — fires once, done. (4) Auto-prompt on subtask completion. The subtask checkbox change handler now detects the transition from "not all done" → "all done" and opens the Done modal with a pre-filled message ("All sub-tasks of 'X' are complete..."). Gated three ways: (a) only fires when a box just went unchecked→checked (so the automatic trigger is tied to a deliberate user action), (b) requires every subtask to be done, (c) suppresses re-prompts within 30 seconds (window._lastAutoCompletePrompt) so check/uncheck thrash doesn't re-surface the modal. The manual "Done" button path still works unchanged; this is a convenience layer. (5) Recurrence metadata preserved across reactivation. When checkFavourites pushes a task from S.archived into S.active, the live task now carries the recurrence fields forward — so when the user completes it AGAIN, they can archive/recur with the original cadence visible in the UI (the Done modal resets to "Archive" default each time; the fields remain on the record for re-selection via the form). (6) Supabase persistence. sbPush now writes five new columns on archived_tasks: auto_interval_days, auto_interval_anchor, auto_weekday, auto_monthday, auto_yearly_date, plus a boolean one_shot. sbPull reads them back with nullable-safe fallbacks (typeof…==='number'?…:null) so pre-migration rows coming back as missing-column nulls don't crash the hydrator. Code-session accounts (which save the whole archived array as a JSON blob) pick up the new fields for free — no separate code path needed. Local localStorage save is JSON.stringify(S) whole, same story. SQL migration required before deploying (safe to run ahead of time; columns are additive and nullable):

ALTER TABLE archived_tasks ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS auto_interval_days integer;
ALTER TABLE archived_tasks ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS auto_interval_anchor text;
ALTER TABLE archived_tasks ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS auto_weekday integer;
ALTER TABLE archived_tasks ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS auto_monthday integer;
ALTER TABLE archived_tasks ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS auto_yearly_date text;
ALTER TABLE archived_tasks ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS one_shot boolean DEFAULT false;

Deployment order: run the SQL first, then deploy the HTML. The hydrator tolerates missing columns (returns null) so the app works either way, but for actual recurrence you want the writes to stick. Log messages: choosing Recur logs "🔁 Contract 'X' set to recur (cadence summary)" to the adventure log. Not in scope: editing recurrence on an already-archived task from the Archive screen (would need a recurrence-editor modal on the archive row), pause/resume of recurrence, recurrence on groups/split-groups (groups archive as individual members — if you want group-level recurrence, let me know; the scoping decision is nontrivial). Undo: the existing tarch undo entry covers recur-to-archive exactly the same as plain archive — undo restores the task to S.active and removes the archive entry. No data-model change for active tasks — recurrence fields only live on archive entries; active tasks carry them forward on reactivation for informational/UI purposes but never actually use them while active.
Version 1.7018 — Three cosmetic polish items. (1) Inbox: Collect All button. The ov-inbox modal grows a new \u2713 Collect All action that auto-collects every ready quest and every ready grind in one click. Implementation: calls the existing autoCollectExpired() for quests (batch path already present), then for each qs.grindPending entry with doneAt ≤ now synthesizes a hidden <button data-a="gcollect" data-gid="...">, appends it to the DOM, dispatches a real click (so the existing document-level click handler on line 23619 runs the full reward-distribution/log/badge-clear path), then removes the button. No duplicate logic. Downtime pending rolls are deliberately skipped because those need the d20 reveal modal (openDowntimePendingModal) to show the player what they got; the button tooltip explains this and the summary log entry reports any downtime rolls that were left pending. Visibility: the button only shows when at least one quest or grind row is actionable in the current inbox contents, computed in openInboxModal() before the empty-state gate. After clicking, openInboxModal() is called again so the user sees the updated (often empty) state — they can then close manually. Logs "\u2709 Inbox: auto-collected N quests + M grinds" to the adventure log so the batch action is auditable. (2) Party screen: collapse/expand all squads. New button in the #party-tagbar row next to \u2694 Manage Squads. Label is dynamic: "\u25b4 Collapse all" when ANY panel is currently open, "\u25be Expand all" when every panel is currently collapsed — so the action the button will perform reads correctly at a glance. Scope includes every real squad name plus every per-territory unassigned bucket (__unassigned__::<territory>, v1.7006). Click handler in the #party-tagbar delegator reads the data-open attribute (pre-computed by the emit code to reflect what the button SHOULD DO on click), walks the full panel-key list, and either sets or deletes S.squadCollapsed[key] for each — matching the v1.7011 persistence pattern so the state sticks across renderParty() redraws and Supabase syncs. Button is suppressed entirely when there are no panels to toggle (empty franchise). (3) Rename "Unassigned" to "Headquarters" (or local HQ building name). The virtual unassigned bucket's display name on the Party screen is now derived from a new territoryHQName(territory) helper. For the origin territory (the territory the franchise started in — no building was required to unlock it), the name is always "Headquarters" — no real estate needed to earn the label. For any later-unlocked territory, territoryHQName walks S.partyAssets in ownership order and returns the first qualifying building's name (either the player's custom meta.name if they named it at purchase, or the base BUILDINGS[*].name). "Qualifying" means price ≥ TERRITORY_UNLOCK_BUILDING_MIN_GP (1000 gp, the same threshold that triggered the territory unlock in v1.7006) and ownerType==='cycle' owned by a cycle whose territory field matches. Since later territories were unlocked BY building such a building, there's always a qualifying asset available (monotonic unlock rule means even if the building is later sold, the territory stays unlocked — in that unusual case the helper falls back to generic "Headquarters"). The panel display name reads "Headquarters · Minsaka" (or "The Crossroads Safe House · Gojimura" if the player named their Gojimura-territory Safe House "The Crossroads"), keeping the territory suffix as a locator. The 🧭 icon remains on the panel header since that glyph is used consistently across the codebase (char-peek unassigned chip, swap modal, grind ad-hoc picker) as the "not in a real squad" signifier — changing it here would create visual drift across three other surfaces. Internal name ('__unassigned__::<territory>') unchanged — the panel key is still the data-model sentinel that every downstream consumer (S.squadCollapsed, event handlers, swap modal) depends on. No SQL change. No data-model change. The new territoryHQName helper reads existing fields on existing state.
Version 1.7017 — Grind eligibility: busy-filter scope fix + stale-archive cleanup migration. Diagnosis from user's rqGrindDiag('Minsaka') output: pool size 34, busy count 38, avail 0. Three squads correctly clocked in, their 27 members correctly pooled, plus 7 unassigned Minsaka chars → 34 in the pool. But busy contained 38 character indices (more than the pool itself), all coming from a combination of (a) duplicate entries in qs.grindPending where the same grind id ran multiple concurrent instances with different char sets, and (b) setProgress[*].grindPending archives that mirrored those same entries as stale copies. Every character in the pool was marked busy by at least one of those stale/duplicate entries, so the available count collapsed to zero even though no live grind was actually running for them. Root cause — busy filter: computeGrindEligibility walked qs.grindPending without gating on doneAt > now, and ALSO walked every setProgress[sid].grindPending archive regardless of whether that cycle was currently active. Ready-to-collect grinds (past their doneAt) kept their chars busy forever; completed-cycle archive pendings pinned chars busy indefinitely. Root cause — archive pollution: saveActiveSetProgress has historically overwritten setProgress[qs.setId].grindPending with the live list on every save, with no dedup. If the live list grew duplicates (a bug in confirmQuestAssignment or the auto-assign path sometime prior), the archive mirror grew along with it. The diagnostic showed identical 22-entry lists in both live and archive for the custom-1776809681966 cycle. Fix #1 (runtime): the live qs.grindPending busy branch now gates on (gp.doneAt||0)>now, matching the guard already used for qs.pending. A grind past its ready time no longer ties up its characters. Fix #2 (runtime): the setProgress[*].grindPending walk is removed entirely. Archives are a record of past/paused cycles; they shouldn't contribute to live busy state. The active cycle's grindPending lives on qs.grindPending (covered by Fix #1); any legitimate paused-cycle work sits in qs.pending which is unchanged. Fix #3 (migration): migrateGrindPendingDedupV1_7017() runs on every load path and does two cleanups. (a) Dedup live qs.grindPending. Entries are keyed by (id, territory, doneAt, sorted chars) and the first occurrence of each key wins; subsequent duplicates are dropped. (b) Shake grindPending off non-active archives. For every setProgress[sid] where sid !== qs.setId, grindPending is set to []. Those archives hold completion history (done, repeatCounts) that's intentionally preserved; the in-progress list is not meant to live there. The active cycle's archive is left alone as a conservative measure. If the helper removes anything, it logs "🧹 Cleaned N stale grind entries (archive dedup)" to the adventure log. Idempotent. No-op on fresh saves. Expected user impact on first load after deploy: the adventure log will show a one-time cleanup message with the count of stale entries removed (in the diagnostic case, ~22 duplicates + ~22 archive mirrors = ~44). The Grinds modal's summary band should then show accurate eligible-character counts that match the in-progress list. Auto-assign should pool characters correctly. Supabase sync: the cleanup mutates S.questState in-place; the next save() and debounced sbPush will persist the cleaned state. If the session ends before push completes, the cleanup just runs again on next load — it's idempotent. No SQL change. No data-model change beyond the shape cleanup. Still in v1.7016's diagnostic toolkit: window.rqGrindDiag(territory?) remains available for any follow-up investigation. After first load on v1.7017, re-running it should show busy count dropping from 38 to something <= the number of currently-running (unready) grinds across active cycle + downtime jobs.
Version 1.7016 — Fix: clockInSquadToGrinds threw TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'push') when clocking any squad into a territory whose grind slot was empty. User report: after clocking three squads out of Minsaka to reset state, trying to clock them back in crashed with the TypeError at line 9844. Also explains why v1.7015's "pool shows 0" fix seemed to have no effect — any clock-in attempted at any point since v1.7013 would have silently failed through the same race if the destination slot was empty, so the live state genuinely had zero clocked-in squads to count. Root cause: race between two helpers that both mutate S.questCycleSquads. clockInSquadToGrinds ran in this order: (1) call cycleSquadMap() to get a reference to S.questCycleSquads, (2) assign map[key]=[] to normalize the slot to an empty array, (3) call squadIsLockedByActiveCycle(squadName) as a guard, (4) call squadGrindTerritory(squadName) as another guard, (5) map[key].push(squadName). Steps 3 and 4 both internally call cycleSquadMap(), which runs a stale-cleanup pass that DELETES any grind-key slot whose array is empty (v1.7013 feature to avoid accumulating empty territory keys). So step 2 created an empty array, step 3 or 4 triggered cleanup that deleted it, and step 5 tried to push onto the now-undefined slot. Only manifested when the destination slot was empty (new franchise on that territory, or freshly cleared by clock-outs) because cleanup only fires for length-zero arrays. Fix: restructure clockInSquadToGrinds so ALL guard checks (active-cycle lock, already-clocked-in-this-territory, clocked-in-different-territory) run BEFORE any state mutation. Only after guards pass does the function acquire the map, ensure the slot exists as an array, and push. No helper call happens between the array creation and the .push, so the race is eliminated. Also tightened the "already in" guard — now checks squadGrindTerritory explicitly instead of reading map[key].indexOf, which is semantically cleaner (one squad can't be in two slots, so the grind-territory value IS the answer to the question). Verified via local simulation: three consecutive clock-ins into a fresh territory now succeed and end-to-end computeGrindEligibility('Minsaka') returns all 9 members across the three squads. No SQL change. No data-model change. Existing v1.7015 rqGrindDiag(territory?) console helper remains available for any follow-up diagnosis.
Version 1.7015 — Grind-pool filter bugfix + diagnostic. User report: three squads clocked in to grind, auto-assign (50 QP) only placed 4 characters, and the Grinds modal's "eligible" readout showed zero remaining despite 25+ party members still being in those squads. Root cause (confirmed): computeGrindEligibility had a subtle back-compat trap introduced in v1.7013. The signature is computeGrindEligibility(territory) where omitting territory means "all territories" (_scopeTerr=null) and passing a specific territory scopes the pool. But the scope resolution was (arguments.length>0)?(territory||''):null — which meant passing an explicit empty string (the legacy zero-value placeholder that some callers use) became _scopeTerr='', NOT null. The function then called grindSquadNames(''), which calls _grindKeyForTerritory('') — and that helper returns GRIND_CYCLE_KEY (the bare legacy key __grind__). After the v1.7013 migration, the bare key is deleted (its contents moved under origin-territory). So map['__grind__'] is undefined, grindSquadNames('') returns [], and no squad members enter the pool — even though three squads are correctly clocked in under __grind__::Minsaka. Affected callers: the auto-assign handler passes _grindsListTerritory||'', and the confirm handler does the same. Both collapse to '' whenever the Grinds modal was opened via a path that didn't set _grindsListTerritory (e.g. the three round-trip reopen sites if they fired before any territory-aware open). Fix: one-line change to the scope resolution — (arguments.length>0&&territory)?territory:null. Now both "no argument" and "empty-string argument" both resolve to the all-territories pool, which is the safe fallback. A falsy territory can never accidentally select the deleted bare-key slot. Why 4 got through anyway: those 4 character indices were already committed to existing grindPending entries in setProgress[*].grindPending before the failing auto-assign call — the 50 QP was spent and 4 grinds launched from prior-state assignments, but the auto-assign loop's attempt to add more failed because the current-call eligibility pool was empty. New diagnostic: window.rqGrindDiag(territory?) dumps the full grind state — origin/unlocked territories, raw questCycleSquads map with legacy-key annotations, per-squad home/grind-slot, grindSquadNames output for all-territories and scoped calls, computeGrindEligibility results for all-territories and scoped calls, plus every live and archived grindPending entry with its territory field. Run from the browser console; returns the full report as a string and logs it. Intended for reports of "pool shows the wrong number" or "my squad isn't eligible even though it's clocked in". No SQL change. No data-model change. No migration required.
Version 1.7014 — Test-account quick-fill pills on the login modal. Two grayed-out dashed-border pills (stable@stable.com and restart1@restart.com) sit below the Register / Log In row; tapping either populates the email field and focuses the password field. Purpose: the user is actively running two test franchises side-by-side (a mid-game stable franchise for runway-building plus a fresh restart for sequence testing) and was re-typing the email on every switch. Passwords are deliberately not stored or auto-filled — security stays with the user. Styling: monospace text, dashed border, parchment background, gold-dark title hint on hover. The pills live inside the email login panel only (not the class-code panel). Delegated click handler attached once on page load via a closure scoped to #loginBox; handler reads data-fill off the clicked pill, so adding more test accounts later is a one-line HTML addition. If this pattern becomes useful for non-test workflows, the pills could graduate into a stored-accounts feature, but for now they're intentionally hardcoded to keep the surface area small. No SQL change. No data-model change.
Version 1.7013 — Per-territory grind shifts. The global Grind Missions card is retired; grinds are now scoped per territory with separate clock-in, separate in-progress tracking, and a grind sub-card surfaced inside each territory's panel on the Quest Map. User rationale: territory expansion should feel like it magnifies franchise output, not just widens squad-slot inventory. Letting squads stay productive in regions the main storyline has moved past — Minsaka still generating GP while Gojimura hosts the active cycle — ties passive income to how many territories the franchise operates in. Data model: cycleSquadMap()['__grind__'] (a single flat array of all clocked-in squad names, v1.6910) is superseded by territory-prefixed keys '__grind__::<territoryName>', each holding its own array. The bare key prefix is preserved and parsed by a new _isGrindKey(k) helper; territory is extracted via _grindKeyTerritory(k). grindPending entries gain a .territory field so the same grind template (e.g. g0 Patrol the District) can run concurrently in Minsaka and Gojimura as independent missions. GRIND[] itself is untouched — grind templates remain universal. Rule: one territory per squad. A squad can be clocked in to exactly one territory's shift. clockInSquadToGrinds(squadName, territory) refuses if the squad is already on a different territory's shift. The territory argument defaults to the squad's homeTerritory (v1.7006) when omitted, so ambiguity doesn't need explicit UI every time. New helper: squadGrindTerritory(squadName) returns which territory a squad is currently clocked into, or ''. Helpers made territory-aware: grindSquadNames(territory) scopes the returned list (omit for all-territories union); availableSquadsForGrindClockIn(territory) filters to squads whose homeTerritory matches; computeGrindEligibility(territory) restricts the character pool to members of squads clocked into THIS territory plus unassigned characters whose homeTerritory matches (unassigned Gojimura-based chars don't leak into Minsaka's shift). Back-compat: all three helpers still work when called without an argument, returning franchise-wide data for legacy callers. Migration: migrateGrindToTerritoriesV1_7013() runs in all three load paths (load(), code-login, sbPull) after migrateToTerritoryV1_7006 has set S.originTerritory. Any pre-v1.7013 flat grind clock-ins under the bare __grind__ key get moved under the origin territory's key (typically __grind__::Minsaka). Existing grindPending entries without a .territory field get stamped with the origin territory so the Grinds modal's territory filter doesn't hide legitimate in-progress missions. Idempotent — rerunning on already-migrated state is a no-op. The legacy bare key is deleted on migration to keep state clean. Quest Map UI change: the old global Grind Missions card between incomplete cycles and the territory panels is removed entirely. In its place, each territory panel (which always renders now for every unlocked territory, even without completed cycles) opens with a green-tinted "⚙ Grind Missions" sub-card showing this territory's clocked-in squads with ✕ clock-out chips, an "➕ Add Squad" button, and an "⚡ Open Grinds" button. Both buttons carry data-terr attributes and route through territory-aware click handlers. The territory panel then shows completed cycles (if any) inside the v1.7011 nested "✓ Completed" <details> as before. Grinds modal scoping: openGrindsList(territory) stores the argument in a module-scope _grindsListTerritory; renderGrindsList reads it to scope the eligibility pool, filter the in-progress list to gp.territory===scope, and emit a territory header above the summary band ("🗺 Minsaka · Grind Shift"). Three reentry sites updated to preserve scope across modal round-trips: the successful-grind-start path (if(grindObj2)...), questModalAbandon(), and the party character-peek Grind button (which passes charHomeTerritory for natural scoping). Clock-in picker: openGrindClockInPicker(territory) uses _grindClockInTerritory as the commit-time handoff. The picker header message updates dynamically to name the territory being clocked into so there's no surprise. Confirm flow: confirmQuestAssignment's grind branch stamps territory:_commitTerr on every new grindPending entry. The error-path alert includes the territory name. Auto-assign: the grind-auto handler reads _grindsListTerritory, scopes its eligibility pool to that territory, and stamps territory on every pending entry it creates. Since the same grind template can now run concurrently across territories, the "already-in-progress" skip checks both gp.id AND gp.territory, so one territory running Patrol the District doesn't block another from running it too. Supabase sync: questCycleSquads is part of the state blob that sbPush serializes, so the new territory-prefixed keys round-trip cleanly via the existing sync path. No SQL change. Deferred: the v1.7007-committed squad-relocation flow (paid move between territories) should, when it ships, auto-clock-out a relocated squad from its current territory's grind shift before updating homeTerritory — otherwise a Minsaka squad moved to Gojimura would still appear on Minsaka's shift. Comment flagged inside clockOutSquadFromGrinds. Deliberately not addressed: no cross-territory grind clock-in (e.g. "clock into both Minsaka and Gojimura for 50 QP/day"); authoring system stayed universal (GRIND templates aren't tagged with a territory — every territory can run all templates). Both are reasonable future extensions but weren't part of this design brief.
Version 1.7012 — Quest Map "🪄 Arcane Offerings" rack: daily-rotated, territory-filtered, QP-priced magic items that unlock hidden cycles on purchase. Quest Editor v1.691 required for authoring. SQL MIGRATION REQUIRED before deploying this version — run both statements in the Supabase SQL editor:
ALTER TABLE items ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS qm_rack BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE; ALTER TABLE items ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS qp_price INT DEFAULT 0;User ask: add a rotating 3-slot rack of magic items to the Quest Map that costs QP instead of GP, and where buying each item unlocks a quest cycle authored specifically for that item. Example: a Ring of Animal Friendship on the rack that unlocks "Storm on Menagerie Isle" when purchased. How unlocking works: the Quest Editor's existing requiredAsset gate (v1.548) on a cycle's cycleGate already handles item-based visibility — if the franchise owns the named item, the cycle un-obscures. Rack purchases drop the item into franchiseStorage, which is one of the three locations cycleGateStatus scans, so ownership and gate satisfaction align without new gate types or new state fields. Rack eligibility: an item is rack-eligible only if (a) it has qmRack:true set in the Quest Editor, AND (b) at least one cycle whose cycleGate.requiredAsset matches the item's name lives in a territory the franchise has unlocked. Items tied to locked-territory cycles don't surface until the territory is unlocked (via the v1.7006 building-purchase system), keeping the rack's offerings relevant to the player's current frontier. Rotation: three slots, seeded by todayStr() + the current set of unlocked territories. Stable for 24 hours within a stable unlocks set; re-seeds when a new territory unlocks so expansion feels rewarded immediately. Fewer than three eligible → empty-slot placeholders render alongside whatever is available. Zero eligible → the card still renders (if the franchise has any unlocked territories at all) with a "Expand your franchise into new territories" nudge, so the feature is discoverable from franchise start. Purchase flow: click a slot → confirm() dialog showing item name, QP cost, and the cycle that will unlock → qpSpend for the cost → push the item into franchiseStorage → adventure-log entry naming both item and unlocked cycle → call healCompletedCycleArchives() + renderCycleMapCards() so the new cycle surfaces and the rack slot flips to "Owned." Matches the v1.6910 real-estate purchase UX pattern. "Owned" state: after purchase the slot stays visible until next rotation but the buy button is replaced with a forest-green "✓ Owned" label. The ownership check reuses the same three-location scan as cycleGateStatus so the slot consistently reflects gate satisfaction. Regular-store exclusion: qmRack:true items are filtered out of every regular-store path — the Magic tab (both dev-mode and live-stock branches), the Adventuring tab (if type matches), the search-all branch. buildStock() also excludes them from the rotating magic inventory. New helpers: _qmRackPrng(seed) deterministic xorshift-based PRNG for daily rack shuffling (adequate for UI determinism, not cryptographic); _qmRackEligibleItems() filtered+sorted pool; _qmRackToday() returns today's three-slot array including nulls; _qmRackOwns(name) three-location ownership check; _qmRackBuy(name) purchase handler. Constants: QM_RACK_SIZE=3. Schema additions: two new columns on the items table — qm_rack boolean and qp_price integer. Existing rows default to false/0, so pre-v1.7012 items continue to live on the regular store unchanged. The Quest Editor item-save path was widened in parallel (QE v1.691) to emit both columns on every save; items edited in QE v1.690 or earlier will keep their defaults until re-saved. loadItemsFromDB reads row.qm_rack and row.qp_price, assigning to the live item's qmRack and qpPrice fields. Placement: rack card sits between the Grind Missions card and the territory-grouped completed-cycles section per design. Incomplete cycles remain above Grind. Authoring workflow: in Quest Editor, (1) author the magic item with Sell on Quest Map checked and a QP price set, (2) author the quest cycle with cycleGate.requiredAsset naming the exact item string and cycleGate.visibility:'obscured' so it's hidden until unlocked, (3) place the cycle in an unlocked territory. The rack wiring is automatic from there. Not addressed: no explicit "refresh the rack now for QP" action like the store's daily refresh — the rack auto-rotates at todayStr() rollover (roughly midnight local, per normalizeResetTime) which felt appropriate for a curated-feel rack rather than a churn-through-rolls economy. Could add a manual reroll in a future version if players want it. No change to the existing requiredAsset gate mechanism — the whole integration composes with the machinery already in place.
Version 1.7011 — Two UX improvements. (1) Quest Map: completed cycles collapse inside their territory panel. Each territory panel still renders open by default (the v1.7010 change), but the completed cycles inside it are now nested inside a closed-by-default "✓ Completed (N) · Tap to view" row. Rationale: a territory with a dozen completed cycles pushed the actionable incomplete cycles and Grind Missions card out of first-scroll view. Incomplete cycles still live above the Grind Missions card — this change only affects the already-finished stack. Per-cycle card bodies remain individually expandable via the existing cycle-toggle behaviour. Future consideration: if a territory panel ever contains a mix of active and completed cycles, the same nesting pattern could split them into "⚔ Active" and "✓ Completed" sub-panels; not needed today since incomplete cycles aren't territory-grouped. (2) Party screen: squad panel open/collapse state now persists across renders. Observed bug: user reported opening a squad panel, clicking "free" on a member to open the free-choice modal (v1.6912), picking Downtime, confirming the downtime job — and returning to the Party page with the squad panel collapsed. Reopening it to send another member on downtime felt repetitive. Root cause: squad panels were <details> elements with no explicit open attribute, so each renderParty() call wrote them back in their default (closed) state regardless of what the user had opened. An S.squadCollapsed field was present in the state schema (line 8645) and in the sbPull sanitizer, but nothing wrote to or read from it — it was dead state. Fix: (a) emit open on the <details> tag unless S.squadCollapsed[panelKey] is explicitly set — default behaviour is OPEN, matching the pre-v1.7011 UX for anyone who never touched a panel. (b) attach a toggle event listener to each panel that writes the inverse state to S.squadCollapsed and calls save(). Collapsing a panel sets the flag; expanding clears it. Panel key is sq.name for real squads and '__unassigned__::<territory>' for each per-territory unassigned bucket (the sq.name already used for those in the v1.7006 bucketing). Why a per-panel listener instead of delegated on #plist: the native toggle event does not bubble, so document-scoped delegation doesn't work; must be attached to each <details> individually. Attached right after el.innerHTML=html, scanning only the newly-rendered panels (no stale listeners).
