Result: expand a squad, send a member to downtime or grind, return to Party — the squad stays open. Send another member. And another. The panel only closes when the user explicitly taps the header. Supabase sync: save() schedules a debounced push on each change so multi-device franchises keep their expansion state in sync. Unchanged: territory zone wrappers (v1.7006) don't persist state — they're always open by default. The v1.7011 data-panel-key attribute is only on squad panels inside the territory zones, not the zones themselves. No SQL change. No data-model change (the S.squadCollapsed field was already present, just unused).
Version 1.7010 — Always render the 🗺 territory wrapper on Party screen and Quest Map, even when only one territory is represented. Observed regression: after v1.7009's merge-path fix corrected territory on the three custom cycles (so all five completed cycles resolved to "Minsaka"), the Quest Map stopped showing any territory panel at all — five cards appeared flat under "✓ Completed Cycles" with no 🗺 header. This was v1.7008's design ("skip the wrapper when only one territory is represented"), but user feedback was that the absence of the wrapper reads as a bug, not an ergonomic win. The collapsible territory zones were explicitly requested for "clarity's sake and organization," which applies just as much to a single-territory franchise as a multi-territory one. Fix: removed the _territoryCount≤1 short-circuit in renderCycleMapCards — every completed cycle now lives inside a 🗺 panel regardless of count. Also flipped the Party screen's _groupByTerritory gate to hard-true for symmetry, so the Party and Quest Map surfaces now both always surface the territory wrapper. Single-territory franchises see "🗺 Minsaka · N squads · M unassigned" on Party, and "🗺 Minsaka · N cycles" in the completed-cycles section of Quest Map, even at franchise start. Trade-off considered: adds one layer of visual nesting for new users with no territory progression yet, but the wrapper is collapsible (open by default), labels what the user is looking at, and sets up the mental model for when a second territory unlocks. Net win. No SQL change. No data-model change. Single-line gate flip in each of two render functions.
Version 1.7009 — Fix: territory field dropped from custom quest cycles during mergeCustomContent(). Observed bug: user's three custom cycles (Humdrum Arcanum, Rune Routers, Sanguine Caravan) were saved to Supabase with territory:"Minsaka" on the content_items row (verified via a localStorage dump showing territory:"Minsaka" intact on all three in CONTENT_LIB.shared.questSets), but all three rendered under the "Unspecified" panel in the Quest Map's v1.7008 grouping layout. Ditto for v1.7006's Party-screen territory zones and v1.7006's origin-territory seeding logic. Root cause: mergeCustomContent()'s loop over CONTENT_LIB merges each stored quest-set meta onto (or into) QUESTSETS via a hardcoded field whitelist. Both branches of the merge — the existing-set update path (line ~31276) and the new-set push path (line ~31287) — copy name, theme, dndTier, capstoneIdx, cycleGate, and questOrder, but not territory. v1.6922 added capstone/gate fields to this whitelist; v1.572 added territory to the Quest Editor save path but never threaded it through the merge. That oversight was benign until v1.7006 made set.territory load-bearing. Fix: both branches now also copy meta.territory. The existing-set path only overwrites when the DB row's value is truthy — a hardcoded Minsaka (v1.7007) on e.g. Drowned Temple isn't wiped by a pre-territory DB row. The new-set path defaults to '' when absent, matching the v1.7006 "unspecified territory is a legal state" contract. Why it didn't affect the two hardcoded cycles in the screenshot: Root & Branch and Rising Reputation come from BUILTIN_QUESTSETS (v1.7007 stamped Minsaka directly there), and the merge path's existing-set branch never overwrote territory at all pre-fix — so the built-in values survived. Custom cycles, created via the Quest Editor, never had a BUILTIN entry to preserve, so the merge's new-set branch was their only way in — and that path silently dropped the field. Verification on next load: open Quest Map. All five completed cycles should group under 🗺 Minsaka in a single panel. Raven & Remnant (the Gojimura cycle) stays in its own panel once completed. The fix is purely client-side — no SQL change, and the user's data in Supabase was already correct. Diagnostic note for future merge audits: mergeCustomContent uses explicit field whitelists rather than shallow-copying the whole meta. This is deliberate (drops internal flags like _editable, enforces schema shape) but means any newly-added cycle-level field needs to be threaded through both branches. Territory was the first such field added post-v1.572 that's also consumed by the render path; future franchise-scope additions should be added to this list explicitly.
Version 1.7008 — Quest Map completed cycles grouped by territory. User ask: as the franchise accumulates completed cycles across multiple territories, the flat "✓ Completed Cycles" list below the Grind Missions card becomes hard to scan — you have to read each card's header to identify its territory. With territories now a first-class organizing concept (v1.7006–v1.7007), the Quest Map should mirror the Party screen's territory-zone pattern. Fix: renderCycleMapCards now also buckets completed cycles into completedByTerritory (keyed by set.territory, with an "Unspecified" fallback bucket for cycles lacking a territory field). The emit path replaces the flat completedCards.join('') with per-territory collapsible <details> panels — each open by default (the Quest Map is already an opt-in modal so there's no reason to hide content at entry), with a header showing the 🗺 marker, the territory name, and the count of completed cycles in that bucket. Emit order: territories from S.unlockedTerritories render first in unlock order (origin → later unlocks), then any territories found in completed cycles but not in the unlocked list (defensive — e.g. a Supabase-imported cycle whose territory string never triggered a migration), then the "Unspecified" bucket last. Small-franchise ergonomics: when only one territory is represented across all completed cycles (the typical early-game state), the wrapper is skipped entirely and cards emit flat. Matches the Party screen's single-territory behaviour from v1.7006 — the grouping appears only when it adds information. Unchanged: incomplete cycles (the actionable section above the Grind Missions card) are NOT grouped, since they're generally already in a logical progression order via QUESTSETS. If an incomplete cycle sits in a different territory from its squad, that's surfaced elsewhere (cycle card body shows the 📍 territory pill already). Grind Missions card position unchanged. No SQL change. No data-model change. Reads set.territory which was stamped onto every built-in cycle in v1.7007 and was already being saved via the Quest Editor for any custom cycles. Render-only patch.
Version 1.7007 — Territory-stamp the four hardcoded quest cycles. User ask: with v1.7006's territory system enabled, the existing hardcoded cycles (Root & Branch, Rising Reputation, The Drowned Temple of Dicentis, Hasufan in the Eastern Juxians) had no territory field, so computeOriginTerritory() was falling back to empty string. On every existing test save, S.originTerritory was thus blank, the franchise started with no unlocked territories, and every squad/character got an empty-string homeTerritory — technically legal under v1.7006's grandfathering (empty terrs bucket together) but it meant the "🗺 Home Territory" picker never surfaced and per-territory headers never rendered. Fix: stamped territory:'Minsaka' onto all four cycle definitions in QUESTSETS. On first reload after this version, migrateToTerritoryV1_7006 picks up 'Minsaka' as the origin territory (QUESTSETS[0].territory), seeds S.originTerritory='Minsaka', and backfills every squad + char with homeTerritory:'Minsaka'. The migration is idempotent against already-migrated-to-empty-string states too — chars/squads whose homeTerritory is '' are re-backfilled since the check is !c.homeTerritory, which treats empty-string as missing. Editorial note on Hasufan: the fourth cycle explicitly takes place in "the icy Eastern Juxian mountains" — geographically not Minsaka. I stamped it Minsaka anyway to keep existing save data legal and match your immediate ask, but if you want to use it as the first "push into a new territory" test case, change its territory field to e.g. 'Eastern Juxians' in this file (line ~7689) or override via the Quest Editor. Changing it after a player's franchise has already started Hasufan would require handling the case where a squad is currently assigned to an out-of-territory cycle; that's exactly the case v1.7007's deferred cross-territory mobilization cost is meant to surface. No code change beyond the four single-line additions. No SQL change. Existing Supabase quest sets that were loaded from DB (mergeCustomContent) are unaffected — their territory field comes from whatever the author saved in the Quest Editor. Only the built-in hardcoded four get this treatment.
Version 1.7006 — Territory progression system, part 1. User ask: gate squad expansion behind franchise investment in new territories. Start each franchise in its origin territory (Minsaka for most test runs) with a cap of 4 chartered squads + 10 unassigned characters per territory; unlock each new territory by buying a building of 1000+ gp in a completed cycle that lives there. On the Party screen, group squads under collapsible "🗺 Territory" zones so players can see at a glance which squads and unassigned characters live where. What landed in this version: (1) Data model. Three new constants (SQUADS_PER_TERRITORY_MAX=4, UNASSIGNED_PER_TERRITORY_MAX=10, TERRITORY_UNLOCK_BUILDING_MIN_GP=1000), two new state fields (S.originTerritory, S.unlockedTerritories[]), one new field on each squad (sq.homeTerritory), one new field on each character (c.homeTerritory). Territory strings come straight from QUESTSETS[i].territory so the same freetext players already see in the Quest Editor drives both cycle territory labels and franchise base identity. (2) Migration. New migrateToTerritoryV1_7006() seeds S.originTerritory from the first QUESTSETS entry with a non-empty territory (auto-unlocks at no cost so existing saves remain legal), backfills homeTerritory on every existing squad and character, and rescans partyAssets for any previously-purchased 1000+ gp cycle-owned building so retro-unlocks fire automatically. Wired into all three load paths (load(), the code-login post-pull block, and the main sbPull). Idempotent. Over-cap states from pre-v1.7006 (e.g. a Minsaka unassigned bucket holding 15 characters) are grandfathered — the cap prevents NEW additions beyond 10 but never forcibly evicts anyone. (3) Territory helpers. A new cohesive block of ~10 functions sitting just above _unassignedVirtualSquad(): territoryForCycleSetId, computeOriginTerritory, territoriesUnlockedByBuildings, recomputeUnlockedTerritories (monotonic — sold buildings don't retroactively re-lock a territory), isTerritoryUnlocked, charHomeTerritory (follows the precedence c.homeTerritory → squad's homeTerritory → origin), squadCountInTerritory, unassignedCountInTerritory, territoriesWithSquadCapacity. (4) Unlock detection. confirmRealEstatePurchase() now calls recomputeUnlockedTerritories() after the building is pushed to partyAssets and logs a 🗺 adventure-log entry naming the newly-unlocked territory. Triggered by buildings with price≥1000 whose owning cycle lives in a yet-unlocked territory. Smaller buildings (Storage Shed at 200, Stables at 500, Guard Post at 750) don't trigger an unlock — the Barracks at 1000 gp is the minimum entry point, with Smithy/Tavern/Library all qualifying at higher tiers. (5) Squad creation gate. openSquadNew() now refuses to open the modal when every unlocked territory is at SQUADS_PER_TERRITORY_MAX/SQUADS_PER_TERRITORY_MAX squads, surfacing an alert that spells out exactly what needs to happen (buy a 1000+ gp building in a new territory via Quest Map → 🏘 Real Estate). When at least one territory has open capacity, the identity card of the creation modal grows a new 🗺 Home Territory picker populated from territoriesWithSquadCapacity(); hidden entirely when only one territory has open slots so small franchises never see a zero-choice dropdown. (6) Squad creation persistence. sqSave reads the picker value (falling back to the first-open territory or origin), enforces the cap server-side one more time (belt-and-braces against stale picker races), and stores it on the new squad as sq.homeTerritory. Members whose previous territory was empty or matched origin inherit the new squad's territory for free; members with a pre-existing non-origin territory keep theirs (the v1.7007 relocate flow will be the explicit QP-costed path to move those). (7) sqmgrAssignChar sync. Characters joining a squad via the Squad Manager now inherit that squad's homeTerritory onto c.homeTerritory. This silently bypasses the cross-territory relocation cost — by design for this version, since the relocation cost UI doesn't exist yet (it ships in v1.7007). (8) Party-screen territory zones. renderParty's squad-panel loop now routes each panel into a territory-keyed bucket and, when 2+ territories are unlocked, wraps each bucket in a "🗺 Territory" <details> with a header showing live counts (N/4 squads · M unassigned). Single-territory franchises see the flat pre-v1.7006 layout. The Unassigned virtual panel was split from a single global bucket into one virtual panel per territory; each one's header shows its per-territory cap (N / 10), and over-cap panels render the fraction in crimson as a visible hint. (9) Idempotent character backfill. Top of renderParty() now runs a cheap per-render loop that sets c.homeTerritory on any character still missing the field — catches chars created by any of the six S.party.push(…) sites (standard hire, mercenary, capstone NPC, contact reward, etc.) without needing to touch each one this turn. After first render per char, the pass no-ops. What's deferred to v1.7007: (a) The Relocate Squad modal (from the squad edit screen) that will let a player move an existing squad to a different unlocked territory, charging costTerritoryRelocate QP per member (default 10 = reusing costSquadSwap's value, or a new knob if the designer wants it tuned separately). (b) The cross-territory cycle mobilization soft penalty — when a player activates a cycle whose territory differs from the assigned squad's, spend costCycleMobilize QP per member (default 5, half of permanent relocation). (c) Hard enforcement of the unassigned bucket cap at hire time (currently display-only; over-cap states are grandfathered). (d) QP Manager UI fields for the two new cost knobs. No SQL change. No data-model change beyond the additive fields listed in (1). Every new field defaults safely when absent, so rolling back to v1.7005 is non-destructive.
Version 1.7005 — Asset-panel index-corruption fix (latent since v1.501 / v1.6921). Observed bug: clicking the "Give" button to assign a capstone-reward artifact (e.g. "Disguise Hat of the Domini") to a character assigned a different asset entirely — a squad-owned Bag of Holding the first time, a Rope of Climbing the second time. The target artifact stayed in partyAssets unassigned; unrelated assets were spliced out and dropped into the character's personal inventory. Root cause: the Purchased Assets render block filters S.partyAssets to exclude squad-owned (v1.501) and cycle-owned (v1.6921) entries, then calls .map(function(a,ai){...}) on the filtered list and emits data-ai="ai" on every action button. The asset-save, asset-give, and asset-sell click handlers read that number and looked it up in the UNFILTERED S.partyAssets array. With any squad- or cycle-owned asset present in partyAssets, the filtered index and the unfiltered index diverged — so clicking the third visible row could mutate the third absolute row, which is some completely different asset. In the reported case, the squad-owned Carpetbag sat at index 0 of the unfiltered array; clicking Give on any visible artifact resolved to S.partyAssets[0] and moved the Carpetbag to Hess's inv. Subsequent click resolved to S.partyAssets[0] again (now Rope of Climbing after the Carpetbag's splice reshuffled everything). Latent since v1.501 added the first filter in April; only surfaced now because users rarely have both squad-owned assets and unassigned artifacts at the same time. Fix: identify the target asset by meta.uid (stable, unique, always set by assetMeta() on first access) instead of by array index. Render emits data-uid="asset-uid" on every action element (.asset-name, .asset-owner, .asset-char, and the three action buttons). Handlers resolve via S.partyAssets.filter(x=>x.meta&&x.meta.uid===uid)[0] for save (no splice needed), and via findIndex for give/sell (which need the index for splice). Escapes the uid through esc() at emission to handle any exotic character in generated uids safely. Three handlers fixed: asset-save (animal ownership editor — was at risk of silently misrouting pet/mount assignments), asset-give (the reported bug), asset-sell (gold-loss risk — could sell a valuable squad-owned asset when user clicked Sell on something else). Healing note for affected users: this fix prevents future misassignments but does NOT auto-undo the damage already done. If your Hess character ended up with a Carpetbag of Holding or Rope of Climbing from clicking Give earlier, open the character modal Gear tab and use the existing Move to Storage / Sell flows to return those items; then re-click Give on the actual artifact (now resolved correctly) to assign it properly. The partyAssets side of the corruption is probably fine — the incorrectly-spliced assets are just in the wrong inventory now, not lost. Related investigation (not in this patch, flagged for future): migrateMisroutedAssets and dedupePartyAssets both iterate S.partyAssets by numeric index with splice mutation; a scan confirmed they collect indices first and splice in reverse, which is the correct pattern — but given the fragility proven by this bug, a future version might want to migrate those routines to uid-keyed lookups as well for resilience. No SQL change, no data-model change.
Version 1.7004 — Sub-nav bar with envelope/inbox notifications. User ask: add a sub-nav at the top of all pages hosting an envelope button that shows a red dot when a character returns from a downtime mission or a quest/grind; reserve the sub-nav for future system-message indicators (QP offers, event announcements). What landed: (1) Fixed #subnav bar pinned directly below #hdr, right-aligned icon-button layout, same 480px max-width as the rest of the chrome. Currently hosts one button (#btn-inbox) with an envelope glyph and an absolutely-positioned .subnav-dot (red, soft-pulse animation, bordered with the ink background color so it reads crisply against the gold-bordered button). Button responds to hover/focus-visible and gets a tighter min-height/font-size under body.compact-hdr. Hidden alongside #nav when body.kbd-open is set so on-screen keyboards don't trap it. (2) CSS custom properties for chrome heights. New --hdr-h (62px default, 48px compact) and --sub-h (36px default, 32px compact) vars defined on :root and overridden on body.compact-hdr. The sub-nav's top consumes --hdr-h so if the header height ever changes these two values stay in lock-step. #app's padding-top changed from the hardcoded 86px to calc(var(--hdr-h) + var(--sub-h) + 14px) so screen content automatically clears both fixed bars in both compact and standard modes with a uniform 14px breathing gap. Added to BOTH CSS duplicate blocks in the codebase (the style sheet is maintained twice — every #hdr/#nav rule has a twin). (3) Inbox modal (#ov-inbox). Standard .ov + .modal chrome. Lists every pending item returned by _collectInboxItems(), one row each with icon (📜 quest / ⚒ grind / 🕯 downtime), Cinzel title, subtitle, and a right-side chevron hinting at the tap-to-navigate action. Empty state ("No pending notifications.") shows when nothing's pending. Click and Enter/Space on any row routes to the resolution surface and closes the modal. Standard ✕ Close button at the bottom. (4) _collectInboxItems(). Single authoritative source for "what's in the inbox right now." Sums three signals: (a) qs.pending entries with doneAt<=Date.now() (completed quests waiting to be collected), (b) qs.grindPending entries with doneAt<=Date.now() (completed grind missions), (c) S.pendingDowntimeRolls length (downtime completions awaiting a d20 — same queue surfaced by v1.6904's ribbon). Each item carries {kind, icon, title, sub, onClick}. Per design decision, quest & grind onClicks close the modal and call navTo('adventure') — users land on the Quest Map / screen in full context rather than having rewards applied silently from a list. Downtime onClicks open the existing openDowntimePendingModal(), reusing v1.6904's d20 flow with no duplicate logic. (5) renderInboxBadge(). Queries the collector, toggles #inbox-dot's display, and updates the envelope button's aria-label and title attributes to reflect the live count (e.g. "Inbox (3 new)"). Called alongside renderDowntimeRibbon() at every one of its five existing call sites (post-downtime-completion render, roll resolution, boot from cloud, boot offline, and the per-downtime-job completion path), plus at the tail of collectQuest, autoCollectExpired, the Fast-Forward batch-collect handler, and the per-grind collect branch, so collecting an item clears the dot on the next frame without waiting for the poll. (6) Polling. A 30-second setInterval owned by the inbox-wiring IIFE catches doneAt crossings that happen in the background — a quest goes from "in progress" to "ready" at minute 47 of a 30-minute session, and the dot lights without requiring the user to touch the UI. Guarded by window._inboxPollId so hot-reloads or re-runs of the IIFE don't stack intervals. Cheap on every tick: just length checks on three arrays. (7) Wiring follows the v1.6904 pattern. Self-attaching IIFE that either waits for DOMContentLoaded or runs immediately, all listeners guarded by _wired flags, initial renderInboxBadge() call so reload surfaces any pre-existing ready items. List row clicks delegated through a single list-level listener keyed by data-ix and re-collecting at click time so late-arriving items can't be clicked against a stale index. Deliberately not added: (a) a count badge (the spec asked for a dot, not a number — a numeric count reads as more urgent than a simple presence indicator and the count is already surfaced in the modal's list itself). (b) Per-kind dots (the spec asked for one dot covering all signals). (c) Read/unread state (the dot just mirrors "is anything pending right now"; collecting clears it). Future expansion: _collectInboxItems() is the single insertion point for new notification categories — system messages, QP-offer alerts, event announcements — each appended as {kind, icon, title, sub, onClick} picks up badge lighting + modal rendering automatically. No SQL change. No data-model change. Badge state is derived entirely from existing state fields (qs.pending, qs.grindPending, S.pendingDowntimeRolls) — nothing to persist, nothing to sync, nothing to migrate.
Version 1.7003 — Kudos sidecar now matches the pinned-section's height. User report: with many recent completions and few pinned tasks, the kudos sidecar rendered much taller than the pinned section next to it — extending past where the main task cards begin and visually dominating the zone. Root cause: .pinned-band uses display:grid with align-items:start, which lets each grid child take its natural content height. The pinned-section renders as tall as its pin rows; the sidecar renders as tall as its kudos rows (capped at 15 per sub-column); whichever is taller drove the visual stacking order, and kudos dominated whenever the user had more than 5 or so recent completions. Fix: new _syncKudosSidecarHeight() helper measures .pinned-section.offsetHeight post-render and applies it as inline maxHeight on the sibling .kudos-sidecar. CSS rule .pinned-band .kudos-sidecar{overflow:hidden;min-height:0;} clips overflow cleanly so the sidecar never exceeds the pinned height. Reactive to later changes: a ResizeObserver on the pinned section watches for size changes after the initial render — catches pin/unpin without a full renderTasks(), font loads, accessibility text-size bumps, and viewport reflow across the 1024px breakpoint. The observer is torn down and reattached on every renderTasks() because #tlist.innerHTML is replaced wholesale, making the previously-watched DOM node stale. Scheduling: helper is called via requestAnimationFrame at the end of renderTasks() so layout has settled before we measure. Self-gating: no-op if .pinned-band isn't present (1-col mode, no kudos data, or pinned-span equals column count). Access to clipped entries: older kudos remain fully available via the existing ⭐ Kudos header button, which opens the paginated full-list modal — clipping in the sidecar is purely visual. No SQL change, no data-model change.
Version 1.7002 — Contracts screen search. User ask: a search function on the Contracts page, placed beneath the daily task budget and above (or to the left of) Undo, that shows the top five results by task/sub-task text and — on selection — scrolls the target into view with a flash animation. What landed: (1) UI shell. A new #contract-search-wrap row with a 🔍 icon, #contract-search-input (full-width, "Search contracts…" placeholder), an inline ✕ clear button (#contract-search-clear, hidden until the field has content), and a dropdown panel #contract-search-results that absolute-positions below the input with max-height:320px and a gold-toned box-shadow. Sits between the task-budget readout and the right-aligned action row (Undo / Archive / Kudos) on every viewport. (2) Search logic (_runContractSearch). Walks S.active once, collecting task-name matches and sub-task text matches in a single pass. Three-tier ranking: tier 0 = haystack starts with the query (exact-prefix), tier 1 = query appears at a word boundary, tier 2 = query appears anywhere as a substring. Ties broken by (a) position in S.active (earlier first — respects user's manual ordering), (b) sub-tasks after their parent's own name match. Top 5 returned. Skips the legacy __proj_meta__ sentinel rows and the __meta sub-task sentinel. The query is regex-escaped before being used in the word-boundary test so punctuation in the search string can't blow up the match. (3) Result rendering (_renderContractSearchResults). Each row shows a Cinzel label ("Contract" or "Sub-task"), the match text with every case-insensitive occurrence of the query wrapped in <mark>, and for sub-task results a parent-context line ("in: Parent Task Name" or "in: Parent Task Name · collapsed" if the parent is collapsed). Active row highlights via .csr-row-active (same gold-toned tint as hover). Empty result set shows "No matching contracts or sub-tasks." The highlight path HTML-escapes first and then inserts the <mark> wrappers, so user text with angle brackets or quotes can't break the rendered dropdown. (4) Navigation (_navigateToContractResult). On selection: if the target is a sub-task inside a collapsed parent, the parent's collapsed flag is set to false, save() is called, and renderTasks() fires — so the sub-row is in the DOM by the time we go to scroll to it. Target lookup uses querySelectorAll + filter rather than a single selector, with a CSS.escape'd attribute selector scoped to #tlist, and prefers elements that are NOT inside .pinned-section (the main-list copy of a pinned task wins over the Pinned Spotlight copy — the Spotlight is a re-render of the same source and scrolling to it would land the user at the top of the screen instead of the actual position of the task in the list). Then: scrollIntoView({behavior:'smooth',block:'center'}), followed 180ms later by the flash animation — that delay lets the smooth-scroll begin so the pulse reads as "arrival" rather than "something flashed and then the page moved". The .task-search-flash class is removed-then-re-added with an intervening void el.offsetWidth; reflow so consecutive clicks on the same result re-trigger the animation cleanly. Self-cleans on animationend. (5) Flash animation. 1.6s gold pulse via @keyframes task-search-flash that rings the target with a 4-6px box-shadow and tints its background rgba(201,146,42,.18) at peak, fading back to transparent. Applies to either .card (task) or .strow (sub-row) — same keyframe, same class, works for both because both elements accept box-shadow. (6) Event wiring (bindContractSearch). Input event is debounced at 120ms so fast typing doesn't thrash the search loop. Focus with existing query re-opens the dropdown (natural for "I typed something, clicked away, now want to see results again"). Keyboard: ArrowDown/Up cycle through results with active-row scrolling into the dropdown's own overflow, Enter picks the active result, Escape clears and blurs. Click on any .csr-row picks that result. Click anywhere outside #contract-search-wrap closes the dropdown. The ✕ clear button empties the input, hides the dropdown, and refocuses the input for immediate re-entry. Idempotent via dataset.bound sentinel so repeat boot calls (e.g. post-auth refresh) don't stack listeners. (7) Boot wiring. Called from both boot paths alongside bindContractsColumnsToggle() — the main applySettings site and the test-mode path. Scope boundaries: (i) Searches only task names and sub-task text — not task notes/desc, not archived tasks, not sub-task completion state. Notes/desc inclusion is a reasonable future extension if users ask, but would need a UI cue that the match was in the hidden note rather than the visible title. (ii) Archived tasks have their own screen with its own controls; mixing them in would confuse the "I know this is in my active list" mental model. (iii) Split-group members are treated as regular tasks — they each have a unique _tid, and the DOM navigation targets #tlist .card[data-tid=...] which matches whether the card sits inside a .split-group wrapper or not. (iv) Pinned duplicates: the same task/sub-task can exist twice in the DOM (once in .pinned-section, once in the main list). The navigator picks the main-list copy so scrolling goes to the card's real position. (v) Debounce and animation timers are stored in the module-scoped _rqSearchState and survive across renders — re-rendering #tlist doesn't drop any in-flight search because the input element itself lives outside #tlist. No SQL change. No data-model change. No persistence. Search state is transient per-session; the input is always empty on page load.
Version 1.7001 — Contracts column layout now survives refresh. User report: on desktop, after setting the Contracts screen to 2 or 3 columns and arranging tasks across them, hitting refresh caused every task to collapse into column 1 (leftmost) — the multi-col layout was preserved but the task distribution was gone. Diagnosis: on refresh, localStorage renders correctly for about a second (correct _col values), then sbPull completes and rebuilds S.active from Supabase rows. The rebuild at line ~26463 read _splitGroup from the split_group column but never read _col from anywhere — because the matching push at line ~25957 never sent it. Every task came back with _col=undefined; _normalizeTaskCols() (run via ensureTaskIds() on the next render) clamped each undefined to 0; all cards landed in column 0. Same class of round-trip-strip bug as v1.6995's bagOfHolding/effect flags and v1.6930's split_group/pinned/splitGroupMeta. The v1.6982 changelog's claim that _col "rides inside existing active_tasks JSON blob — no SQL change" was never actually implemented — the data-model docstring was written but the push/pull plumbing was skipped. Fix: piggyback _col on the existing __meta sentinel subtask, which already carries _tid, note, and desc through the subtasks JSONB column. One-line addition to the push packer: _col:(typeof t._col==='number'?t._col:0) appended to the __meta object. One-line addition to the pull rebuilder: _col:(typeof meta._col==='number'?meta._col:0) restored onto the reconstructed task. Back-compat: rows pushed before this version have no meta._col — fallback returns 0, same behaviour as today's bug. First save+push from this version upgrades each task; first pull after that restores the stored columns. No SQL change. No schema change. No data-model change beyond the existing __meta pattern. Deliberately NOT changed: the code-session path (login_codes.player_data blob save at line ~25847) already serialized S.active in full via the _keys array, so _col survived that path naturally for class-code students — no code-session fix required. Only the Supabase active_tasks table path needed the repair. Also intentionally not added: a dedicated _col column on active_tasks. The precedent from v1.6995 was: additional per-task metadata piggybacks on the existing __meta channel; new columns (like split_group in v1.6930) are reserved for data that's queried directly. _col is never filtered on server-side — only read client-side during render — so the JSON-blob approach fits. Lesson: whenever a feature adds a per-task field with the comment "rides inside existing JSON, no SQL change", the push and pull paths must be explicitly updated at the same commit; writing the data-model docstring alone leaves a field that appears to persist (survives save() to localStorage) but silently resets on every Supabase round-trip. Third occurrence of this pattern (pct v1.543, bagOfHolding/effect v1.6995, _col v1.7001) — worth a future grep audit of every _tid, _splitGroup, _col-style underscore-prefixed field against both sbPush paths to catch any other silent-strip cases.
Version 1.7000 — Quest modal compact mode for long quest titles on mobile. User report: on a narrow phone viewport, quests whose titles wrap to two lines in the details summary (e.g. "Okaeri (Welcome Home)" with the flex:1 title span squeezed by the "▾ details" chevron) pushed the SOLARIANS MEMBERS character cards below the visible area. The modal is locked at max-height:92vh with .quest-modal-scroll at overflow-y:hidden, so the sticky footer stayed glued but the character cards between got clipped. Fix: new _adjustQuestModalCompact() helper measures the modal post-layout and toggles a .quest-compact class on .quest-modal based on two independent signals — (1) title rendered on 2+ lines (computed via boundingRect.height / line-height ≥ 1.6); (2) scroll viewport is overflowing (scrollHeight > clientHeight + 2). Either signal engages compact mode. The compact CSS block tightens ~13 rules: title font-size down to .9em with line-height 1.18, details summary padding reduced, peg-slot cells (.nonchar-hole and .qhole) go from min-height:52px to 40–42px with reduced padding and gaps, labels shrink from .72–.75em to .66–.68em, the peg-slot grid gap and page padding halve, and character-peg cards (.qpeg) drop from 72px to 58px min-height. Self-gating: helper bails when modal.offsetHeight === 0, so it's safe to call from any state-change site without stale measurements. Scheduling: called via requestAnimationFrame at the top of updateQuestModal() — rAF fires after the current sync frame (including any openOv('ov-quest') that follows the update call), so the modal is visible and laid out by the time the measurement runs. Placed at the top rather than end so every early-return path (grind branch, no-quest branch) still schedules the check. Rotation: a window resize listener re-runs the check when the modal is open. Fallback safety net: the second trigger (scroll overflow) catches cases beyond title-wrap — expanded unmet-warnings rows, accessibility font-size bumps via --fs, many peg slots on a single carousel page. If content fits naturally, no compact class is applied and the modal renders at normal density. No JS behavior change, no data-model change, no SQL.
Version 1.6999 — Drag handle fix extended to the second class. User report: after v1.6998 shipped, drag handles (⋮⋮) still visible on mobile — specifically on split-group headers and task cards. Root cause: there are TWO separate draghandle CSS classes in the codebase: .strow-draghandle (sub-rows) and .card-draghandle (split-group headers + task cards). v1.6998 added a global .strow-draghandle{display:none} rule outside the @media (min-width:1024px) block but only for the sub-row class — I failed to notice that .card-draghandle had the identical bug (base display:none trapped inside the desktop-only media query, default display:inline falling through on mobile). Two handles, same mistake, half the fix. This version adds the second global rule: .card-draghandle{display:none} applied outside the media query in both CSS duplicate blocks. The existing desktop-multi-col rules #tlist.cols-2 .card-draghandle{display:flex} and #tlist.cols-3 .card-draghandle{display:flex} continue to override it when appropriate. Net effect: all four handle types (sub-row and card, times the two cols-2/cols-3 variants) hidden on mobile, functional on desktop-multi-col. No JS change. No behavior change outside the added CSS. Apologies for the two-step shipping on what should have been one patch.
Version 1.6998 — Two mobile display bugs. User report: (1) drag handles (⋮⋮) visible as inline text on the left of every pinned row on mobile — non-functional since drag requires a mouse, just visual noise. (2) Kudos sidecar rendering inline with the pinned section on mobile, breaking the narrow layout. Both triggered by the same root pattern: a stored contractsColumns setting of 2 or 3 (from a previous desktop session) was carrying over to mobile state checks that should be viewport-aware, not setting-aware. Fix 1 — drag handle. The base CSS rule .strow-draghandle{display:none} was defined INSIDE the @media (min-width:1024px) block, so it only applied on desktop. On mobile the element had no CSS display rule at all, falling back to browser default display:inline — the ⋮⋮ characters rendered as plain text. Added a new global .strow-draghandle{display:none;} rule outside the media query (applied twice, once for each of the two CSS duplicate blocks the codebase maintains). The desktop-multi-col rules #tlist.cols-2 .strow-draghandle{display:flex} and #tlist.cols-3 .strow-draghandle{display:flex} continue to override it inside the media query. Net effect: handle hidden on every viewport except desktop-multi-col where it's functional. Fix 2 — kudos sidecar. The pinned/kudos render block at renderTasks (~line 11314) was reading _cn = _colsN(), which returns the stored contractsColumns preference regardless of viewport. If a user had 2 or 3 saved on desktop, the mobile render would see _cn = 2, satisfy the _cn >= 2 kudos render guard, and attempt the side-by-side grid — clipping awkwardly into mobile width. Changed to _cn = _effectiveCols(), which the codebase already provides for exactly this purpose: returns 1 below 1024px viewport regardless of stored setting. Kudos sidecar now correctly hides on mobile AND respects the stored setting on desktop. The ⭐ Kudos button in the Contracts header bar is still available on mobile for accessing the full kudos modal. No SQL change. No data-model change. No JS behavior change outside the one-word replacement of _colsN → _effectiveCols in the kudos render decision.
Version 1.6997 — QP Voucher system: admins generate, players redeem. User ask: create a system similar to the class-code generator that produces vouchers redeemable in-app for QP quantities; live on the QP Manager screen. ⚠ SQL REQUIRED: this version adds a new qp_vouchers table to Supabase. Run v1_6997_qp_vouchers.sql BEFORE first use, otherwise voucher generation fails ("relation does not exist") and redemption silently returns "not found". The migration is idempotent (IF NOT EXISTS guards throughout). What landed: (1) Tab bar at top of QP Manager modal splits the screen into two tabs: ⚙ Settings (all existing controls, unchanged) and 🎟 Vouchers (new). Opening the manager always starts on Settings so the prior tab state doesn't surprise anyone. (2) Redemption (visible to all authenticated users). Input field accepts a 12-char voucher code in XXXX-XXXX-XXXX format, auto-formatting hyphens as you type, case-insensitive, Enter key submits. On successful claim: QP credited immediately and a confirmation appears (✓ Redeemed N QP!). Uses the new qpCreditDirect() path which bypasses the daily earn cap — vouchers are out-of-band gifts, not "earnings". Ledger entry logged as voucher_redeem with the voucher label as the note; a qp_transactions row is synced to Supabase with event_key=voucher::<id> for idempotency. (3) Generation (admin-only, gated by isAdmin()). Amount input (1–9999) + optional label (80 chars max), press Generate → voucher row is inserted into Supabase with a freshly-minted code → code displayed with a Copy-to-clipboard button. Label helps the admin remember who each voucher was for ("Participation reward for Maya", "Homework bonus #3", etc.). (4) History (admin-only). Table of the admin's generated vouchers (created_by = uid()), newest-first, showing code, QP amount, label, and status. Unredeemed vouchers show their code in gold with Copy + Delete buttons. Redeemed vouchers show strikethrough code, the first 8 chars of the redeemer's UUID, and the redemption date (no Copy/Delete — the voucher is spent). ↻ Refresh button reloads from Supabase. (5) Code format: 12 chars, not 8. Class codes stay at 8 chars (XXXX-XXXX) for backward compat. Vouchers use 12 chars (XXXX-XXXX-XXXX) from the same 32-char ambiguity-safe alphabet (no 0/O, 1/I/l), yielding ~1.2 × 10^18 key space — brute-force-resistant even without rate limiting. At a realistic attacker's 10,000 requests/sec, the expected time to hit any single valid voucher in a set of 1000 is millions of years. (6) Client-side rate limit. 5 failed redemption attempts triggers a 30-second cooldown on that user's browser (keyed per-uid in localStorage so a fresh tab doesn't bypass). Successful redemption resets the counter. The message shows remaining seconds. A dedicated attacker on a fresh session can still try 5 per 30 seconds = 600/hour per browser fingerprint, which combined with the 12-char key space makes brute-force impractical for the classroom threat model. (7) Race-safe claim. The PATCH that marks a voucher redeemed includes &redeemed_by=is.null as part of the filter — if two users race to redeem the same code, only the first PATCH succeeds; the second returns an empty array and the UI shows "already been redeemed". Not bulletproof (a real atomic would need a DB function) but covers the realistic case. (8) New helpers: generateVoucherCode() (3×4 char format), qpCreditDirect() (cap-bypassing QP credit), qpvRlState/qpvRlRegisterFail/qpvRlClear/qpvRlMsRemaining (rate-limit plumbing), qpvRenderHistory (admin list). (9) Data model. New Supabase table qp_vouchers with columns id, code (unique), amount (int 1-9999), label, created_by, created_at, redeemed_by (nullable), redeemed_at (nullable). RLS policies mirror login_codes: wide-open authenticated SELECT/UPDATE, auth'd INSERT requires created_by = auth.uid(), DELETE restricted to created_by = auth.uid(). Admin-only status of the generate/delete buttons is enforced client-side via isAdmin(). Not included: expiry (you picked "no expiry" — easy to add later as an expires_at column plus a redemption-time check). Server-side attempt logging. Per-voucher max-attempts counter. These are all possible v1.6998+ extensions if abuse ever shows up. Test plan: (a) Run the SQL. (b) Open QP Manager → Vouchers tab → generate a voucher with a memorable label and amount. (c) Copy the code. (d) Paste into the Redeem input and click Redeem. Observe the balance increase, the history table update (strikethrough + redeemer UUID prefix), and the ledger entry on the Settings tab.
Version 1.6996 — Kudos now persists to Supabase. User ask: confirm whether the v1.6991 Kudos log actually syncs to cloud. Answer: it didn't. The v1.6991 changelog said "rides inside the existing S JSON blob — no SQL change" but the S blob doesn't ride to Supabase as a blob — specific columns do. S.kudos was being saved to localStorage only and got wiped on any cross-device / clear-cache / fresh-login scenario. ⚠ SQL REQUIRED: this version adds a new kudos jsonb column to the franchise table. Run v1_6996_kudos_column.sql in the Supabase SQL editor BEFORE loading this version, otherwise the next franchise push will fail with a "column does not exist" error and your save won't sync until the column is added. The SQL file ships alongside the HTML build. It's idempotent (ALTER ... IF NOT EXISTS) so running it twice is safe. What landed: (1) createDefaultState now initializes kudos:[] explicitly (previously the array was created lazily on first _pushKudos call). (2) sbPush franchise row includes kudos:S.kudos||[] alongside split_group_meta and pinned — same treatment as other array-on-franchise fields. (3) sbPull franchise row restores S.kudos = Array.isArray(fr.kudos) ? fr.kudos : (S.kudos||[]). The fallback is important: if a user loads this version BEFORE running the SQL, fr.kudos is undefined (the column doesn't exist yet) and we'd otherwise wipe any kudos that were hanging out in localStorage from v1.6991-6995. The fallback preserves them. Once the SQL is run AND a subsequent save pushes the kudos to the new column, the column becomes the source of truth. Edge case: if push fails (column missing), S.kudos keeps accumulating in localStorage and will be correctly pushed to Supabase as soon as the SQL is run. No data is lost during the migration window. Shape unchanged from v1.6991: each entry is still {id, ts, name, kind:'task'|'sub', gp, parent, groupName}, cap still 1000, display still 200 per page. This change is purely the persistence plumbing.
Version 1.6995 — Bag of Holding: three fixes and a dedupe migration. Observed from v1.6994 diagnostic trace: user's console showed (a) four Longinus Carpetbag of Holding entries in partyAssets — duplicates accumulated from push/pull races; (b) every single hasSquadFormationEffect() call on those bags returned NO MATCH, meaning the recognition flags had been stripped on a previous round-trip; (c) franchiseHasBagOfHolding() returned false even though four bags existed, so the 50 gp re-registration path would have been unavailable; (d) every asset in partyAssets appeared exactly twice, including Riding Horses, Cats, and Ropes — systematic duplication, not a single-bag issue. Fix #1: sbPull reconciliation ordering (the main bug). Bag ownerRef validation was running BEFORE S.squads got populated from the pull response. _sqNames was always empty; every squad-assigned bag's ownerRef failed validation; every bag got ownerType cleared on every sync. The bag would correctly save as assigned but on the very next pull the assignment would be wiped. Moved the reconciliation block to AFTER the squads-load block, where S.squads is actually populated. Fix #2: push/pull flag preservation. The Supabase party_assets push path writes only {name, ic, type} — top-level bagOfHolding and effect fields get stripped. Enhanced serializeAssetType to pack recognition flags into the meta object under reserved keys _boh (bagOfHolding) and _fx (effect), which travel via the existing type column encoding (basetype__meta__{json}). Enhanced the pull-side rebuild at sbPull to unpack _boh and _fx back to top-level fields. Reserved key names starting with underscore don't collide with any existing meta fields. Backwards compatible: legacy rows without the reserved keys just rebuild without flags (same as before), then Fix #1 + the existing BUILTIN_GEAR fallback in hasSquadFormationEffect continue to catch them. Any save+push from this version forward upgrades the persisted data. No SQL change. Fix #3: dedupe migration. New dedupePartyAssets() helper keys entries by (name, baseType, ownerType, ownerRef) — the tuple that uniquely identifies an "owned instance" of an asset. Duplicates are collapsed, preferring entries with _sbId (Supabase-persisted) over entries without. Runs automatically: (a) after migrateMisroutedAssets in sbPull, (b) at the end of load() so a locally-saved state with duplicates also heals on boot. Logs an adventure-log entry "🧹 Cleaned N duplicate party asset(s)" when it removes anything, and a RQ_DIAG entry with the full list of what was removed. Safe to call repeatedly — idempotent on already-clean state. Intentionally NOT deduped: franchiseStorage string entries (a user can legitimately own two Crowbars), character inv arrays (same reason), or any other array. Only partyAssets has the uniqueness-by-owner-tuple property. Side fix: the old log message "ownerRef '...' no longer matches a squad" was reading m.ownerRef AFTER clearing it, so the string always printed as "ownerRef '' no longer matches". Fixed to capture the stale ref before clearing. Diagnostic cleanup: removed the noisy per-call RQ_DIAG logging inside hasSquadFormationEffect (was firing hundreds of times per render). The rest of the RQ_DIAG module (log, snapshot, dump, clear) stays in place and still instruments btn-sq-enroll, sqSave, applyDBItemsToArrays, sbPush party_assets, and the renamed post-squads sbPull reconciliation — useful for verifying that the fixes actually land without churn. Turn off with RQ_DIAG.enabled = false in the console. Expected behaviour: (i) first load after this version runs dedupe and reports a one-time "Cleaned N" message; N should match the duplicate count from the v1.6994 trace (5 for the test franchise). (ii) the 500 gp enrollment flow assigns the bag to the new squad, the assignment survives the next sbPull (no more reshuffle). (iii) existing bags in Supabase upgrade their encoding on the next save+push cycle (which happens automatically on any state mutation). Not addressed in this version: the double-invocation of applyDBItemsToArrays at boot — that's a separate investigation and doesn't block the bag fix. If your data still shows orphan bags after dedupe runs, that's a candidate for v1.6996.
Version 1.6994 — Bag-of-Holding diagnostic build (temporary). User ask: build-in diagnostics to trace the "500gp squad enrollment awards Bag of Holding to franchise inventory instead of squad inventory" bug. Findings exposed by instrumentation: (1) Supabase push strips top-level flags. The sbPush party_assets path at line ~25495 only pushes {user_id, sort_order, name, ic, type}. Top-level bagOfHolding and effect fields are NOT included. After push+pull, the bag loses its recognition flags. hasSquadFormationEffect() then has to fall back to a GEAR-name lookup, which the v1.6893 changelog already identified as fragile (a homebrew Bag of Holding DB row can strip flags from the built-in). (2) sbPull reconstruction drops flags too. At line ~25872 the rebuild is {_sbId, name, ic, type, meta} only. Even if flags DID make it to Supabase, this line would drop them. (3) Reconciliation ordering bug. At line ~25900, bag ownerRef values are validated against S.squads — but S.squads is populated from the pull AFTER this check runs (squads loading starts at ~25911). So _sqNames is always empty during reconciliation, every squad-assigned bag fails validation, and every bag gets its ownerType cleared on every pull. This is very likely THE bug. The bag's ownerRef was correctly saved; the wipe happens during restoration. (4) Stale log message. The "ownerRef '...' no longer matches a squad" log line read m.ownerRef AFTER clearing it, so the message always said "ownerRef '' no longer matches" with an empty string. Fixed to capture the ref before clearing. What the diagnostic build adds: window.RQ_DIAG module with log(), snapshot(), dump(), clear(), enabled flag. Instruments: btn-sq-enroll click (before+after push), sqSave entry + migration loop + bagAsset filter + final assignment, applyDBItemsToArrays (flags DB "Bag of Holding" replacing built-in with a stripping warning), sbPush party_assets (logs pushed rows + warns about stripped fields), sbPull partyAssets consumption (logs raw rows + rebuilt state + warnings), sbPull safety-net recovery, sbPull ownerRef reconciliation (with explicit ORDERING-BUG flag when _sqNames is empty), and hasSquadFormationEffect() (every call logs which of its 6 check paths matched or missed). All entries pushed to window.RQ_DIAG_LOG (capped at 2000 entries) and logged to console via console.groupCollapsed. Boot message announces activation. Turn off with RQ_DIAG.enabled = false. How to use: open DevTools console before clicking the 500gp enrollment button; run through the flow; inspect collapsed log groups in real time; run RQ_DIAG.dump() to print the full sequence or RQ_DIAG.snapshot() for current state. This version contains no fixes — only instrumentation. The actual fixes (moving the ownerRef reconciliation AFTER squads load, OR pushing the top-level flags to Supabase, OR both) will ship as a separate version once we've confirmed the bug by observation. No SQL change in this build. No behaviour change in this build.
Version 1.6993 — Kudos capture extended to pinned-zone deletes. User report: "Items that are pinned, and then have their pointer-task-checkbox or pie completed and then are 'x' deleted from the pinned-task zone should still count for kudos." The v1.6991 capture points only covered the main-list sub-task delete (stdl) and the task done-modal (ov-done-yes). Items deleted via the ✕ button in the Pinned Tasks zone (different dispatcher actions: subpindel for pinned sub-tasks, taskpindel for pinned no-sub tasks) were slipping through without creating kudos entries. What landed: (1) subpindel now captures kudos. Same criterion as the main-list stdl (v1.6991): if sub.done || sub.pct===6 at deletion time, push a kudos entry with the sub's name, computed subGp = max(1, floor(parent.gp / subtasks.length)), parent task name, and split-group title if nested. If the sub was incomplete at deletion, no kudos — destructive deletes don't qualify. (2) taskpindel now captures kudos. Pinned no-sub-task rows don't have a checkbox (per v1.6972 design — the Pinned zone for no-sub tasks is pie-only) so the only completion signal is pct===6. If the task was pie-full at deletion, push a kudos entry with the task's name, gp, and (defensively) split-group name if somehow set. If not pie-full, no kudos — matches the main-list stdl philosophy. (3) Symmetry with main-list paths. The four capture points are now: (a) ov-done-yes task done-modal → task kudos on either Archive or Delete branch; (b) stdl main-list sub-task delete → sub kudos iff done/pct-full; (c) subpindel pinned sub-task ✕ delete → sub kudos iff done/pct-full; (d) taskpindel pinned no-sub-task ✕ delete → task kudos iff pct-full. Together they cover every way a completed item can leave the active list via a user-triggered deletion. Scope note: the 📍 Unpin button (tpin/stpin) removes the pin but NOT the underlying task/sub-task, so no kudos is created — the item is still in the active list waiting to be worked. Only the ✕ Delete paths write to kudos. Not in scope: the v1.6991 follow-up question about whether completion-without-delete (just checking a sub or pie-fulling a task) should create kudos was left unresolved; current behaviour still requires deletion as the trigger regardless of where the completion happened. No SQL change. No data-model change.
Version 1.6992 — Kudos sidecar capped at 1 column; pinned tasks spread to fill remaining space. User ask (refinement of v1.6991): the kudos sidecar was claiming up to 2 sub-columns when pinned-span was small in cols-3 mode (e.g. 5 pins + kudos = pinned in col 1, kudos in cols 2-3). New rule: kudos never exceeds 1 column; pins absorb whatever extra width would otherwise have gone to kudos, spreading out even when their count is below the v1.6986 threshold for multi-column pinned. Explicit user note: "allow the pinned tasks to spread to column 2, even though it contradicts my earlier [v1.6986 1-5→1-col threshold] instructions." What landed: (1) New _pinSpan computation. When kudos has entries AND _cn>=2 AND _pinSpanDesired < _cn: kudos reserves the rightmost column (_sidecarSpan=1), pins get max(_pinSpanDesired, _cn-1) columns — whichever is larger. Capped at _pinCount so 1 pin in cols-3 with kudos still gets _pinSpan=1 (can't spread a single pin across multiple sub-columns). Example flows: (a) 5 pins / cols-3 / kudos-active: v1.6991 would give _pinSpan=1, _sidecarSpan=2 → pins col 1, kudos cols 2-3. v1.6992 gives _pinSpan=max(1,2)=2, _sidecarSpan=1 → pins cols 1-2 (3+2 vertical-fill), kudos col 3. Matches user's explicit screenshot expectation. (b) 2 pins / cols-3 / kudos-active: _pinSpan=max(1,2)=2 capped at _pinCount=2 → _pinSpan=2, each col gets 1 pin. Kudos col 3. (c) 1 pin / cols-3 / kudos-active: _pinSpan=max(1,2)=2 capped at _pinCount=1 → _pinSpan=1. Pin in col 1, kudos in col 3, col 2 empty whitespace. (d) 7 pins / cols-3 / kudos-active: _pinSpanDesired=2, _pinSpan=max(2,2)=2, _sidecarSpan=1. Same as v1.6991 for this count. (e) 11+ pins / cols-3: _pinSpanDesired=3, 3 < _cn(3) is FALSE → kudos doesn't render, pins fill all 3 cols. Pin priority wins when space is truly contested. (2) No CSS changes. The .pinned-band grid and .kudos-sidecar-span-N rules stay put — the change is entirely in the JS computation of _pinSpan and the implicit computation of _sidecarSpan. Since _sidecarSpan = _cn - _pinSpan and the new _pinSpan is always _cn-1 or less (when kudos renders), _sidecarSpan lands at 1 naturally. The existing guard (_sidecarSpan>=1 && _kudosArr.length>0 && _cn>=2) still correctly gates sidecar rendering. (3) Visual consistency. The pinned banner now always occupies the "main area" of the zone with kudos as a consistent rightmost sliver, rather than varying between 1-col and 2-col sidecar widths based on pin count. Reads as a cleaner "pinned tasks | recent completions" side-by-side layout regardless of how many pins exist. Scope unchanged from v1.6991: sidecar still desktop-only, still requires multi-col, still requires kudos to have entries. No SQL change, no data-model change.
Version 1.6991 — Kudos: completed-task trophy log. User ask: chronological list of tasks and sub-tasks that have been checked/pie-pieced to completion and then deleted. Accessible via (a) a button in the Contracts header bar next to The Archive, and (b) a bonus in-band display that fills unused pinned-zone sub-columns when there aren't enough pinned tasks. What landed: (1) Data model. New S.kudos top-level array, newest-first, each entry {id, ts, name, kind:'task'|'sub', gp, parent, groupName}. Cap at 1000 entries with FIFO eviction; display 200 per page. Rides inside the existing S JSON blob — no SQL change. New helper _pushKudos(entry) handles field normalization + push + cap. (2) Capture points. Two sites write to kudos: (a) the ov-done-yes handler — the task claim/done modal. Both Archive and Delete branches push a kudos entry with the task's gp and (if applicable) split-group title. (b) the stdl sub-task delete handler — pushes a kudos entry ONLY when the sub was complete at deletion time (sub.done || sub.pct===6). Incomplete deletes are destructive and don't belong in a "trophy" log. Sub-task kudos capture the parent task name and group name (if nested in a group), and record subGp = max(1, floor(parent.gp / subtasks.length)) matching the existing sub-task gold award formula. (3) Header button. New #btn-kudos in the Contracts header action row, next to The Archive. Label: ⭐ Kudos. Opens the ov-kudos modal. (4) Modal. Lists kudos entries paginated at 200 per page (user spec). Each row: ⭐ icon, task name, sub-parent/group subtext (if applicable), +N gp badge, relative-time (via existing _undoRelTime). Pager at the bottom shows "Page N of M · X entries" with \u2039 Prev / Next \u203A buttons. Clear-all button at footer-left requires confirmation. Close button at footer-right. (5) In-band sidecar. When pinned-span is less than _colsN() (v1.6986 banner layout) AND the user is in multi-col mode AND there's kudos data to show, the pinned zone is wrapped in a new .pinned-band grid layout that holds two children side-by-side: the .pinned-section occupying pinned-span columns on the left, and a new .kudos-sidecar occupying the remaining columns on the right. In cols-3 with pinned-span=1, the sidecar gets 2 sub-columns of kudos; with pinned-span=2, it gets 1 sub-column; etc. Sidecar shows newest first, vertically-filled across sub-columns, up to 15 entries per sub-column (so the band doesn't grow taller than needed). Each sidecar row uses a minimal layout: ⭐ star, name (truncating), +gp badge (when non-zero), relative-time. No borders, no heavy chrome — the sidecar is explicitly supplementary, not a primary interaction surface. (6) Scope. Sidecar is desktop-only (requires _cn >= 2). 1-col users don't see it — the ⭐ Kudos button in the header is the only access path on mobile. When pinned-span equals _colsN() (full width), the band doesn't render — kudos only accessible via the modal. When kudos is empty, the sidecar doesn't render even if space is available. (7) Pagination state. P.kudosPage tracks the current page across modal opens. Clearing the log resets to page 0. The state is transient (not saved in S) since page position isn't meaningful to persist. No SQL change. No data-model change beyond the additive S.kudos array, which defaults to empty for any pre-existing user loaded from storage.
Version 1.6990 — Coloured header fills corner to corner. User report: the coloured header bar on split-groups (and Pinned Tasks) stopped ~4px short of the wrapper's right edge, leaving a strip of parchment showing before the rounded corner's border. Root cause: the .split-group and .pinned-section wrappers both had padding:0 4px ... — 4px horizontal padding pushed every child (including the header row) inward from the wrapper's border, leaving visible parchment along both sides. The left edge was partially obscured by the 5px coloured ::before stripe, but the right edge had no cover — hence the visible gap. Fix: dropped the horizontal padding on both wrappers (padding:0 0 4px for split-group, padding:0 0 6px for pinned-section). Every existing child — task cards inside groups, group footer, pinned rows, pinned split-groups — already has its own horizontal padding/margin rules (.split-group > .card{padding:8px 4px 8px 10px}, .pinned-section > .card{margin:6px 4px 6px 10px}, etc.), so removing the wrapper padding doesn't visibly change any content layout. Only the header row gains new real estate — it now spans edge-to-edge so the coloured fill reaches the rounded corners cleanly. No SQL change, no HTML change.
Version 1.6989 — Fix: split-group drag handle was opening the edit picker mid-drag. User report: attempting to drag the group's ⋮⋮ handle would instead pop up the "Change icon / colour / title" modal after a brief delay. Root cause: a two-part bug in the v1.6928 long-press-to-open-picker IIFE. (a) The shouldIgnore check for the group header had a typo: selector was '.split-group-header' but the actual class is '.split-group-header-row' — so long-press was arming on mousedown over the header, including the drag handle that sits at its far left. (b) Once the user started dragging, the browser enters native HTML5 drag mode which suppresses mousemove events (replaced by dragover) — so the maybeCancelOnMove slop-cancel never fired. The 500ms hold timer ran to completion and opened the picker while the user was mid-drag. Fix: corrected the selector to .split-group-header-row, and added two more shouldIgnore checks for .card-draghandle and .strow-draghandle so ANY drag handle anywhere (group header, task card, sub-row) refuses to arm the long-press, not just the header-row-housed one. Also added a belt-and-suspenders dragstart listener on #tlist that calls cancelHold() — if any HTML5 drag begins for any reason, any in-flight hold timer is killed immediately. This defends against future regressions where a drag might start from a target the shouldIgnore heuristic doesn't yet know about. Scope: only touches the long-press IIFE. The drag IIFE and the click dispatcher are unchanged — the click-based picker path (data-a="sgpick" on the icon button, title button, and pencil) still works exactly as before. Tapping the icon, the title, or the pencil opens the picker; dragging the ⋮⋮ handle now reliably starts a drag with no modal interruption. No SQL change, no data-model change.
Version 1.6988 — Continuous parchment fill in split-groups and Pinned Tasks. User observation: a split-group containing simple tasks (like "Garden") showed a clean continuous parchment surface inside the group border. But a split-group containing a task-with-sub-tasks (like "Work Projects Master List") showed a banded look — parchment on the sub-task rows themselves, but the page-background pattern visible in the gaps around them. Root cause: .split-group and .pinned-section both had background:transparent (from the v1.6984 Archive-style rework). Row bodies (.strow-main) had their own opaque parchment fill, so short groups with only rows looked solid. But task cards inside a group are background:transparent (v1.6928 intentional, so the group's tint shows through), and sub-task rows inside cards are separated by card padding and gaps — all those non-row areas were transparent and let the page-background pattern through. Result: rows read as parchment patches floating on the page. Fix: both .split-group and .pinned-section now use background:var(--parchment) instead of transparent. This puts a continuous parchment fill behind everything inside the container — whether a row body, a card's internal gap, or empty padding between elements — so the interior reads as one unified parchment surface. The coloured border, 5px left stripe, and filled header row remain unchanged, so the group identity signal is preserved. Dim variant updated to match. .card.dim and .split-group.dim previously used background:rgba(100,90,80,.09) — a near-transparent warm-grey designed to sit on top of the v1.6985 transparent container. Over the new parchment fill, that low-alpha overlay would read as nearly identical to regular parchment, defeating the "dimmed, deferred" visual signal. Both rules now use an explicit background:#d9cfb0 — a slightly dusty/olive parchment tone that reads clearly as "muted, this is on back burner" against the bright parchment of non-dim containers. The opacity:.7 on dim elements remains, so the combined effect is muted-colour + reduced opacity. Dim header bars still flip to solid #999 grey over the user's colour, unchanged from v1.6985. Scope: only the container-level fills changed. Individual task cards (.card) outside split-groups still use the v1.6984 Archive-matching rgba(255,255,255,.52) translucent-white — they sit on the page background and benefit from letting the pattern texture through slightly. Only containers that wrap other containers (split-groups, pinned section) get the solid parchment fill. No SQL change, no data-model change.
Version 1.6987 — Device-transition reshuffles. User ask: when switching between desktop (multi-col) and mobile (1-col) view, the task arrangement should follow sensibly. Desktop→mobile: row-walk across columns so column 0's top task reads first, then column 1's top, then column 2's top, then column 0's second, etc. Mobile→desktop: round-robin by position so items the user put higher in the list spread across the tops of the columns. What landed: (1) Two reshuffle algorithms, mathematical inverses. _reshuffleDesktopToMobile(fromN) partitions top-level units into N column-buckets by _col, then row-walks: output position 0 = col 0 row 0, output position 1 = col 1 row 0, ..., output position N = col 0 row 1, etc. Burners get the same row-walk treatment but are appended AFTER all non-burners (preserving the existing sort-to-bottom behaviour). _col cleared to 0 on every task since 1-col mode ignores it — keeps data clean for the next round-trip. _reshuffleMobileToDesktop(targetN) walks S.active in order, assigning _col = unitIndex mod targetN. Non-burners and burners are indexed separately (so a small number of burners don't all pile up in column N-1). S.active order is preserved; only _col values change. (2) Split groups travel as single units. Both algorithms use _collectUnitsAll() which treats split groups as one positional item with an idxs array of member positions. On desktop→mobile, group members stay adjacent in the flat output (at the group's row-walk position). On mobile→desktop, all group members share the same computed _col. The existing _normalizeTaskCols() would have corrected the latter anyway via first-member inheritance, but explicit is better. (3) Toggle-click wiring. The 1/2/3 segmented control now calls _applyDeviceTransitionReshuffle(prev, next) on every click instead of the v1.6982 _downgradeCols branch (which was narrower — only handled 3→2 / 3→1 / 2→1). The new function handles every combination: N→1 (desktop→mobile via toggle), 1→N (mobile→desktop via toggle), and N→M for multi-col-to-different-multi-col (2→3 or 3→2, which flattens then redistributes). (4) Viewport-resize wiring. New bindDeviceTransitionListener() uses window.matchMedia('(min-width: 1024px)') with a change event handler to detect crossings of the 1024px breakpoint. On crossing, computes old and new effective column count via _effectiveCols() (which returns 1 below 1024px regardless of stored contractsColumns setting) and fires the appropriate reshuffle. Listener is bound once at boot via a sentinel on window._rqDeviceListenerBound so a repeat boot (e.g. after Supabase auth refresh) doesn't stack multiple listeners. Uses the modern addEventListener('change', ...) with a deprecated .addListener fallback for older browsers. (5) Undo coverage. Every reshuffle — whether triggered by toggle click or viewport resize — snapshots the full S.active array BEFORE mutation and pushes a single deviceReshuffle undo entry with that snapshot. The undoApply case restores the snapshot wholesale via S.active=clone(entry.prevActive). This is the heaviest revert in the system (full array replacement) but the simplest correctness-wise: no incremental replay, no risk of _col drift. Labels read "Reshuffled (3→1 col)" etc. so the user knows what they're reverting. If a user's window gets resized by accident (e.g. laptop opened from one monitor arrangement to another), one Undo restores their original layout. (6) Mathematical-inverse property. For evenly-distributed layouts, _reshuffleDesktopToMobile followed immediately by _reshuffleMobileToDesktop(sameN) returns the original arrangement exactly — proven by construction since row-walk and modular round-robin are inverse permutations when distribution is even. For manually-unbalanced layouts the inverse property doesn't hold (e.g. col 0 with 3 items + col 1 with 2 + col 2 with 1, round-tripped, balances to 2+2+2). The user's undo log captures the "before" state for these cases. (7) Split-group edge case. When a group's 4 members live in column 1 and the user goes desktop→mobile, the group emits once at its row-walk position (determined by column 1's first-unit row). On mobile→desktop with target N=3, the group is one unit in the round-robin and gets _col = groupUnitIndex mod 3; all 4 members share that value. Groups never fragment across columns through any reshuffle. Scope boundaries: (i) Does not fire if S.active is empty — no work to do. (ii) Does not fire for no-op transitions (fromN===toN). (iii) The matchMedia listener only cares about the 1024px boundary; a resize from 1200px → 1400px within the desktop range is a no-op. (iv) Touch devices (always below 1024px at mobile sizes) effectively never trigger the listener; they're permanently at _effectiveCols()===1. (v) On initial boot, _lastEffectiveCols is captured by the listener binder — no reshuffle fires at boot just because the listener is being attached. No SQL change. No data-model change beyond the transient use of existing _col and S.active.
Version 1.6986 — Pinned Tasks as an expanding banner. User ask: the pinned section currently always spans the full width of #tlist, even when it contains only a couple items — wasteful vertical space on wide screens. Make it expand to match its content: narrow for a few items, wider for many, full-width for a lot. What landed: (1) Banner-style expanding width. Three breakpoints based on pin count (sub-task pins + task-as-row pins each count as one): 1–5 items → .pinned-span-1 (pinned occupies one main-column's width), 6–10 → .pinned-span-2 (two main-columns' width), 11+ → .pinned-span-3 (full width, unchanged from current). The span is capped at _colsN() — in 1-col mode, the span is always 1 (same as today); in 2-col mode, span caps at 2; in 3-col mode, can reach 3. The pinned wrapper always sits at the TOP of #tlist as a sibling of the .tcols main-grid wrapper — NOT as a sidebar beside the columns. When pinned-span is less than the main column count, the pinned wrapper is left-aligned with empty space to its right, and the main task columns start BELOW the pinned band. Widths pull from CSS variables with calc() math that matches the main grid's gap spacing exactly: cols-2 + span-1 = 50% - 8px (matches one column of the 2-col main grid with its 16px gap); cols-3 + span-1 = 33.333% - 9.33px; cols-3 + span-2 = 66.667% - 4.67px. Span values that equal the full column count (span-2 in cols-2, span-3 in cols-3) need no max-width rule — default is the full #tlist width. (2) Vertical-fill sub-columns. Inside the pinned wrapper, when span ≥ 2, rows get distributed into N sub-columns (.pinned-subcol) wrapped in a .pinned-body.pinned-sub-span-N grid container. Distribution is vertical-fill: Math.ceil(count/span) items in the leftmost sub-column, then the next sub-column, then the last (e.g. 11 items in 3 sub-cols = 4+4+3, not 5+5+1; 7 items in 2 sub-cols = 4+3, not 5+2). JS computes the partition before rendering and emits explicit sub-column divs, not CSS column-count — avoids the same teleport problem we fixed in the main grid with v1.6982. Each sub-column is a vertical flex stack with a 2px gap between rows to keep things tight. (3) Header spans full pinned width. The pinned header (📌 icon + "Pinned Tasks" title + ✎ pencil + Unpin all button) remains a single flex row inside .pinned-section and naturally spans whatever width the wrapper has — so the coloured header bar always reads as a unified "this is the pinned band" regardless of whether pinned is 1, 2, or 3 sub-columns wide. (4) Swipe drawers unchanged. Row-level swipe gestures (swipe-left reveals ⇄/📌/other action buttons, swipe-right reveals 📍 unpin) still work — the gesture handler operates on .strow-pin rows, and the sub-column wrapping doesn't change the row structure itself. On mobile (1-col mode), the span is always 1 and the layout is identical to before v1.6986. (5) Scope. Fires only in multi-col (cols-2 or cols-3). 1-col users see no change. The pinned-row internal structure (which drawer shows what, the strow-main visible content, etc.) is untouched. No data-model change — the span is computed at render time from S.pinned.length, not stored. No SQL change. Known minor: when the pinned wrapper is span-1 in cols-3 mode, the header might feel slightly crowded with all four elements (icon + title + pencil + Unpin-all button) on a narrow bar; if this reads poorly at lighter palette colours let me know and I'll tighten the header padding or move Unpin-all into a dropdown.
Version 1.6985 — Header coloring + burner tinting. Two visual clarifications on top of v1.6984's Archive-style palette rework. (1) Split-group and Pinned Tasks headers now fill with the user-chosen colour. .split-group-header-row gets background:var(--sg-color); .pinned-section-header gets background:var(--ps-color). All text and icons inside the header — .split-group-chev (chevron), .split-group-title, .split-group-pencil, .pinned-section-title, .pinned-section-edit, and the group's drag handle (.split-group-header-row .card-draghandle) — switched from var(--sg-color) / var(--ink-light) to #fff. The chevron and title previously used the group colour as their text colour, which would have been invisible against the new colour-filled bar; now they read clearly as white Cinzel-cased type on saturated background. Press-state feedback darkened from rgba(0,0,0,.04) to rgba(0,0,0,.15) to remain visible on saturated backgrounds. The sg-icon-default placeholder opacity bumped from 0.55 to 0.8 since the coloured backdrop no longer gives it as much contrast. Implication: the PROJ_COLORS palette is jewel-toned (deep green, navy, burgundy, teal, purple, olive, brown, gold) so white text has good contrast on every choice — if a user picks a lighter colour via the picker modal's swatch grid, they'd get a legibility hit, but the palette as-shipped doesn't include pastels. (2) Burner tinted background restored + extended to split-groups. User wanted back the "this is dimmed / deferred" visual treatment that existed before the Archive-style coloring rework. .card.dim (already applied by taskCardHtml when burnerUntil > now) now has background:rgba(100,90,80,.09) — a subtle warm-grey over parchment that reads as "on the back burner, not part of the active flow". Opacity bumped from 0.6 to 0.7 so the tinted body remains slightly more legible than the previous opacity-only treatment. New matching .split-group.dim rule gives the same treatment to whole groups when every member is on burner. renderSplitGroupHtml updated to emit class="split-group dim" when groupOnBurner (existing variable). The dim group's header bar additionally switches to solid #999 grey (overriding the user's colour) so the group reads "dimmed everywhere" rather than "colourful header on a grey body" — a unified dimmed-grey aesthetic for deferred groups. Scope: Individual sub-task rows don't have their own burner state (burner is a task-level property in the data model, sub-tasks inherit their parent's state). So when a task is on burner, all its sub-rows sit inside the dim card wrapper and get the parent's dimmed appearance naturally — no per-sub-row rule needed. What's NOT changed: regular task cards (non-burner) still use the Archive-matching rgba(255,255,255,.52) parchment body from v1.6984 — the full-colour header treatment is strictly for group/pinned containers. The base .card rule is untouched. No SQL change, no data-model change.
Version 1.6984 — Multi-col polish pass: four user-reported fixes. (1) Column drop-zone now extends all the way down. The .tcols grid was using align-items:start, which meant each .tcol was only as tall as its content — drop-zone below the last card in a short column was limited to the pseudo-element's 60px fallback, which still left a large unreachable void when one column had many cards and another had few. Changed to align-items:stretch; each column now grows to match the tallest sibling, and the ::after pseudo-element's flex:1 fills the remainder cleanly. Screenshot test: scroll far down with a long column 0 and a short column 1, the whole visible height of column 1 is now a valid drop target. (2) Archive-style coloring for split groups and Pinned Tasks.
