Per user spec, the coloured body tint on .split-group (--sg-tint) and .pinned-section (--ps-tint) was visually overwhelming when stacked alongside regular task cards — the whole Contracts screen turned into a patchwork of coloured rectangles. Now both containers use background:transparent, matching the Archive page's approach: the coloured border, 5px left stripe, and header-row border-bottom remain as the grouping signal, but the interior is the same neutral parchment as unhoused task cards. The --sg-tint and --ps-tint CSS variables are still assigned inline on the wrappers via the picker-driven style="" attribute (so they persist across re-saves), but no CSS rule reads them anymore — harmless dead data, left in place to avoid merge noise with the picker modals that set them. (3) Auto-balance columns on first desktop launch. New boolean flag S.settings.contractsColsAutoBalanced (default false) added to DEFAULT_SETTINGS and coerced to boolean in normalizeSettings. New helper autoBalanceContractsColsOnFirstLaunch() checks the flag at boot — if false AND contractsColumns >= 2, calls _rebalanceCols(n), sets the flag true, saves. Wired at both boot sites (main boot path and the test-mode bootApp path) right after applyContractsColumns(). Handles two upgrade paths: (a) users who set multi-col pre-v1.6982 (column-count era) now have every task with _col:0 after the v1.6982 migration — their first desktop load after upgrading distributes tasks across columns automatically. (b) Users who first enable multi-col later (e.g. on a new laptop) get an even distribution immediately rather than having to manually drag every task or click Rebalance. Deliberately placed OUTSIDE applyContractsColumns() itself — because renderTasks calls that function as a defensive reapply, placing the auto-balance inside would trigger spurious rebalances post-render; keeping it at boot only is cleaner. After the flag flips to true once, the user's manual arrangement is never touched again by this logic. (4) Rebalance button unicode fix. The v1.6982 ↻ glyph was written as \\u21BB inside an HTML attribute context (button innerHTML). Unlike JavaScript, HTML doesn't process \\u escape sequences — they render as the literal 6-character string \\u21BB. Replaced with the actual ↻ character (U+21BB). Same class of bug as the v1.6933 fix (emoji in attribute strings need to be the actual codepoint, not a JS-style escape). No SQL change. No data-model change beyond the additive boolean flag, which defaults cleanly to false for any pre-existing user loaded from storage — they'll get the auto-balance on their next desktop load as intended.
Version 1.6983 — Three multi-col refinements: split-group drag, sub-row button reordering, extended column drop zones. User asks (follow-up to v1.6982): (1) split groups need drag handles too — currently only standalone task cards and individual sub-tasks can be dragged, so split groups are stuck wherever they first landed. (2) Move the ✕ delete and ⇄ move buttons to the right side of the sub-row alongside the pin; keep the ⋮⋮ handle on the left. (3) Extend the drop zone on each column so the user can drop sort targets even when scrolled down past the last card in a short column. What landed: (1) Split-group drag handle. renderSplitGroupHtml now emits a <span class="card-draghandle" data-draghandle="group" data-sg="...">⋮⋮</span> as the first child of .split-group-header-row, inline with the group's icon, title, and edit pencil. Reuses the existing .card-draghandle styling (same ⋮⋮ glyph, same hover/active states as task-card handles) so the visual language is consistent. The mousedown arming handler (which previously armed only .strow or .card sources) now also arms .split-group when the handle's data-draghandle is 'group'. New _dragKind==='group' flow: dragstart captures the group's data-sg as _dragSrcGid, and the first member's _col as _dragSrcColSnap for undo. dragover treats cards, other split-groups, and empty columns as valid drop targets — same semantics as task drag. drop walks S.active to collect every entry with _splitGroup===_dragSrcGid, splices them all out (reverse-index order to keep splices stable), updates every member's _col to the target column, then re-inserts them as a contiguous block at the computed destination position. The destination is resolved from the drop target: a card target reads that card's _col and uses insertBefore/after for position; a split-group target uses its first member's _col and inserts the whole block before/after the target group's range; an empty column target appends to the end of that column. New dragGroup undo kind snapshots memberTids + prevIdxs + prevCol so revert can find members by _tid (robust across any re-ordering since the drag), restore their original _col, and splice them back into their original indices. (2) Sub-row button repositioning. Per user spec, the visual order in multi-col is now [⋮⋮ handle] [pie] [checkbox] [name] [✕] [⇄] [📌] — handle on the far left, the three action buttons on the far right, pin last. Implemented via CSS order property rather than shuffling HTML: .strow-draghandle{order:0}, .strow-main{order:1}, .strow-del{order:2}, .strow-actions{order:3}. This keeps the DOM structure intact (so 1-col swipe mode still works — left drawer reveals ✕ via swipe-right, right drawer reveals ⇄ 📌 via swipe-left, unchanged). The CSS order rules apply only inside #tlist.cols-2 / #tlist.cols-3. Before v1.6983 the DOM-order-based rendering put both drawers to the left of .strow-main in multi-col, which visually read as [⋮⋮] [✕] [⇄📌] [pie cb name] — functional but awkward. Now reads naturally. (3) Extended column drop zones. Each .tcol gets a ::after pseudo-element with flex:1 0 60px; min-height:60px; that grows to fill the remaining vertical space below the last card in a column. Since .tcols uses display:grid with align-items:start by default, short columns would previously end at their last card's bottom edge — dragging a target to the empty space below the last card in a short column required hovering in the gap area. With the pseudo-element the drop zone extends downward, making the whole visible area of each column a valid hit target regardless of column height differences. Also fixed: a pre-existing operator-precedence bug in the v1.6982 dragover handler — colT && list.classList.contains('cols-2')||list.classList.contains('cols-3') was parsing as (colT && ...) || ..., which would match the column-drop branch on the right-hand disjunct even without a valid colT. Added parens: colT && (list.classList.contains('cols-2')||list.classList.contains('cols-3')). Undo integration: new dragGroup kind in undoApply. Uses _tid-based member lookup rather than stale indices (since other actions could have shifted things since the drag). Restores _col from prevCol and re-inserts at prevIdxs in ascending order so splices remain valid during reinsertion. What's NOT in scope: (i) group drag handle visible ONLY in multi-col, same as card/sub handles — single-column mode has no drag (use ↕ Reorder modal on the split group as before). (ii) Drop onto empty space within a split group (e.g. between two of its members) — would be interpreted as re-entering the same group, which the drop handler short-circuits via the targetSg===_dragSrcGid check. Split-group internal member reorder still happens via the member-card's own ⋮⋮ handle (task-kind drag), which lets you rearrange within the group wrapper. No SQL change. No data-model change — _col is still the only new per-task field from v1.6982; groups don't need a separate _col since all members share the first member's value (enforced by _normalizeTaskCols).
Version 1.6982 — Sticky columns: tasks stay in their column regardless of height changes. User report (follow-up to v1.6978-6981): collapsing / expanding / deferring / adding sub-tasks to any card caused the CSS column-count layout algorithm to rebalance heights and teleport cards between columns. Jarring every time the user touched anything. Root cause: column-count is a pure layout primitive — it sees #tlist's children as a flat stream and balances total column height on every re-render. Any height change anywhere re-runs the balance. Fix: abandon column-count; partition tasks into explicit column buckets via a new per-task _col field (0/1/2). What landed: (1) Data model. Each task in S.active gets an optional _col integer. Missing → 0. Invalid (out-of-range for current column count) → 0. Rides inside existing active_tasks JSON blob — no SQL change. Split-group members inherit _col from their first-encountered member so groups never visually fragment across columns (enforced in _normalizeTaskCols). (2) Render. renderTasks now emits an explicit <div class="tcols"> wrapper containing N <div class="tcol"> children when contractsColumns>=2. Each .tcol is a vertical flexbox of the tasks assigned to that column. Pinned Tasks is rendered BEFORE .tcols as a sibling — it's naturally full-width and doesn't need column-span:all magic anymore. In 1-col mode the classic flat render is unchanged. (3) New helpers. _colsN() returns current column count (1|2|3). _normalizeTaskCols() clamps every task's _col to [0, N-1] and propagates split-group column sharing. Called from ensureTaskIds so every render pass has valid data. _downgradeCols(fromN, toN) reassigns tasks orphaned by a column-count reduction (3→2, 3→1, 2→1) to the shortest remaining column, order preserved. _rebalanceCols(n) distributes tasks evenly across N columns by count (treating split groups as single units so they don't split). (4) ↻ Rebalance button. Added next to the cols toggle, hidden when columns=1. Redistributes tasks evenly by count across the active columns, preserving S.active order within each column. Complement to manual drag: the layout never auto-rebalances on its own, but the user can trigger a rebalance whenever they want. (5) Drag integration. Three changes. (a) The existing card-drag's dragstart previously checked src.parentElement===#tlist; with cards now living inside .tcol inside .tcols inside #tlist, the check was extended to accept either direct-child-of-tlist (1-col) or child-of-.tcol (multi-col). Guard against other nested .card elements (character cards, store cards) is preserved via the .tcols lineage check. (b) When a card is dropped, the handler reads the target card's _col from S.active and writes it onto the dragged card. This way dragging from column 0 to column 1 actually moves the card to column 1 — just shuffling S.active positions wouldn't be enough since cards are now filtered by _col into buckets. (c) New drop target: an empty .tcol (or the padding zone below the last card in a column). Dragging onto an empty column highlights it with a subtle teal tint (.drag-target-col) and drops append the card to the end of that column. Tracked via new tgtKind:'col' branch in the drop handler. (6) Insertion bar narrowed. Previously the gold bar spanned the full #tlist width, which in multi-col made it visually ambiguous which column you were dropping into. positionBarAt now reads the target's .closest('.tcol') and sizes the bar to match that column's getBoundingClientRect().width. If no .tcol ancestor (1-col or card-body drop), the bar falls back to full width. (7) New task default. Tasks created via the "+ Add" button get _col:0 explicitly — new tasks always go to the leftmost column where the user will see them immediately, consistent with how they appear at the top of S.active (unshift). (8) Column-count downgrade handling. When user toggles 3→2, _downgradeCols walks tasks with _col===2 and reassigns each to the shortest remaining column (load-balanced), preserving S.active order. 2→1 reassigns all column-1 tasks to column 0 (cosmetic in 1-col mode where _col doesn't affect rendering, but keeps the data clean for when they switch back). Going UP (e.g. 2→3) leaves tasks put; column 2 just starts empty until the user drags something in. (9) Empty-column affordance. Empty .tcol elements render with a subtle dashed outline + 80px minimum height so the user can see the column exists as a valid drop target even when no task lives there yet. (10) Undo. dragTask undo entries now capture prevCol at dragstart time; the undoApply dragTask case restores _col alongside the position splice so a reverted drag puts the task back visually AND logically. dragSub doesn't need _col handling since sub-tasks don't have their own column assignment — they live inside a parent task which has one. Out of scope / known limitations: (i) Dragging a task card to a DIFFERENT column while also inserting at a specific position works — the handler updates both _col and S.active position. (ii) The rebalance heuristic is count-based, not height-based — a column with 3 tall cards can still look taller than a column with 4 short cards after rebalance. Height-based balancing would require DOM measurement and is deferred as a possible v2 enhancement. (iii) Touch devices still see the single-column layout (multi-col only activates at ≥1024px, and touch drag is not supported). No SQL change. No data-model change beyond the additive _col field, which is a plain integer that defaults cleanly to 0 for any pre-existing task loaded from storage.
Version 1.6981 — Drag-and-drop revision: insertion bar + handle repositioning. User report (follow-up to v1.6980): sub-task drag-within-task was jumpy and landed sub-tasks in positions that didn't match the visual preview. Root cause: the v1.6980 "shift rows out of the way via translateY" drop preview created a mismatch between what the user saw (rows shifted up/down around a gap) and what the drop handler computed (cursor coords vs pre-shift DOM positions). The fix is structural: replace the shift-preview with a single persistent insertion bar. What landed: (1) Gold insertion bar replaces shift-rows. A single <div class="rq-drop-bar"> is appended to #tlist on first drag, then repositioned between rows via absolute top offset on every dragover. 4px thick, full list width, var(--gold) fill with a soft gold box-shadow for visibility. #tlist gets position:relative in multi-col so the bar's absolute positioning resolves to list coords. transition:top .08s ease makes the bar slide smoothly between targets rather than snapping, but the transition is short enough that it feels responsive. (2) What the bar does differently from the v1.6980 shift approach. The bar stashes its target row + insertBefore boolean directly on the DOM element (bar._rqTarget, bar._rqInsertBefore, bar._rqTargetKind) during dragover. The drop handler reads those stashes directly rather than recomputing from e.clientY vs DOM positions — which was the source of v1.6980's visual/actual mismatch. What the user sees (bar above row N) is exactly what they get (insert before row N). No more coordinate translation between preview and commit. (3) Card-body cross-task drop unchanged. When a sub-task is dragged onto a card body (outside any of its sub-rows), the card still gets the teal dashed outline .drag-target-card, signalling "append to end of this card's sub-list". The bar hides in this case; two different visual signals for two different drop semantics (insert-at-position vs append-to-end) keeps the interaction legible. (4) Drag handle moved to far-left of sub-row. Previously the ⋮⋮ handle sat inside .strow-main as the first child, meaning the left drawer ✕ button appeared to the handle's left in multi-col. Per user spec, the handle is now the FIRST child of .strow itself (outside any drawer), so the flex-row reads left-to-right as: [⋮⋮] [✕] [pie] [checkbox] [name] [⇄] [📌]. The handle's flex-shrink:0 guarantees it holds its 24px width even when the name crowds. Applies to all three sub-row renderers: main-list renderSubRow-style emission inside renderTasks, renderPinnedSubRow, and renderPinnedTaskAsSubRow. (5) Pin stays far-right, already was. 📌 was already the last element of .strow-actions in multi-col, and .strow-actions is the last flex child of .strow. No change needed — just noting that the "pin to far right" part of the ask was already satisfied by v1.6980's layout. Undo integration unchanged from v1.6980 — dragSub, dragTask, dragPin cases continue to work identically since the insertion-bar approach commits exactly what the user saw (making undo straightforward: snapshot source, then revert). Scope boundaries unchanged — drag still only fires in #tlist.cols-2 / .cols-3; touch devices and 1-col mode are unaffected. The arm-via-handle pattern (handle mousedown sets draggable on parent just-in-time) stays as-is. No SQL change. No data-model change.
Version 1.6980 — Drag-and-drop returns for desktop multi-col. User ask: give back the drag handles we killed in v1.6967, but only on desktop multi-column mode; the handle can then replace the ⇈ ↑ ↓ (super-up / up / down) buttons, freeing significant inline space and simplifying the right drawer. The ⇄ (cross-task move) stays — the user accepted the redundancy with cross-task drag because the modal is sometimes faster than scroll-and-drop for long-distance moves. What landed: (1) HTML5 native drag, mouse-only. No touch long-press hybrid — the v1.6967 post-mortem flagged that hybrid as the flaky part, and this implementation avoids it by gating all drag behaviour on #tlist.cols-2 / #tlist.cols-3 (desktop multi-col only). Below 1024px or in 1-col mode, drag is inert and the classic swipe drawers with explicit ⇈ ↑ ↓ buttons are the reorder path. (2) Drag handle glyph: ⋮⋮ (U+22EE × 2). Visible only in multi-col via .strow-draghandle (sub-rows) and .card-draghandle (task cards). Hover state brightens to gold and boosts opacity to 1.0; active/dragging state adds a subtle scale(1.1). letter-spacing:-2px tightens the two dots so they read as a unit rather than spaced puncts. (3) Three drag kinds. (a) Sub-task neighbour reorder — grab the sub-row's ⋮⋮, drag above or below another sub-row in the same parent, drop. Splices within subtasks[]. (b) Sub-task cross-task move — grab a sub-row, drag onto a different task's card (or a sub-row inside a different card), drop. The sub moves into the target task's subtasks[]; redundant with the ⇄ modal but more discoverable for visible targets. If dropped on the card body itself (outside any sub-row), the sub appends to the end of that card's sub-list. If dropped between two sub-rows of another card, it inserts at that position. (c) Task-card reorder — grab the card-header ⋮⋮, drag above or below another card in the grid, drop. Splices within S.active. (d) Pinned-row reorder — grab a pinned sub-row's ⋮⋮, drag to another position inside the Pinned Tasks section, drop. Splices within S.pinned. Pinned rows can only reorder among each other (the ⋮⋮ on a pinned row isn't a valid drop target for main-list sub-rows and vice versa, by scope of the .strow-pin check). (4) "Rows shift out of the way" drop preview. On every dragover, the nearest drop target gets a .drag-shift-up or .drag-shift-down class which applies a transform:translateY of ~36px (sub-rows) or ~30px (cards) via a CSS variable --drag-shift-dy. Transition is 0.12s ease so the movement reads as smooth live-preview rather than a snap. Cross-task drop onto a card body uses .drag-target-card instead — teal dashed outline + subtle background tint, signalling "drop here to append" rather than "insert at this position". The dragged element gets .drag-source (opacity .35, desaturated) so the user can see what they're moving. (5) Arm-via-handle pattern. Rather than draggable="true" in the static HTML (which would allow dragging on any part of a row or card, including from text selection), the handle's mousedown sets draggable on its closest .strow / .card parent just-in-time, and mouseup clears it. This way a user who clicks a sub-task name to select text, then drags to select more, doesn't accidentally start a reorder operation — they have to grab the ⋮⋮ specifically. A _rqDragArmed sentinel on the source element is the handshake. (6) Undo integration. Three new undoApply cases: dragSub re-homes the sub-task back to its source parent + original index (resolves by _sid not position, so siblings shifting in the interim doesn't orphan the undo); dragTask restores the task to its original S.active index (resolves by _tid); dragPin restores the full S.pinned snapshot (simplest since the whole array is ≤20 entries). All three push standard undo log entries with descriptive labels. (7) Icon cuts. The ⇈ super-up, ↑ up, ↓ down buttons are display:none'd via CSS in multi-col mode — they stay in the HTML so 1-col mode still renders them for touch users. Net right-drawer contents in multi-col: ⇄ 📌. Net left-drawer: ✕. Plus the ⋮⋮ handle on the main row itself. On long sub-task names this is a dramatic improvement over v1.6979's 3×2 grid which was ~100px wide; the flex-row layout with 2 buttons is ~68px. Scope boundaries: (i) Drag fires only when #tlist has .cols-2 or .cols-3. Below 1024px the toggle is hidden and the class is never applied, so touch devices never see drag at all — v1.6967's rationale for killing the hybrid is preserved. (ii) Nested .card elements (character cards, split-group member cards) are not draggable — the dragstart handler checks src.parentElement===#tlist to only arm top-level cards. Split-group members can be reordered via the existing group reorder modal. (iii) Keyboard accessibility not included — users who can't mouse-drag can switch to 1-col via the cols toggle and use the ↑ ↓ buttons. Future enhancement if requested. What's dead-code now: the v1.6967-era .st-drag-handle CSS class (lines 165-167, 2029-2031, 3985) and its two closest('.st-drag-handle') guards in the swipe handler are still harmless — they never match anything in the current DOM. Leaving them in place rather than cleaning up because the cleanup is zero-benefit and adds merge noise to a later consolidation pass. No SQL change. No data-model change. S.pinned and subtasks[] mutation is in-place, rides in the existing active_tasks JSON blob.
Version 1.6979 — Multi-column sub-row drawer: 3×2 grid layout. User report (follow-up to v1.6978): on a 3-column Contracts layout at typical desktop widths (~1600px viewport), each column ended up ~500px wide. The v1.6978 always-visible drawer rendered its 5 right-side buttons (⇈ ↑ ↓ ⇄ 📌) + 1 left-side button (✕) in a flat row totalling ~220px of drawer width, leaving only ~280px for the sub-task name. Names with long words collapsed to ~4 characters wide and wrapped vertically — e.g. "Rebuild lanyard badge connector hole" stacked as "Rebu / ild / lanya / rd / badg / e / conn / ector / hole" in a tall vertical stack. Functionally it worked but the screen was hard to scan. Fix: reshape the right drawer from a single-row flexbox into a 3×2 CSS grid (3 cols × 2 rows) using display:grid;grid-template-columns:repeat(3,minmax(0,32px));grid-auto-rows:22px. The 5 right-drawer buttons fill cells 1-5 with reading order preserved (⇈ ↑ ↓ on top row, ⇄ 📌 on bottom row with one empty cell); cell 6 renders empty. Two rows of 22px ≈ 44px total drawer height, matching the baseline touch-target minimum. Each cell is ~32px wide so the full grid is ~96-100px — vs ~180px in v1.6978 for the same buttons in a flat row. Net width reclaimed: ~80px per sub-row, giving the name column ~25-30% more room on a 500px column without widening the screen. Button glyphs and data-a actions unchanged — muscle memory from v1.6978 carries over, only the layout reflows. The left drawer (single ✕) stays as-is since it doesn't need compression. Meta labels hidden in multi-col: on pinned rows the right drawer normally shows +N gp and a from: parent button alongside the reorder buttons. Those labels compete with the grid cells for space, so they're hidden via display:none on .strow-meta-gp / .strow-meta-from when multi-col is active. The info is still available in 1-col mode where the drawer slides open on swipe with full labels intact — a deliberate tradeoff that keeps the multi-col grid clean. Button sizing inside the grid: .strow-drawer-btn in multi-col gets min-width:0;min-height:0;width:100%;height:22px;padding:0 2px;font-size:.95em so the buttons fill their grid cells tightly without overflowing. The narrow-by-default .sd-reord (↑ ↓) keeps its compact padding, already appropriate. Left drawer ✕ gets a small breathing-room boost: min-width:32px;min-height:44px;padding:0 6px — alone in its drawer, it doesn't need to pack. Scope: the grid layout fires only inside #tlist.cols-2 / #tlist.cols-3, which fire only at viewports ≥1024px. At 1-col or below 1024px, the existing swipe drawer + single-row flex layout is unchanged. No JS change, no data-model change, no SQL change.
Version 1.6978 — Contracts screen desktop refinements. User ask: on wide browser windows, let task cards flow into a second / third column, and stop hiding the ✕ / 📌 / ↑↓ icons behind the swipe drawer — there's plenty of room to show them inline. What landed: (1) Column-count toggle. A 1/2/3 segmented control added to the right-aligned action row (same row as ⟲ Undo and The Archive). Persists to new S.settings.contractsColumns field (default 1; normalized to 1|2|3 in normalizeSettings). Toggle is hidden below 1024px viewport via #cols-toggle-wrap{display:none} in the base CSS, shown via display:inline-flex !important inside the @media (min-width:1024px) block — so tablet and mobile users never see it. (2) #app widening. At 1024px+, body.cols-2 #app bumps max-width from the default 760px to 1200px; body.cols-3 #app bumps to 1600px. #nav widens in lockstep so the nav bar tracks the screen width. At 1-col the classic 760px cap still applies, matching how every other screen in the app reads. (3) Multi-column task flow. #tlist.cols-2 applies column-count:2;column-gap:16px; cols-3 applies column-count:3;column-gap:14px. Task cards (.card) and split-group wrappers (.split-group) get break-inside:avoid + display:inline-block;width:100% so they flow as intact units without mid-card column breaks. The Pinned Tasks section gets column-span:all, pinning it as a full-width band at the top regardless of column count — pins are the focus area and splitting them across columns would fragment attention. (4) Always-visible sub-row drawers on wide+multi-col. Previously .strow-del (left drawer: ✕) and .strow-actions (right drawer: ⇈ ↑ ↓ ⇄ 📌, or for pinned rows: +gp · from · ⇈ ↑ ↓ + 📍 unpin / ✕ delete) were absolutely-positioned behind .strow-main at rest, revealed via swipe. In multi-col mode the CSS flips .strow from its absolute-overflow-hidden layout to a horizontal flexbox (display:flex;overflow:visible); drawers become position:static;background:transparent and sit inline alongside the main content. Drawer buttons are compacted to min-width:36px (30px for the narrow .sd-reord ↑↓ buttons) so they don't eat excessive row space. The ⋯ tap-to-open dot is hidden — the drawer is already visible. Swipe-class translations (.strow-show-actions, .strow-show-del) are neutralized with transform:none !important so a stray laptop-touchscreen gesture doesn't visually shift anything. The JS swipe handler and every data-a action remain untouched — same buttons, same dispatcher, just rendered inline instead of behind a gesture. (5) New functions. applyContractsColumns() reads the setting, toggles body.cols-2 / body.cols-3 classes, toggles #tlist.cols-2 / cols-3, and reflects the .on state on the segmented buttons. Called at boot (alongside applyAccessibilitySettings, two sites: main applyWelcomeState path and the test-mode bootApp path) and at the end of renderTasks (defensive — innerHTML rewrites children, not the #tlist element itself, but future refactors could change that). bindContractsColumnsToggle() wires one delegated click listener on #cols-toggle that reads data-cols, validates 1|2|3, writes S.settings.contractsColumns, applies, and saves. Idempotent via dataset.bound sentinel. Deliberate scope: applies only to the Contracts screen; no other screens inherit the body.cols-N-driven #app widening because the selectors are scoped through body.cols-N #app combined with the Contracts-specific toggle that's the only thing that sets the class. Below 1024px the toggle is hidden AND the @media gate means none of the layout rules fire even if a stale contractsColumns:3 is loaded — a user on their phone sees the classic single-column layout. Not in scope: the Pinned Tasks section stays single-column by design. Split groups stay intact units (they're already visually bounded, splitting them would look wrong). Drawers stay swipe-activated on narrow viewports (where they earn their keep) — the always-visible treatment fires only when multi-col is active AND viewport ≥1024px. The ⋯ dot is preserved on single-column for touch users. No data-model change, no SQL change.
Version 1.6977 — Grind-assignment default: "All eligible" is now the initial picker selection. User report (follow-up to v1.6976): the grind-assignment modal now correctly showed 10 Stellani members, but not the 10 Terrans or the 9 truly-unassigned characters. Root cause: populateQuestSquadPicker's grind branch defaulted the ov-q-squad dropdown to grindSquads[0].name — the first clocked-in squad in alphabetical/insertion order. With Stellani first, my v1.6974 filter's "selected squad narrowing" correctly scoped partyEntries down to Stellani's 10 members. The filter behavior was correct for "you picked this squad, show its members" — but the user's mental model is "by default, let me see everyone who can possibly grind," not "let me see whichever squad happened to be alphabetically first." Fix: renamed the existing __unassigned__ option from "🧭 Unassigned (ad-hoc)" to "⭐ All eligible (N)" where N is the full eligibility pool count (via computeGrindEligibility().pool), and changed the default from first-squad to __unassigned__. The sentinel value is unchanged so every downstream handler (applyQuestSquadSelection's "peg slots stay free" branch, confirmQuestAssignment's squadName coercion to '', both v1.6974 and v1.6976 filter sites) continues to work without modification. Selected-squad narrowing still works if the user wants to scope to one clocked-in squad — they just have to pick it from the dropdown instead of getting it by default. Carousel label for this option changed from "Eligible Adventurers" to "All Eligible" to match the dropdown's new user-facing name. The "All eligible" option now sorts to the top of the picker ahead of the clocked-in squad options, making it the visually natural default. User's now-correct starting state on a fresh modal open: picker shows "⭐ All eligible (29)"; carousel shows all 29 grind-eligible characters (both clocked-in squads + truly-unassigned), sorted by how well they cover the grind's peg slots; support slots naturally find takers among the broader pool; hireling-recruit cards only appear if no one in the full 29 can cover a specific prereq. Deferred: the "ad-hoc" connotation was preserved in the internal sentinel name (__unassigned__) to avoid a refactor across 12+ call sites; the user-facing label in this one picker is now descriptive of behavior rather than data-model. No SQL change. No data-model change.
Version 1.6976 — Grind-assignment carousel fix (follow-up to v1.6974). Bug: user reported opening a grind with two clocked-in grind squads (29 eligible characters total) and the carousel showed zero characters — only "Recruit Hireling" slots. The v1.6974 fix had correctly widened partyEntries in updateQuestModal to use computeGrindEligibility().pool, but a SECOND filter in qcarBuildEntries (line ~29772) re-narrowed the list down to activeCycleSquadName()'s members regardless of grind vs quest context. In the user's save, Stellani was the active cycle squad (2 members), so the carousel fell back to those 2 — neither of whom covered the grind's Survival or Investigation prereqs — which then made qddSlotNeedsHireling return true for both character slots, filling the carousel with hireling-recruit cards and excluding all other eligible characters from the grind squads that SHOULD have been offered. Fix: branched qcarBuildEntries on qddIsGrind. In grind mode, filtered = partyEntries (the authoritative pool built by v1.6974 in updateQuestModal — already constrained to the selected squad or the full eligibility pool for Unassigned ad-hoc). In quest mode, the original active-cycle-squad narrowing runs unchanged. Hireling recruit cards: kept. Per user spec, recruit slots continue to appear when any prereq is unmet by the currently-visible pool, regardless of whether a specific grind squad is selected or Unassigned (ad-hoc). The user's design choice: if the squad can't cover a prereq, always offer recruitment — don't force a workflow change. Support slots: not directly affected by this change, but naturally start working again because the carousel now has real characters in it — support slots accept any adventurer, and the characters who were being filtered out included valid support candidates. Why this wasn't caught in v1.6974: my audit focused on the updateQuestModal partyEntries construction because that's where the grind-pool vs cycle-squad distinction first surfaces. qcarBuildEntries is called later in the same function and looked like a pass-through — but its own squadIdxSet construction silently re-applied the cycle-squad filter to whatever pool it received. Lesson: future filter-narrowing audits should search for ALL consumers of active-cycle-squad identity, not just the first one. No SQL change. No data-model change.
Version 1.6975 — Hireling graduation class preview in the Level Up modal. User ask: warn the player which class a hireling will become before the graduation level-up is committed, so they can back out if it's not what they wanted. What landed: a bordered preview panel inside ov-lvlup, beneath the cost confirmation message. Panel shows only when the character is about to graduate — c.hireling && !c.contact && c.level===0. For regular level-ups (Lv 1 → Lv 2, etc.) and for Contacts the panel stays hidden. Panel contains: heading "Class preview: [Class]", a one-line reason ("Matched existing skill: Stealth → Rogue" or "Top stat: DEX → Rogue" or "No stats yet — defaults to Fighter"), and a compact bullet list of the class's distinctive kit items (from a new CLASS_SIGNATURE_KIT map). Deliberate trimming in the kit list per user spec: only the identity-defining items are shown — Thieves' Tools for Rogue, Holy Symbol for Cleric / Paladin, Arcane Focus + Component Pouch for Wizard/Sorcerer/Warlock, Druidic Focus + Herbalism Kit for Druid, Smith's + Tinker's Tools for Runesmith, Longbow & Quiver for Ranger, Musical Instrument proficiency for Bard (the specific instrument is randomly rolled at graduation time and deliberately NOT surfaced in the preview — honesty about the surprise is preferred over pre-rolling). Classes without distinctive kits (Fighter, Barbarian, Monk) render "No distinctive kit items at graduation." Skills, tools, weapons, and generic items (Adventurer's Pack, languages, faith) are intentionally omitted from the preview to keep it scannable. New helpers: predictHirelingGraduationClass(c) mirrors the existing logic in ov-lv-yes (existing-skill match against CLASS_SKILLS[*].mandatory in Object.keys order, falling back to the 6-class stat map). Returns {cls, reason, reasonDetail} or null when not applicable. _renderHirelingClassPreview(c) populates the panel or hides it. CLASS_SIGNATURE_KIT map holds the distinctive-items list per class. Wired into 3 opener sites: (a) party-list data-a="lv" pill handler, (b) character-peek footer ov-charpeek-lv button, (c) quest-modal .qcb-lvup peg handler. All three now call _renderHirelingClassPreview(c) after setting the ov-lv-msg text, before openOv('ov-lvlup'). Backout is free: the existing Cancel button (ov-lv-no) already abandons the level-up without charging gold, so no new UI was needed to allow the player to back out — the preview just surfaces the information they need to decide. Not in scope: the predictor is deterministic (no random rolls), so it will correctly match the actual graduation result EXCEPT for (i) the Bard's random instrument, which is a future enhancement if a pre-roll-and-stick pattern is ever desired, and (ii) the random top-up picks from DND.tools/DND.weapons that graduation runs AFTER the class essentials are applied. Those randomized tops-ups weren't part of the preview spec. No SQL change. No data model change.
Version 1.6974 — Grind-assignment modal pool fix: active-cycle squad members were being offered as grind candidates even when their squad wasn't clocked into grinds. Bug report: user opened a grind (Translate the Ledgers) and saw Javvox — a member of a squad (Solarians) assigned to the active quest cycle — listed in the candidate pool. Solarians wasn't clocked into grinds. Two other Solarians members were also being offered. Per v1.6946's narrowed rule, only (a) members of squads clocked into grinds or (b) characters in no squad at all should be grind-eligible. Root cause: updateQuestModal at line ~29825 unconditionally filtered partyEntries to activeCycleSquadName()'s member list regardless of whether the modal was opened for a quest or a grind. That filter was correct logic for quest assignment (where you want to pre-populate the active-cycle squad) but wrong for grind assignment, where the pool must come from computeGrindEligibility() instead. The existing eligibility helper was correct and already called from 6 other sites per v1.6923's audit — but this one site silently bypassed it, overriding the entire eligibility system for the modal's carousel/picker pool. Fix: branched the filter on !!(P4 && P4.grind). In grind mode, partyEntries is built from computeGrindEligibility().pool, further narrowed to the currently-selected squad's members if ov-q-squad has a real clocked-in squad selected, or left as the full eligibility pool when __unassigned__ (ad-hoc) is selected. In quest mode the original active-cycle-squad filter runs unchanged. Cosmetic fold-ins: the carousel label now reads "SquadName Members" when a clocked-in grind squad is selected, "Eligible Adventurers" when __unassigned__ is selected, matching the grind flow's looser pool semantics; the slot-bar squad tag (qhole-car-squad-tag) is suppressed in grind mode since the selected squad is already surfaced via the carousel label. Why this didn't trip any of the 6 existing eligibility-consumer sites: renderQuestAssignmentList's grind branch (line ~19715) and the hard-reject backstop in confirmQuestAssignment (line ~21048) both correctly consult computeGrindEligibility; this bug was in the carousel-building path which lives in updateQuestModal's pre-render filter — a seventh site the v1.6923 audit missed because it looked like a quest-specific pre-filter. What this doesn't change: none of the dispatchers, no data model, no SQL. Only the filter branch + 2 label lines in updateQuestModal. Existing grind-assignment validation (including the ad-hoc Unassigned ✨ path) is unchanged.
Version 1.6973 — Pinned-row left-drawer reveal widened. Bug: v1.6972 added a ✕ Delete button next to the existing 📍 Unpin in the left-side swipe drawer on pinned rows, but the drawer's reveal translation stayed at its original translateX(70px) value — which was tuned for a single 📍 button. Swiping a pinned row to the right showed 📍 but the ✕ was clipped off-screen to the left. Fix: bumped .strow.strow-pin.strow-show-del .strow-main translation from 70px to 120px. Budget: 2 × 44px (button min-width) + 2 × 8px (drawer padding) + small gap ≈ 112px; 120 gives buffer. The base non-pinned rule (line 3817, .strow.strow-show-del .strow-main at 70px) is untouched because main-list sub-rows still have a single-button left drawer. Why this only affects pinned rows: the v1.6972 ✕ delete was only added to pinned-row drawers (renderPinnedSubRow and renderPinnedTaskAsSubRow); main-list sub-rows' left drawer remains just ✕-delete as it was pre-v1.6972. Gesture threshold (SWIPE_THRESHOLD) is untouched — it only decides whether to snap open vs snap closed; the reveal width comes from CSS. CSS-only fix, one line changed. No JS, no data-model, no SQL changes.
Version 1.6972 — Contracts screen: Pinned Tasks redesign + main-list pie/pin/checkbox adjustments. Scope: six coordinated changes with a silent data migration. (1) Pin scope narrowed. Only sub-tasks and tasks WITHOUT sub-tasks are pinnable now. The 📌 button is removed from the action row of tasks with sub-tasks (subs.length > 0) — grid shrinks from 7 to 6 columns in that case. The sgpin button (📌 Pin / 📍 Pinned) is stripped from the split-group footer entirely. Existing kind:'splitGroup' and kind:'task' pins whose target has grown sub-tasks are dropped silently by ensureTaskIds on next render — no user-facing tip, no confirmation. Rationale: users who want a whole cluster pinned can pin its sub-tasks individually; users who want a task-with-subs at the top can use the reorder modal's super-up button. (2) Pinned no-sub tasks render as sub-rows. New helper renderPinnedTaskAsSubRow(t, pi, pinTotal) emits a .strow.strow-pin.strow-pin-task swipe row with the same geometry as a pinned sub-row but without a checkbox column (per Q2b: the Pinned zone is read-only for progress on no-sub tasks except via the pie). Left drawer: 📍 Unpin + ✕ Delete. Right drawer: gp meta + ⇈/↑/↓ reorder. The pinned zone renderer's p.kind==='task' branch now calls this helper instead of taskCardHtml(…, pinnedView=true), so the whole Pinned zone is a uniform list of slim swipe rows. (3) ✕ Delete in pinned-row left drawers. Two new dispatcher actions: subpindel (sub-task row) splices the sub-task from its parent AND removes the matching pin entry; taskpindel (no-sub task row) splices the task from S.active AND removes the matching pin entry. Both actions log their own undo kinds. (4) Compound undo. Two new undo kinds — subpindel snapshots {tid, subIdx, sub, prevPinned} so revert re-inserts the sub-task at its original index AND restores S.pinned; taskpindel snapshots {tid, activeIdx, taskSnapshot, prevPinned} so revert re-inserts the task at its original active index AND restores S.pinned. Both guard against double-revert via _tid/_sid dedup checks. (5) Main-list checkbox-to-pin swap on pinned sub-tasks. When isSubPinned(tid, sid) is true, the <input type="checkbox"> in the main-list .stlist sub-row is replaced with a <button data-a="pinscroll">📌</button> that smooth-scrolls .pinned-section into view. The pie stays put so progress is still visible in the main list. Progress and checking-off now happen exclusively from the Pinned Tasks section — a deliberate tradeoff that makes the Pinned zone a real primary surface rather than a mirror. (6) Pie padding. Added padding-left: 6px to .strow-main > .pct-tog so sub-row pies get breathing room from the left edge of their row. Previously the pie sat flush; visual reported as "feels cramped" on dense sub-task lists. Pinned-section rows already had their own 10px row margin from v1.6963 so the net visual there is 16px from the gold stripe — consistent, not cramped. (7) Q10 auto-unpin + super-up. When a user adds the first sub-task to a currently-pinned no-sub task, the task is silently unpinned (it no longer qualifies for the narrowed pin scope) AND moved to position 0 in S.active via a splice+unshift, preserving the "this matters to me" signal the pin previously carried. Log line announces both actions. The ensureTaskIds filter would have dropped the pin on next render anyway; doing it inline lets us tack on the super-up. Deliberately not changed: the existing tpin/stpin (toggle pin) handlers are untouched — they still just toggle; users discover the new pin scope because the buttons are gone from disallowed contexts, not via validation at click time. No SQL change. No data-model change.
Version 1.6971 — Grinds list context preserved across the grind-assignment modal. User report: now that grinds are only reachable via Quest Map → Open Grinds (v1.6913), the v1.6970 z-index fix — which closed ov-grinds-list before opening ov-quest to avoid stacking beneath it — dumped the user to the main screen on cancel, forcing them to re-navigate Quest Map → Open Grinds to try a different grind. Fix: three coordinated changes. (1) questModalAbandon() now captures P4.grind before clearing it, and if the modal was opened from the grind flow, calls openGrindsList() after closeOv('ov-quest'). Triggered by the ✕ Cancel button (ov-q-no) and by the tap-outside-modal backdrop handler — both cancel paths route through questModalAbandon(). (2) Successful grind-start path (line ~20867) now also calls openGrindsList() after the close, so the user sees the just-started mission transition into the In Progress section without navigating back through the Quest Map. The existing renderGrindsList() refresh is kept as a defensive fallback for environments where openGrindsList is somehow unavailable. (3) Collateral fix: v1.6970 used raw closeOv('ov-grinds-list') when opening ov-quest from the grind flow, which bypassed stopGrindsCountdown() and leaked the 1-second setInterval that powers the "ready / countdown" timer on each grind card. Switched to closeGrindsList() (the paired helper that stops the timer). This was latent behavior — the interval would keep firing against a hidden modal, making a no-op renderGrindsList() call every second until the user either reopened and properly closed the modal or left the page. Not observable as a bug, but burning a recurring main-thread cycle for no reason. What this doesn't change: the auto-re-open only fires when P4.grind was set (i.e. modal opened from grind flow). Regular quest-assignment cancels — from cycle map quest cards — still close cleanly without reopening anything, matching their existing behavior. The check is wrapped in typeof openGrindsList==='function' so the handler stays robust if the helper is ever renamed or moved. No SQL change. No data-model change.
Version 1.6970 — Two-part package: JSON export/import gating relaxed + grind-assignment modal z-index fix. (1) JSON save-game export/import now available to all users. Previously applyDevModeAccess called setSectionVisibility('settings-data-sec', true) which hid the entire Data section (View Ledger, Export Save, Import Save, import status) whenever dev mode was inactive. The underlying handlers at btn-export (line ~22346) and btn-import-trigger / import-file-input (lines ~22354–22375) contain zero dev-mode checks — btn-export is a plain JSON.stringify(S) with a Blob download, and import is a plain JSON.parse + for(k in d)S[k]=d[k] merge — so the gate was pure UI paternalism with no safety justification. Fix: removed the setSectionVisibility('settings-data-sec', true) line from applyDevModeAccess. The section now stays visible to everyone. View Ledger is already user-facing (reachable from the Contracts screen via the 📖 Log button), so there was never a reason to gate it either. Rationale: save portability is a core user need (backups before risky operations, moving between devices, recovering from cloud-sync issues). Power users have been able to access this via URL-parameter dev mode or by editing settings state; making it a first-class user feature is overdue. Individual dev-only buttons below this section (Content Editor, Milestone Loader, Set Morning, Test Mode, d20 Animation, Edit Gold) remain individually gated via their own setSectionVisibility calls or runtime isDevModeActive() guards. (2) Grind-assignment modal stacking fix. User reported that clicking "Assign & Send" on a grind card inside the Grind Missions modal (ov-grinds-list, z:510) opened the grind-assignment modal beneath the still-visible grinds list. Root cause: the grind-assignment modal is actually the repurposed quest-assignment modal (ov-quest, opened at line 19518 with P4.grind set instead of P4.quest). ov-quest carries no explicit z-index, so it inherits the base .ov stacking context of z:500 — lower than ov-grinds-list's z:510 (bumped in v1.6945 to stack above the Quest Map). Why not just bump ov-quest to z:520? Because ov-contact-warn (z:510) and ov-dt-forfeit (implicit z:500) are child modals opened from the quest-assignment flow and would then render beneath the bumped parent. Cascading z-index bumps across a chain of 4+ modals is fragile and future-hostile. Fix: call closeOv('ov-grinds-list') immediately before openOv('ov-quest') in the gassign handler. Matches the v1.6913 Cycle-Map → Grinds handoff pattern: closing the parent modal before opening a child avoids the stacking problem entirely. User returns to the main screen when ov-quest closes; they can re-enter Grinds via the Quest Map's Grind Missions tile (this also matches how the Quest Map → Grinds flow already works). Child modals of ov-quest continue to stack correctly at their existing z-indexes because ov-quest's z:500 is no longer below anything else. No SQL change. No data-model change.
Version 1.6969 — Quest-map cycle visibility fix for capstone completions + latent renderCycleMap() typo. Bug reported: after completing Rune Routers' capstone quest, the downstream Sanguine Caravan cycle (gated on requiredCycleSetId:'rune-routers') was invisible on the Quest Map until the user closed and reopened the app. Root cause: renderCycleMapCards() was never invoked from the quest-completion or capstone-award flow. All 9 call sites were tied to user-initiated Quest Map interactions (opening the modal, toggling cards, activating/abandoning, buying real estate). When a capstone completion flipped isSetCompleted(currentSetId) from false to true, nothing forced the map to re-evaluate cycleGateStatus on the dependent cycles — so newly-unlocked cycles stayed hidden. The healCompletedCycleArchives() self-heal (v1.6948) that runs at every load path is what made the problem appear to fix itself on reload. Fix: three call sites now call healCompletedCycleArchives() followed by renderCycleMapCards() after saveActiveSetProgress(): (a) collectQuest — the real quest-completion path, covering both autoCollectExpired and the manual data-a="qcollect" button since both delegate to collectQuest; (b) devMarkQuestComplete — dev-mode per-quest completion; (c) the multi-collect handler at what used to be line 19180. Guards: each call is wrapped in typeof fn==='function' so the handlers remain robust if either helper is ever renamed or IIFE'd. Perf: healCompletedCycleArchives is O(cycles × history) with a no-write guard when spDoneCount already matches totalQuests, so post-completion invocation is essentially free; renderCycleMapCards is a lightweight DOM rebuild of a modal that is often not even open. Latent bug surfaced during investigation: the multi-collect handler called renderCycleMap() (no trailing "Cards"), which is a function that does not exist anywhere in the codebase. Any user who used the "collect all ready quests" path would have triggered a ReferenceError, silently killing the handler after the first error. Corrected to renderCycleMapCards(). Symptom reported by user was not reproducible post-patch: the completed-cycle archive and setProgress for Rune Routers were both intact in the diagnostic dump, so a subtle timing or ordering issue in the pre-reload session may still exist — but the added healCompletedCycleArchives call at completion time papers over any such residual inconsistency immediately rather than waiting for a reload. Deliberately not changed: autoCollectExpired already inherits the fix via its inner collectQuest loop; worst-case it triggers N redundant renders during a burst collect, but correctness trumps this minor inefficiency. No SQL change. No data-model change.
Version 1.6968 — Reskinned-item capstone reward support (paired with Quest Editor v1.689). What it is: a new reward type alongside the existing Specific Item / Pet / NPC Contact options. An author picks an existing store item as the "source" and overrides its name, icon, and description (e.g. "Longinus Carpetbag of Holding" as a reskin of plain Bag of Holding). The reskin inherits the source item's type, price, wt, and effects, optionally gets a rarity bump, and can stack additional effects on top of the source's native ones — but counts as a distinct entry for quest-cycle prerequisites and the Franchise Dashboard. Main-app changes: (1) new branch in the capstone award block (~line 18525) resolving override.sourceItemId against GEAR/VEHICLES/BUILDINGS by _dbId (the column set in loadItemsFromDB from row.id). Unions source's native effects[] with QE's extraEffectIds, then rebuilds the legacy .effect object from the merged effect list using the same effect_type switch as loadItemsFromDB — so semantics stay in sync. Source's bagOfHolding flag is inherited per spec. (2) new branch in _autoCreateDashCard — reskin cards use a bronze/copper gradient (#4a2818/#8b5a2b/#c8995a) to distinguish them from true-unique artifacts (purple), and append "(Reskinned {source-name}.)" to the description when the source can be resolved. (3) graceful fallback when the source item isn't in the live catalog (unpublished, deleted, load lag): the reskin degrades to a plain artifact with _reskinOrphan:true so the player doesn't lose their reward and the issue is traceable via console warning. Prereq differentiation is free: the item-prereq pathway already matches by name, so a reskin in partyAssets named "Longinus Carpetbag of Holding" satisfies only prereqs referencing that exact name — not generic "Bag of Holding" prereqs. Dashboard uniqueness is free: capstone dash-card IDs are dc_cap_{setId}_{idx}, already unique per capstone quest. Provenance: the reskin asset stores _reskinSourceId and _reskinSourceName for future tooling. No SQL migration required — rides in the existing partyAssets and content_items.data blobs.
Version 1.6967 — Killed sub-task drag-to-reorder, replaced with ↑ / ↓ buttons in the swipe drawer. Why: the HTML5 drag API + touch long-press hybrid was creating visual + interaction noise — the ⬢ drag handle (U+2B22) competed for attention with the pie-progress icon in pinned rows, touch long-press sometimes misfired, and the drag UX was inconsistent with how Pinned Tasks already worked (↑ ↓ buttons). What changed: (a) main-list sub-row rendering no longer emits draggable="true" on the <div class="strow">; (b) .st-drag-handle span removed from the main row; (c) the entire drag-and-drop IIFE in renderTasks — HTML5 dragstart/dragend/dragover/drop handlers + the touch-long-press + clone-drag code — deleted wholesale (~120 lines); (d) new ↑ ↓ buttons added to the main-list sub-row right drawer between ⇈ super-up and ⇄ move; (e) new stup / stdn dispatcher handlers implement neighbour swap within the parent task's subtasks array, no-op at boundaries. Final main-list sub-row drawer = ⇈ super-up · ↑ up · ↓ down · ⇄ move · 📌 pin. Drawer width: translation bumped from -148px (3 buttons) to -220px (5 buttons); budget: 2×44px default + 2×36px .sd-reord narrower + 1×44px + padding ≈ 216px. What DIDN'T change: Pinned Tasks sub-rows (already had ↑ ↓ since v1.6957); the ⇄ move / "move to different task" feature (kept per user spec); split-modal scissors drag handle (different UX, not for reordering); merge-modal merge-sub-row drag handle (kept — split/merge drag interfaces untouched). Dead code left in place for future cleanup: .st-drag-handle CSS class definitions and two swipe-handler closest('.st-drag-handle') guards — harmless since the class no longer appears in the DOM. No SQL or data-model changes.
Version 1.6966 — Rolled back v1.6964 (translucent pinned row background) and v1.6965 (drawer opacity-hide). Reverted pinned .strow-pin rows to opaque parchment inherited from base .strow-main, with the 10px left margin from v1.6963 restored. The translucency experiment introduced visual artifacts that weren't worth the aesthetic gain. The v1.6963 margin + v1.6961 super-up button + v1.6957 swipe-drawer structure all remain in place. A separate investigation into reported grab-handle visibility on pinned rows is pending user clarification.
Version 1.6965 — Pinned Tasks: fixed drawer bleed-through. In v1.6964 the pinned row's .strow-main was changed from opaque parchment to translucent rgba(255,255,255,.52) so the Pinned Tasks section's amber tint would show through — but this unintentionally let the absolutely-positioned drawers behind the main row (.strow-actions on the right holding +gp / from / ⇈ ↑ ↓ buttons, and .strow-del on the left holding the 📍 unpin) bleed through at rest. All those drawer elements were visible through the 48%-transparent main row even when the user hadn't swiped, creating visual clutter: +gp labels overlapping with sub-task names, "from" button text ghosting through, reorder arrows peeking out. Fix: hide both drawers by default inside .strow-pin with opacity:0 and transition in on the swipe-state class change (.strow-show-actions → right drawer opacity 1, .strow-show-del → left drawer opacity 1). 0.18s ease transition matches the row's translate animation so the reveal doesn't feel abrupt. Only applies to pinned rows inside .pinned-section — regular main-list sub-rows still use opaque parchment and don't have this bleed issue, so their drawers stay always-present (correct behavior for that context). The user's incidental request to "remove the movement-grab-handle" is satisfied by this same fix: the "handles" they saw were the drawer's ⇈ ↑ ↓ reorder buttons bleeding through; they now hide at rest and only appear when the user swipes the row. Note: the multi-colored pie (.pct-tog) visible in the screenshot was NOT a bleed-through — that's the correct progress-pie rendering with each wedge in a different PCT_TOG_COLORS entry. No JS changes; CSS-only.
Version 1.6964 — Pinned Tasks + split-group visual polish. (1) Pie-progress breathing room. In v1.6963 pinned rows got a 10px left margin, but the pie icon inside the row still sat flush against the row's own left edge, leaving the pie visually coincident with the row's left border. Reduced the row margin to 8px (nudges the row further left) and added padding-left:6px to the pie (.pct-tog) inside pinned rows. Net: pie now has 6px breathing room from the row's left edge, and the row itself is 2px further left, so the gold stripe clearance stays comfortable. Also added border-radius:6px;overflow:hidden on the pinned row so the softened background and drawer reveals stay within rounded corners. (2) Row background contrast reduced. Previously the .strow-main background was fully opaque var(--parchment), which clashed against the Pinned Tasks section's light amber tint (rgba(201,146,42,.06)) — rows read as stark white stripes against the tinted container. Changed to rgba(255,255,255,.52) (same translucent-white treatment .card already uses elsewhere), so the container's amber tint shows through the row. Much more harmonious visual hierarchy. (3) Same treatment for split-group sub-task rows. Task cards inside split-groups already had background:transparent so the group's tint came through; but if you had a task card with sub-task rows INSIDE a split-group, those sub-rows still used opaque parchment and created the same stark-stripe effect against the group's amber tint. Added .split-group .strow .strow-main{background:rgba(255,255,255,.52)} so sub-task rows inside groups match the translucent treatment. Regular (non-split-group) task card sub-rows keep the fully-opaque parchment for best contrast against the page background — unchanged. No JS changes, no data-model changes.
Version 1.6963 — Two small Pinned Tasks fixes. (1) Undo button displayed the literal text "\u27F2" instead of the intended ⟲ glyph. Two sites — the main button in the Contracts header action row (#btn-undo) and the undo modal's <h3> header — contained \u27F2 in HTML text context. JS string escapes aren't interpreted by the HTML parser; only JavaScript string literals process \u escapes. Both replaced with the actual ⟲ character (U+27F2). Verified no other raw \u escapes remain in HTML text content anywhere in the file. (2) Pinned sub-task rows rendered flush against the 5px gold stripe on the left edge of the Pinned Tasks container, leaving no breathing room between the pie-progress icon and the stripe. Root cause: in v1.6957 pinned sub-task rows were switched from the legacy .pinned-sub-row class to the unified .strow.strow-pin structure (to pick up the swipe-drawer mechanism shared with main-list sub-rows), but the CSS rule that gave rows a 10px left margin still targeted the old selector: .pinned-section > .pinned-sub-row{margin:4px 4px 4px 10px}. Added matching rule .pinned-section > .strow.strow-pin{margin:4px 4px 4px 10px}. Rows now align with the other pinned-section children (.card, .split-group) which already had the correct left offset. No impact on swipe-drawer alignment — the drawers are absolutely positioned inside the .strow and shift together with the row. No SQL, no data-model change.
Version 1.6962 — Quest-assignment carousel: refined auto-advance guard for squadReqMin unmet case. Bug: v1.6960's guard on the page 1→2 auto-advance was too broad. It stopped auto-advance whenever page 2 was all support slots (the intent was "don't auto-jump to an optional bench"), but it also stopped the genuinely useful flip when page 1 was fully filled (prereqs + non-char slots like Gold satisfied) yet squadReqMin still wasn't met with distinct characters — a case where the user clearly needs to see page 2 to add more members. Concrete scenario that surfaced this: a "Rune Corrupted" quest with squadReqMin=3, 5 character prereq slots + 1 Gold slot on page 1, with one character (Runesmith Javvox) satisfying 4 slots and another (Dhovain Keldric) satisfying the 5th. All 6 slots showed as filled, but only 2 distinct characters were assigned; page 2 didn't auto-reveal despite being the only way to add the 3rd. Fix: refined the guard so auto-advance fires when page 1 is fully filled AND either (a) page 2 contains real prereq slots [original case], OR (b) the quest has squadReqMin > 0 AND fewer than squadReqMin distinct characters are currently assigned. The optional-bench suppression still applies when squadReqMin is 0/unset or is already met. Mechanism: added _distinctNow / _distinctCount / _minNotMet computations inside the auto-advance IIFE — counts unique ci values across qddSlotAssignments, compares to squadReqMin. Non-char slots (gold, items, building) remain correctly treated as auto-satisfied for the "page filled?" check. No SQL, no data-model change.
Version 1.6961 — Contracts screen: three UX refinements. (1) Split-group reorder. The group footer (previously Defer + Pin only) now includes a \u2195 Reorder button that opens a new ov-group-reorder modal, parallel to the existing task reorder modal. The modal lists all top-level non-burner items in display order (loose tasks + groups, groups shown with their icon, title, and member count) with the selected group highlighted. Top/Up/Down/Bottom buttons move the group as a contiguous block within S.active. Implementation complication handled: split-group members can be scattered through S.active in arbitrary positions because the renderer collects them at the first-member's display position via a .filter() pass. groupReorderApply rebuilds S.active from the reordered items list, emitting group members in their current internal order (preserving within-group ordering the user set via per-task reorder) and appending burner tasks at the end. getTopLevelItems() is the shared helper — returns {kind:'task'|'group', ...} entries — used by both renderGroupReorderModal and groupReorderApply. (2) Super-up on pinned rows. Added \u21C8 super-up button to the pinned-row right-drawer, before the existing \u2191 \u2193 reorder pair. Action pinsupup moves the entry to position 0 in S.pinned (top of the Pinned Tasks list). Disabled when already at top. Drawer translation bumped from -220px to -230px to fit the extra button; offset by the shorter "from" label change (#3 below) so the overall visible-row width actually gains a few px. (3) "from: X" minimized to tappable "from". The meta-label in the pinned-row right-drawer that previously read "from: ParentTaskName" (max-width 110px, truncated with ellipsis) is now a compact button showing just from. Tapping it (action pinfromreveal) spawns an ephemeral floating toast positioned above the button showing the full "From: ParentTaskName" phrase in a 2.5s fade. The toast auto-positions below the button if the viewport clips above, and stays within horizontal viewport bounds. Only one toast exists at a time — tapping another "from" replaces the previous toast rather than stacking. CSS: .strow-meta-from rewritten as a button (button-reset, dashed-transparent border, hover/active state reveals a faint outline). Mobile-friendly: no reliance on title tooltip which doesn't exist on touch devices. What's NOT changed: the main-list sub-task rows (non-pinned) — they have a different drawer layout and don't show "from:" labels. Pinned-task-level rows (not sub-tasks) also unchanged. No SQL change, no data-model change.
Version 1.6960 — Quest-character-assignment modal: page-2 support bench + distinct-character assignment. (1) Page 2 of 6 support slots. qddBuildCharSlots now generates up to 12 total slots (6 prereq/page-1 + 6 bench supports on page 2). Page 2 is always generated when squadReqMax doesn't cap lower — the carousel arrows and swipe gestures (which already existed but had nothing to navigate to) now actually work. Does NOT auto-advance from page 1 to page 2: bench supports are optional and users who want them click/swipe manually. The auto-advance block is gated so it fires only if page 2 contains prereq slots, not supports. Respects squadReqMax: a quest with squadReqMax=4 still generates 4 slots total (no page 2); squadReqMax=10 generates 10 slots (4 on page 2); no cap = 12 total. (2) Auto-assign: distinct-character mode. Previous behavior greedily assigned one multi-skilled char to every slot they qualified for, which was confusing on quests with squadReqMin > 0 — page 1 looked "full" with 1 character in 3 slots, masking that 2 more characters were still needed. New two-mode algorithm in qddAutoAssign: if squadReqMin is 0 or unset, keep the original greedy behavior (good for solo-startable quests where one char satisfying all prereqs is the right outcome); if squadReqMin > 0, switch to distinct-first — for each unfilled prereq slot in scarcity order (slots with the fewest qualified chars go first), assign a DIFFERENT character when possible. Ties broken by minimum-regret scoring (prefer the less-versatile char so the most-versatile char stays available for harder slots). Only double up a character when there's literally no un-assigned qualified candidate for a slot — per user spec that an empty prereq slot is worse than doubling. (3) Manual spread: min-team pre-check. When a user places a character in a slot, qddAssignToSlot historically spread that char into every other empty slot they qualified for. If a quest had squadReqMin=3 and a char qualified for multiple slots, the spread would absorb slots that other characters needed to fill to meet the minimum. New behavior: before spreading into another slot, call _qddCanSpread(slotKey, ci, allSlots) — a hypothetical-apply + reachability check that asks "after this spread, can the remaining unfilled prereq slots still be covered by enough OTHER distinct qualified chars to reach squadReqMin?" If yes, spread. If no, leave the slot empty for a different character. When squadReqMin is 0 or unset, the helper returns true immediately — no behavior change for solo-friendly quests. Shared between sites. The _qddCanSpread helper is called from both the manual placement path and implicitly by the MODE B scarcity-sort (which prefers un-assigned chars by construction). Busy characters (on active pending quests, grindPending, or downtime jobs) are excluded from the available pool. Active cycle's squad membership is respected — if a squad is assigned to the current cycle, only squad members count toward squadReqMin reachability. What this fixes in practice: on a quest requiring Stealth + Athletics + Perception with squadReqMin=3, if char A has all three skills and chars B/C each have one, placing A in Stealth no longer greedily spreads A into Athletics+Perception — those slots stay empty for B and C to fill. Auto-assign achieves the same distinct-character layout automatically. No SQL change, no data-model change.
Version 1.6959 — Admin lockdown + critical credential leak fix. Superseded v1.6958: the 8888 passcode approach was rolled back (hardcoded client-side secrets are trivially bypassable via devtools and offer no real protection). Replaced with a UID-allowlist gate. New: ADMIN_UIDS constant (currently ['566fc455-3cd5-4a93-8663-9b361296e5a9']) and isAdmin() helper — add another UID to the array to grant admin to a second account. isDevModeActive() rewritten: now requires BOTH isAdmin() AND S.settings.devModeOverride. A non-admin account can no longer enter dev mode even if it somehow carries a stale devModeOverride=true flag. Three admin-only buttons gated: btn-enable-dev, btn-gen-code, btn-gen-bulk — each shows an "administrator accounts only" alert if a non-admin clicks. UI visibility: renderSettings now fully hides #settings-dev-toggle-sec (Developer Tools) and #settings-classcodes-sec (Class Codes) from non-admin accounts — they never see the buttons. Defensive cleanup: non-admins with stale devModeOverride=true from a previous era have the flag auto-cleared on next Settings render. CRITICAL CREDENTIAL LEAK FIXED: the ?dev=1 URL parameter previously auto-logged in with hardcoded admin credentials baked into client-side JS (DEV_LOGIN_EMAIL/DEV_LOGIN_PASSWORD). Anyone who discovered the trick had password-equivalent access to the admin account. DEV_MODE_URL is now pinned to false; credential constants are blanked; bootApp's dev-auto-login branch is dead code but retained for structural compatibility. User action required: change the admin account password at Supabase — the exposed credential was in client code and may have been seen by anyone who opened devtools over the life of the deploy. Why not server-side only: Supabase RLS policies are the real security boundary (and should be audited separately to ensure non-admin writes to login_codes and official content_items rows are blocked). The client-side gate is UX (hide buttons that wouldn't work anyway). A diagnostic SQL script ships alongside this version — run it in Supabase to audit whether any students exploited the leak: checks for suspicious codes, dev-flag on student saves, non-admin-owned official content, and abnormal gold/party accumulations. What's NOT restricted: disabling dev mode and exporting existing codes stay unguarded (safe operations). No SQL change required for the app itself; the diagnostic SQL is optional audit tooling.
Version 1.6957 — Passcode gate on privileged generators. Three buttons in Settings now prompt for a 4-digit passcode (8888) before proceeding: btn-enable-dev (Enable Developer Mode), btn-gen-code (+ Generate Code), and btn-gen-bulk (Generate 30 Codes). A cancel or incorrect entry short-circuits the handler before any state mutation. Helper: _gatePasscode(purpose) — shows a prompt() with contextual purpose text ("enable Developer Mode", "generate a class code", "generate 30 class codes"), returns true only on exact match to _PASSCODE. Defined alongside isDevModeActive for locality. What's NOT gated: disabling dev mode (btn-disable-dev, dev-bar-devoff) stays unguarded — backing out is always safe. Copy-all-codes (btn-export-codes) also stays unguarded since it only exports codes the user already generated. The ?dev=1 URL parameter still bypasses the in-app toggle without a passcode — that path is for development-env access and wasn't in scope for this request. Security note: this is a UI speed bump, not cryptographic protection. The passcode is hardcoded in client-side JavaScript and visible to anyone who inspects the source. Its purpose is to prevent casual mis-taps in a classroom setting (student at the teacher's logged-in device), not to defend against a motivated attacker. No SQL change, no data-model change.
Version 1.6957 — Contracts screen UX revision: rename + pinned-row swipe drawers + undo history. (1) Pinned Spotlight renamed to Pinned Tasks. Title in the header, colour-picker modal heading, and three tooltip strings (pin task, pin sub-task, group pin) all updated. Internal CSS classes (.pinned-section, .pinned-section-header) and the S.settings.pinnedSpotlightColor field keep their legacy names — renaming those would cascade into 40+ selectors and the settings blob schema for zero user-facing benefit. (2) Pinned-row swipe drawers. Each pinned sub-row now uses the same .strow structure as main-list sub-rows (from v1.6925), so the existing #tlist swipe gesture handler picks them up automatically via event delegation. Left drawer (swipe right on row) = 📍 Unpin in gold (not crimson — unpin isn't destructive like delete). Right drawer (swipe left on row) = the previously-visible clutter (+N gp, from: parent) as non-interactive informational labels, PLUS two new reorder actions (↑ Move up, ↓ Move down) that swap the entry with its neighbour in S.pinned[]. New actions pinup/pindn in the click dispatcher, auto-disabled at the bounds. New CSS: .strow-pin modifier extends the right-drawer translation from -148px to -220px to fit meta labels + 2 reorder buttons; .sd-unpin, .sd-reord, .strow-meta-gp, .strow-meta-from styling variants. The visible row now has ~40% more horizontal space for the sub-task name (previously severely truncated to 3-4 chars on narrow viewports). The old .pinned-sub-row class is preserved in CSS for any edge-case fallback but no longer emitted by renderPinnedSubRow. (3) Undo history. New ⟲ Undo button next to The Archive opens ov-undo modal listing up to 20 most-recent reversible actions. Each entry shows label · relative-time with a Revert button. Data model: S.undoLog is a ring-buffer array of {id, ts, kind, label, ...payload} objects capped at 20 entries (FIFO eviction via unshift + length=20). Each entry's reverse payload is plain JSON — no closures — so the log survives localStorage and Supabase roundtrips. Action kinds covered (Phase 1, 8 sites): (a) stck subtask check/uncheck — restores done flag; (b) stpct subtask progress pie — restores 0-6 pct step; (c) stdl subtask delete — re-inserts the deleted sub object at its original index with dup-guard; (d) tbrn task defer — restores burnerUntil value (regular task path only, group/project defers excluded as they'd need multi-task rollback); (e) tgold task gold claim — claws back the awarded gp, restores task.count, AND restores taskBudgetRemaining so the daily cap isn't permanently consumed by an undone claim; (f) tpin/stpin pin toggles — snapshots the whole S.pinned array before mutation and restores via clone; (g) tarch task archive via 💰-done modal — attaches _tid to the archived record + stores a full pre-archive clone(task) snapshot, so revert removes the archive entry AND re-inserts the task at its original active index with all fields intact; (h) tname task name edit via the edit modal — restores prior name, only logs an undo entry when the name actually changed. Helpers: _pushUndo, undoApply, renderUndoList, _undoRelTime, _findTaskByTid, _findSubBySid. Orphan handling: if a revert target has since been deleted or moved such that its _tid/_sid can't be found, undoApply returns false and the entry is dropped from the log with a user-facing alert. Out of scope for Phase 1: split/merge/reorder (would need multi-task rollback + stable identity across structural rearrangement), archive-via-edit-modal path, group-defer, archive-group operations. Those remain TODO for Phase 2 if requested. No retroactive coverage: only actions taken from v1.6957 onwards are logged; actions from prior sessions aren't undoable. No SQL change — S.undoLog rides inside the existing settings blob.
Version 1.6956 — Two-part package. (1) 48-hour defer pill. ov-burner modal's quick-pick row now includes a 48h option (data-burner-min="2880") slotted between 24h and the Pick Date/Time button. The existing burnerPillClick handler parses the attribute as arbitrary minutes (parseInt(mins,10)*60000) so no handler changes were needed. Note: with 14 pills the row now wraps to two lines on narrow phone viewports via the existing flex-wrap:wrap. (2) PHB-aligned character recruitment & hireling graduation. Previously, recruitment assigned tools:pick(DND.tools,2) and weapons:pick(DND.weapons,rndInt(1,3)) — class-blind random picks — so a Rogue could walk into the roster with Cook's Utensils and Rapiers but no Thieves' Tools, a Wizard with no Arcane Focus, a Bard with no instrument. Same pattern affected hireling graduation. New data: CLASS_ESSENTIALS_DEF table maps each class to its PHB proficiencies and starting items: Barbarian/Fighter/Paladin get Martial Weapons + Simple Weapons; Ranger gets Bows + Longbow & Quiver; Rogue gets Thieves' Tools (both the tool proficiency AND the inventory item) plus Sleight of Hand as a bonus skill; Wizard/Sorcerer/Warlock get Arcane Focus + Component Pouch; Druid gets Herbalism Kit + Druidic Focus; Cleric/Paladin get Holy Symbol; Bard gets Musical Instrument proficiency plus a randomly-rolled concrete instrument (Lute, Drum, or Flute) via the __bardInstrument sentinel resolved at runtime; Runesmith gets Smith's Tools + Tinker's Tools. New helper: classEssentials(cls) returns a deep-copied {skills,tools,weapons,items} bundle, resolving the instrument sentinel per call. New GEAR rows (8 items, all common): Arcane Focus (20 gp), Druidic Focus (5 gp), Component Pouch (25 gp), Thieves' Tools (25 gp, type:kit), Lute (35 gp), Drum (6 gp), Flute (2 gp), Longbow & Quiver (50 gp, matches the existing Shortbow & Quiver naming convention). All 8 also ship as Supabase content_items rows (type:gear, visibility:official, admin-owned) via an accompanying SQL insert script — editing them in Quest Editor overrides the built-ins via applyDBItemsToArrays. Three integration points: (a) classStarterItems(cls) now merges in classEssentials(cls).items at the end, dedup'd against the existing starter list so a Druid doesn't double-up on Herbalism Kit. (b) The recruit-candidate builder (recruitCandidate) now computes skills/tools/weapons from essentials first, then tops off from DND.tools/DND.weapons via random picks to maintain the ~2 tools / 1-3 weapons totals the existing UI expects. Skills merge essentials.skills into pickClassSkills(cls)'s result (so Rogue gets Stealth + Sleight of Hand both). (c) The hireling graduation block in ov-lv-yes now applies the same essentials-first logic to skills/tools/weapons, AND grants essentials.items to c.inv (dedup'd). The graduation alert now includes a Class kit line listing the items granted on graduation (e.g. "Class kit: Holy Symbol" for a Cleric graduate). What didn't change: background-triggered equipment (existing bgMap in recruitLoadoutByClassBgAndCost still applies on top), tier-based loot (starter/veteran/elite), and racial proficiencies — those remain TODO. The existing random loadout behavior is preserved where essentials don't specify — a Fighter still gets two random weapons on top of Martial + Simple Weapons category proficiencies since essentials cover category but not specific items. No SQL migration required for existing party members — this only affects newly-recruited characters and newly-graduated hirelings from this version forward.
Version 1.6955 — Four-item package spanning both apps. (1) Quest Editor prereq selector missing leadership kits.
